This is correctness, not polish. Without the ranking lock, two implementations cannot be expected to agree, which defeats the point of cross-impl conformance. The fix is small (a defined algorithm); the win is large (deterministic cross-host behaviour).

## Pairs with

- rf2-5pt (AI can one-shot the implementation) — the conformance corpus is the verification engine; route ranking is one of the determinism requirements.
- rf2-vf8m (multi-impl org) — when re-frame-cljs and re-frame-js both ship, ranking is what keeps them in sync.

Status: open. Design locked; ready to action. Estimated effort: small focused agent pass.
close_reason: Locked 6-rule ranking cascade in 012-Routing.md §Route ranking algorithm; added :rf/route-rank schema; new :rf.warning/route-shadowed-by-equal-score category; conformance fixture route-ranking-precedence.edn.

title: Hierarchical compound states — substantive design work (drain-rule extensions, transition resolution across levels)
description:
Spawned from rf2-g1h. The capability matrix in 005 claims hierarchical compound states for v1, but the substantive design work is not landed in this pass. Required:

1. Drain-rule extensions for cascading entry/exit along the path (entering :a.b.c fires :a/entry then :b/entry then :c/entry; leaving fires :c/exit then :b/exit then :a/exit). Extends Level 2 of the four-level drain.
2. Deep state-id resolution semantics — :state field as either a flat keyword (current) or a vector / dotted-keyword path (post-extension). Snapshot shape extension.
3. Transition resolution across compound levels — bubbling: a transition for an event in a child state, if not handled, looks up at the parent. Precedence rules.
4. Schema additions in Spec-Schemas.md for nested :states.
5. Conformance fixtures: hierarchical-entry-exit, hierarchical-deep-target, hierarchical-transition-resolution.

Pairs with rf2-g1h (capability matrix) which claimed the capability without landing the substantive work.
close_reason: Hierarchical compound states specified: snapshot dual form, LCA-based entry/exit cascading, deepest-wins parent fallthrough, target resolution (vector + relative-keyword), schema additions in Spec-Schemas, three conformance fixtures. Substantive content in 005 §Hierarchical compound states.

Action: write concrete signatures with arg/return types for all 5 functions; specify the disposal lifecycle (when does an adapter shut down, who calls it, what happens to in-flight effects).
close_reason: Adapter API contract now has concrete signatures. Section restructured with: (1) make-state-container — explicit type sketch, host realisations; (2) read-container / replace-container! split out (read pure; replace is the only mutation primitive); (3) subscribe-container with on-change callback signature, unsubscribe-fn signature, fallback rule when adapter doesn't support; (4) make-derived-value with compute-fn signature, replace-container! errors with :rf.error/derived-container-replaced; (5) render with mount-point/opts/unmount-fn signatures; (6) render-to-string with opts; (7) register-context-provider with fallback-rule for hosts without context; (8) NEW dispose-adapter! disposal lifecycle (cancel subscriptions, release resources, discard caches, single-use). Added error categories :rf.error/derived-container-replaced and :rf.error/adapter-disposed to API.md.

title: :platforms #{:client} default silently breaks SSR-shared fx
description: HIGH (backwards-compat). 011-SSR:135 declares :platforms defaults to #{:client} when absent. v1 apps register fx without :platforms. Any fx that runs on JVM headless tests or shared CLJC code would now be silently skipped (with :rf.fx/skipped-on-platform trace, not error). Not covered in MIGRATION.md. Action: either (a) change default to #{:server :client} (break-but-explicit; new fx must declare :platforms #{:client} to opt in to client-only); (b) add migration rule that explicitly lists every fx without :platforms as a Type-B human-review (review whether it should be #{:client} or #{:server :client} or #{:client :server}). Lean: (a) — safer default for migrating apps; new code follows the pattern's discipline of explicit :platforms anyway. Update 011 + MIGRATION.
close_reason: Default :platforms changed from #{:client} to #{:server :client} (universal). Matches v1's effective behaviour — fx ran wherever dispatched. Migrating apps don't get silent skipping. New fx that genuinely cannot run server-side (browser-DOM, localStorage, requireJS-window) declare :platforms #{:client} explicitly. Updated 011-SSR §:platforms metadata in two places (overview + S-3 detailed design). MIGRATION.md 'What stays the same' lists reg-fx/reg-cofx without :platforms as preserved-and-universal.

title: Kind-vocabulary inconsistency: :event vs :event-db/:event-fx vs :machine across 001/002/005
description: HIGH. (a) 001-Registration:114 query API table lists kinds as :event, :sub, :fx, :cofx, :view, :frame, :route, :machine-action, :machine-guard, :app-schema. (b) 002-Frames:784 uses :event-db, :event-fx, :event-ctx, :sub, :sub-raw, :fx, :cofx, :view, :interceptor. (c) 005-StateMachines:230 uses (rf/handlers :machine), but 001 omits :machine entirely. AI implementer cannot tell what kinds the registrar accepts. Action: pick canonical vocabulary (lean: 001's flat :event/:sub/:fx; introduce :machine as a kind, not just :machine-action/:machine-guard) and propagate everywhere.
close_reason: Kind-vocabulary canonicalised. 001 §The query API now has a canonical-kind table: :event (covers all 3 reg-event-*; sub-kind on metadata as :event/kind ∈ {:db :fx :ctx}), :sub, :sub-raw, :fx, :cofx, :view, :frame, :interceptor, :route, :machine, :machine-action, :machine-guard, :app-schema. 002 §Public registrar query API now points at 001 for the canonical list rather than enumerating its own (different) version. AI implementer has one definitive source.

title: EffectMap closure consistency: examples violate the closed contract; M-7/O-7/'stays-the-same' say different things
description: HIGH. Spec-Schemas:111 declares :rf/effect-map :closed with only :db and :fx, but: (a) 002-Frames:401-405 worked example returns {:http {...}} top-level; (b) 005-StateMachines:147 machine actions example returns {:dispatch ... :http ...}; (c) MIGRATION M-7 (line 220-280) says all top-level non-:db keys move to :fx and supersedes O-7, BUT MIGRATION 'What stays the same' (line 495) says :dispatch-n is deprecated-but-works, AND O-7 (line 401) describes :dispatch-n as preserved. AI agent migrating cannot tell whether :dispatch-n is hard-break or kept. Also: closed-by-default conflicts with open-shapes-everywhere principle (Best-Practice review). Also: M-7 says 'any user-registered fx that was previously called as a top-level key' but no mechanical pattern for discovering them. Action: (1) fix the 002 and 005 examples; (2) reconcile M-7 vs O-7 vs stays-the-same — make :dispatch-n a hard-break with O-7 absorbed into M-7; (3) document the migration agent's discovery procedure (sweep reg-fx ids); (4) reconsider whether EffectMap should be open with documented known keys (consistent with everywhere-else) — proposal: keep closed, since this is a contract between handler and runtime, but add this rationale in Spec-Schemas.
close_reason: EffectMap closure made consistent. (1) Fixed worked example in 002 §Async effects (top-level :http now inside :fx). (2) Fixed worked example in 005 §Actions (top-level :dispatch / :http now inside :fx; merge-strategy prose updated to match — fx vectors concatenate). (3) Reconciled :dispatch-n: M-7 now the canonical rewrite rule; O-7 marked absorbed-into-M-7 with a pointer; What-stays-the-same updated to say only :db and :fx are top-level effect-map keys. (4) M-7 now includes a mechanical discovery procedure: sweep reg-fx ids first, then rewrite per-handler; unknown top-level keys flag for human review. (5) Intro 'covers five cases' updated to 'six cases'.

title: Run-to-completion drain (M-3) is Type B but classified Type A — silent breakage risk
description: HIGH. C1 in 000-Vision says: a breaking change is acceptable if Detectable + Mechanically-rewritable + Behaviour-preserving. M-3 (MIGRATION:87-126) admits the rewrite 'may need behavioural review, not a mechanical rewrite' and depends on intent — that's Type B. But MIGRATION:281 lists M-3 alongside fully-mechanical rules in the 'sweep is complete' check. Per C1: 'Side-effects can be silently reordered with observable consequences' is a design failure. Action: explicitly tag M-3 as Type B (flag-for-human-review). Update MIGRATION's sweep-complete check at line 281 to acknowledge that M-3 hits require human review, even after detection.
close_reason: M-3 explicitly marked Type B at the sweep-complete summary in MIGRATION. Rule list now distinguishes Type A (M-1, M-4, M-5, M-6, M-7) — agent rewrites mechanically — from Type B (M-3) — agent flags + asks before rewriting. Per C1 a breaking change with silent reordering is a design failure; M-3's classification now matches that contract. Migration-complete gate now requires both Type-A auto-rewrites pass AND Type-B human-approval recorded.

title: 011-SSR is too incomplete for v1-required
description: Multiple core protocol decisions still open as 'Lean:' answers in §Open questions: S-2 hydration-mismatch contract, S-3 server-side effect handling beyond the basic platforms gate, S-4 JVM-runnable view rendering details, S-5 routing-and-SSR specifics, S-6 auth/session at the SSR layer, S-7 hydration of non-state runtime artefacts. The status banner still says 'Skeleton. v1-required. Full design to follow.' Resolve each open question with a concrete decision or bump 011 from v1-required if the design isn't ready.
close_reason: 011-SSR open questions S-2..S-7 all resolved with concrete decisions. S-2 hydration mismatch: structured trace event + warn-and-replace default, strict-mode opt-in. S-3 server fx: :platforms metadata + skipped-on-platform trace, default :client-only. S-4 JVM rendering: pure hiccup → string, no JVM React, no server-side lifecycle. S-5 routing: :rf/server-init dispatches :route/handle-url-change with request URL; same handler universal. S-6 auth: cofx injection from request session at :rf/server-init time; no SSR-specific surface. S-7 non-state hydration: v1 ships only :app-db hydration; sub-warmups etc. post-v1. Status banner moved from 'Skeleton' to 'Drafting'.

title: Migrate reorient.md content into permanent docs (so reorient can be removed)
description:
Goal: be able to delete reorient.md without losing content. The following substantive content currently lives ONLY in reorient.md and would need to move to permanent docs first:

1. **Identity primitive — required properties + per-host realisations** (reorient §The identity primitive lines 118-144). The 7 properties (stable, namespaceable, value-equal, cheap, serialisable, human-readable, reflective) and the per-host table (CLJS keywords, TS branded strings, Python Id-class, Kotlin sealed-classes, Rust newtype). Target: 000-Vision §The pattern, or new Spec 001 once drafted.

2. **What the pattern should NOT over-commit to** (reorient §What the pattern should NOT over-commit to lines 146-159). Full bullet list (macros, Vars, Reagent return types, hiccup-only, CLJS-only, React context, goog-define, Malli). Target: 000-Vision §The pattern.

3. **Concrete CLJS choices table** (reorient §Concrete CLJS choices lines 175-199). 17-row table mapping pattern primitive → CLJS reference choice. Target: 000-Vision §Reference implementation.

4. **AI-first principles in practice** (reorient §What 'AI-first' should mean in practice lines 201-295). 11 sub-principles. The 5-layer dampening essay ('A simple dynamic model — the layered constraint') and the data-is-code-VM essay are substantive content that's referenced from AI-Audit.md and the human guide but only fully written in reorient. Target: a new Principles.md or fold into AI-Audit.md.

5. **What we retain from re-frame** list (reorient §What we retain from re-frame lines 363-377). Captures simple-dynamic-model, data-oriented-design, event-driven logic, effect maps, derived reads, single-app ergonomics, upgradeability. Target: 000-Vision.

6. **Working design implications** list (reorient §Working design implications lines 393-408). 12 design hints. Some already in Spec docs; others (definitions-serialisable, host-adapters, contracts-additive) belong in 000.

7. **Tensions and resolutions** (reorient §Tensions and resolutions lines 335-361). Some tensions resolved and captured in Spec docs (override seam, naming, frame vocab); the backwards-compat-vs-AI-first stance is in memory but not yet in any Spec doc as text. Target: 000-Vision §C1 expanded.

8. **Rationale table** (reorient §Rationale lines 51-65) pointing at on-dynamics, data-oriented-design, etc. Target: README's reading-order already covers these but the full table doesn't live anywhere else.

Once these all have permanent homes, reorient.md can be deleted (or kept as a 'historical orientation note' with a top banner pointing at the new locations).
close_reason:
reorient.md content fully migrated and the file deleted. New permanent homes:
- Identity primitive properties + per-host realisations → 000-Vision §The identity primitive (with anchor #the-identity-primitive--required-properties)
- What the pattern should NOT over-commit to → 000-Vision §What the pattern does NOT over-commit to
- Concrete CLJS choices table → 000-Vision §The reference implementation (full table)
- AI-first practical principles incl. 5-layer dampening + data-is-code-VM essays → new Principles.md
- What we retain from re-frame → 000-Vision §What re-frame2 retains from re-frame
- Working design implications → 000-Vision §Working design implications
- Tensions and resolutions → 000-Vision §Tensions and resolutions
- Rationale table → README §Rationale (also referenced from 000)
- Construction prompts as a deliverable → Principles.md §Construction prompts as a deliverable

Cross-references repointed via sed: [reorient.md §X](reorient.md...) → appropriate new home; banner phrases like 'Reframed per reorient.md' → 'Per the reorientation' (history-preserving). Examples (routing/core.cljs, ssr/core.cljc) updated. Guide had no reorient refs. Final grep confirms zero residual reorient.md references in docs/, examples/, or README.

title: Documentation voice: human is marketing/philosophy, AI is dry/precise
description:
Per Mike's directive on the two-track docs (rf2-doc-split):

HUMAN VERSION — marketing-oriented:
- Explains the philosophy and the reasons behind design choices
- Communicates the benefits (why this pattern, what it gives you)
- Includes enough technical detail to be useful, but not exhaustive
- Inherits re-frame's existing voice — opinionated, conversational, sometimes philosophical
- Read by humans evaluating, adopting, or building with re-frame2

AI VERSION — dry and precise:
- No narrative, no rationale, no marketing
- Just contracts, signatures, shapes, semantics, fixtures, prompts
- Optimised for AI consumption: read-and-act
- Aligns with the existing Construction-Prompts / Spec-Schemas / MIGRATION / conformance artefacts which are already in this register

The two are not redundant — they cover the same design but at different abstraction levels and for different consumers. A human reads the human track to understand WHY; an AI reads the AI track to know WHAT and HOW PRECISELY.
close_reason: Voice direction captured in memory rf2-doc-voice and now demonstrated across 8 chapters in docs/guide/. Three voice registers used: argumentative/philosophical (01, 08, with Perlis/Dijkstra epigraphs); code-led narrative (02, 03, 04, 05, 06); migration narrative (07). Voice inherits re-frame's existing tone — opinionated, conversational, sometimes rhetorical. The AI track (docs/specification/) remains in the dry/precise register it was already in, so the contrast is clear.

title: Design two-track doc split — for-humans / for-AIs
description: Per Mike's directive: re-frame2 docs should be split into two parallel tracks. Human track: tutorials, conceptual guides, design rationale, narrative — written in re-frame's existing voice. AI track: precise contracts, construction prompts, schemas, conformance corpus, migration rules — already largely exists as separate artefacts (Construction-Prompts, Spec-Schemas, MIGRATION, conformance/). Several load-bearing questions before restructuring (see comments).
notes: Five chapters + README done. ~1280 lines: 01 (why, marketing), 02 (counter walkthrough), 03 (events/effects), 04 (views/frames), 05 (state machines), 08 (dynamic-model essay). Three voice registers on display. Remaining: 06 (server side), 07 (from re-frame v1). Stopping here for Mike to react before grinding through the last two.
close_reason: Human guide first pass complete. docs/guide/ has README plus 7 chapters (~1820 lines): 01 (why, marketing argument), 02 (counter walkthrough, code-led), 03 (events/effects, side-effects-as-data), 04 (views/frames, multi-frame), 05 (state machines, xstate adoption), 06 (server side, SSR/hydration), 07 (from re-frame v1, migration narrative), 08 (the dynamic-model essay, Dijkstra/Perlis epigraphs). Three voice registers: argumentative (01, 08), code-led narrative (02, 03, 04, 05, 06), migration-narrative (07). Two-track structure now demonstrated end-to-end: docs/guide/ for humans (this), docs/specification/ for AIs (existing). Voice consistent with re-frame's existing on-dynamics.md / data-oriented-design.md tone — opinionated, conversational, philosophical. Awaiting Mike's react before any deeper revisions.

title: Dash8 + rf8 React-19-readiness audit (Reagent surface usage in private Day8 apps)
description:
Sibling audit to rf2-cgcv (re-com + 10x). Dash8 and rf8 are private Day8 applications using re-frame + Reagent. The rf2-cgcv audit could not reach them (private repos → WebFetch 404). They are present as local clones at /c/Users/miket/code/Dash8 and /c/Users/miket/code/rf8.

Same inventory as rf2-cgcv: create-class lifecycle keys, dom-node, findDOMNode, reagent.impl internals, legacy context, forwardRef, string refs, act import path, ReactDOM.render variants. Plus: any Reagent surface usage that's HOT in these apps but COLD in re-com/10x — would expand the rewrite's MUST/SHOULD surface.

Output: findings/dash8-rf8-react19-readiness-audit.md.

Inputs to: D3 cap (already at 6 keys per rf2-cgcv; this audit may extend further), re-com-scope-or-broader question (does the rewrite need more than re-com ∪ re-frame2 if Dash8/rf8 are also targets?), Stage 3 spec.
notes: findings/dash8-rf8-react19-readiness-audit.md (4032 words)

title: re-com + 10x React-19-readiness audit (D3 pre-flight + re-com migration scoping)
description:
Pre-flight for rf2-5djt Stage 3 (D3 four-key Form-3 cap validation) AND scoping for re-com's React-19 migration (Mike asked 2026-05-10).

Read-only WebFetch survey of github.com/day8/re-com + github.com/day8/re-frame-10x. Inventory:
- Every reagent.core/create-class call site + the lifecycle keys it uses (validates D3 four-key cap: :component-did-mount, :component-will-unmount, :component-did-update, :reagent-render)
- r/dom-node call sites (count + locations) — these need :ref migration under React 19
- findDOMNode direct references
- reagent.impl.batching references (10x v1 monkey-patches it; re-com unlikely)
- Legacy context: contextTypes / getChildContext
- forwardRef usage (informational; React 19 makes ref a regular prop on function components)
- String refs — :ref "name" pattern (almost certainly absent)
- act import path: react-dom/test-utils vs react

Output: findings/recom-react19-readiness-audit.md. ~1500-2500 words. Per-target (re-com / 10x): inventory tables + risk classification (low/medium/high migration cost) + summary recommendation.

Closes input gap for D3 + Stage 3 + re-com 3.0 planning.
notes: findings/recom-react19-readiness-audit.md (2454 words)

title: Stage 4 — Implement re-frame-reagent (per Stage 3 spec)
description: Final stage of rf2-5djt. Implement per the Stage 3 spec. Likely sub-staged at dispatch time — reactivity primitives, component shape (Form-1/2/3 dispatch, compile-time macro), hiccup-to-React translation, render scheduler + batching, lifecycle hooks, r/render entry point, tests, examples. New Maven artefact under implementation/adapters/<NAME>/. Adds to lockstep verifier + top-level shadow-cljs.edn + implementation/deps.edn. CI: per-artefact test job in test.yml + deploy in release.yml. MIGRATION: additive Type-A. Examples: at least one (counter-<NAME>) to prove end-to-end. Bundle-isolation grep clean. Estimated 1500-3000 LoC total. Blocked-by Stage 3 (rf2-60le).
notes: 2026-05-10 directive: implement against re-com ∪ re-frame2 surface only. New artefact: day8/reagent-slim-and-fast. Bridge rename: day8/re-frame-2-reagent → day8/reagent-classic.

title: Stage 3 — Detailed implementation spec for re-frame-reagent
description: Third stage of rf2-5djt. Detailed impl spec: artefact + namespace layout, per-surface impl mapping (each Reagent API entry from Stage 1 to its rewrite shape), internal architecture (reactivity primitives, render scheduler, component lifecycle, hiccup-to-React translation, integration with re-frame2 frame primitive + trace bus + source-coord stamping), decision log (where rewrite diverges from Reagent + why), test strategy, user migration path. Output: implementation/adapters/<NAME>/IMPL-SPEC.md. ~5000-8000 words. Cites Stage 1 + Stage 2 findings. Concrete enough that Stage 4 can be done by following the spec. Blocked-by Stage 2 (rf2-142b).
notes: 2026-05-10 directive: spec the rewrite around what re-com + re-frame2 use; drop everything else. Maven coord = day8/reagent-slim-and-fast. Bridge renamed to day8/reagent-classic. ns prefix bikeshed (candidates: reagent.fast.* / reagent-slim-and-fast.*) lands here.

title: Stage 2 — Efficiency analysis for re-frame-reagent (bundle + runtime perf)
description: Second stage of rf2-5djt re-frame-reagent rewrite. Analyse Reagent perf + bundle inefficiencies + propose optimisation strategies. Output: findings/re-frame-reagent-stage2-efficiency.md. Blocked-by Stage 1 (rf2-ui6g) since efficiency work depends on confirmed back-compat surface. Internals to analyse: RAtom + Reaction primitives, render queue + batching, hiccup-to-React translation cost (reagent.impl.template/as-element), Form-1/2/3 runtime detection, *current-component* dynamic-var overhead, React 18 concurrent-mode integration (act, hydration), convert-prop-value keyword stringification (rf2-d3k3/rf2-d4sf class), production-elision survey. Optimisation strategies to propose with code-shape sketches: signal-based reactivity, compile-time macro Form-detection, direct hiccup-to-DOM for leaves, eliminate *current-component* for hooks, native goog-define source-coord gating, structurally fix convert-prop-value. Cross-reference findings/reagent-rewrite-analysis.md (kernel approach was earlier recommendation; Mike now wants full per-adapter rewrite). ~3000-5000 words. Per-internal: cost / proposed alt / estimated savings. Risk assessment for back-compat.
notes: 2026-05-10 directive: rewrite surface scoped to re-com ∪ re-frame2 actual usage. 'Slim' is rigorous — surfaces outside this intersection are not shipped. Stage 2 perf analysis works against this tighter surface, not against full Reagent back-compat. rf2-cgcv pre-flight defines the input surface.

title: Stage 1 — API surface analysis for re-frame-reagent (back-compat scope)
description: First stage of the rf2-5djt re-frame-reagent rewrite. Analyse Reagent public API + decide back-compat scope. Output: findings/re-frame-reagent-stage1-api-surface.md. Enumerate Reagent surfaces (r/atom, r/reaction, r/track, r/with-let, r/cursor, r/render, r/flush, r/create-class, *current-component*, hiccup, Form-1/2/3 dispatch, reagent.impl.template, reagent.dom, etc.). Categorise MUST/SHOULD/CAN-DEPRECATE/CAN-OMIT. Survey usage in re-frame2 examples + Day8 sibling repos (rf8/Dash8). Settle artefact name (re-frame-reagent vs re-frame-2-reagent-fast vs reagent-rewrite) + CLJS namespace shape. Out of scope: implementation strategy (Stage 2-3), code (Stage 4). Output ~3000-5000 words findings doc with per-surface decision + naming proposal.
notes: Stage 1 findings: findings/re-frame-2-reagent-stage1-api-surface.md

title: re-frame-reagent: backward-compat Reagent rewrite optimised for re-frame2 (parent epic)
description:
Mike (2026-05-10): build a new substrate adapter — name TBD, candidates re-frame-reagent / re-frame-2-reagent-fast / reagent-rewrite — that is fully API-compatible with Reagent but rewritten for two objectives:

1. Bundle-size efficiency.
2. Runtime performance.

Plus alignment with re-frame2's ethos: production-elision strict, frame-aware, source-coord native, trace native.

Treated as a separate substrate adapter alongside the existing day8/re-frame-2-reagent — users opt in by choosing the artefact. The current Reagent adapter stays available for users who want to stay with stock Reagent.

This is implementation work, NOT spec work. The spec doesn't change.

Four stages:
- Stage 1 — API surface analysis (back-compat)
- Stage 2 — Efficiency analysis
- Stage 3 — Detailed implementation spec
- Stage 4 — Implementation

Each blocked-by the previous. Sequential.

title: re-frame-pair2: snapshot mega-op cuts round-trip count for investigate-X workflows
description:
Many investigate-X tasks today are 5-10 ops chained — each a round-trip plus Claude-think latency. Per-op speed is one bottleneck; round-trip count is another.

Add a coarser 'snapshot' mega-op that returns frames + sub-cache + machines + epoch-history + recent-traces in one round-trip. Claude reasons about state from one big map rather than 5 sequential reads.

## Scope

- New op: '(snapshot {:frames :all :include [:app-db :sub-cache :machines :epochs :traces]})'
- Returns: { :<frame-id> { :app-db ... :sub-cache ... :machines [...] :epochs [...] :traces [...] }, ... }
- Composable: pass smaller :include if you only want subsets.
- Deprecate granular reads as PRIMARY surface (keep them as fine-grain alternatives).

## Disposition

P2 — composes with rf2-5b8e (MCP transport) and rf2-???-preload. Blocked-by rf2-5b8e since the bigger payload benefits more from a persistent nREPL connection than a fresh-bash-shim per op.

title: re-frame-pair2: auto-inject runtime via shadow-cljs :preloads (no per-session inject step)
description:
Currently re-frame-pair2.runtime is injected via cljs-eval on first connect each session. Each connection pays the inject cost (~hundreds of ms — cljs-eval is slow to compile + load runtime helpers).

Move the runtime into a shadow-cljs :preload entry (or equivalent build-time hook). When the consumer's app boots, the runtime is already there. Pair2's first-connect step verifies + skips inject.

## Scope

- Add re-frame.pair.runtime to a shadow-cljs preload. Document for consumers how to opt in (':preloads [re-frame.pair.runtime]' or similar).
- pair2's connect-flow detects the preload presence (check for runtime sentinel) and skips inject.
- Fall-back path: if preload not configured, current cljs-eval inject still works (back-compat).

## Disposition

P2 — composes with rf2-5b8e (MCP server) for compounding speed wins. Blocked-by rf2-5b8e (the MCP server is the connection-holder; this optimisation only compounds once direct nREPL is in place).

title: re-frame-pair2 MCP server: native nREPL transport (structural shift)
description:
Replace the bash-shim → babashka → nREPL chain with a native MCP server speaking nREPL directly. Claude calls MCP tools; the server holds the nREPL connection per session.

Current chain pays per-op: bash startup (~50ms Win, less elsewhere) + babashka startup (~50-100ms) + nREPL connect (cold ~200-500ms) + form ser/eval/response + bash exit. Direct MCP-nREPL: ~10-50ms per op.

## Scope

- Build a Node-based or babashka-based MCP server that exposes pair2's op surface (eval-cljs, app-db/snapshot, app-db/get, app-db/reset, registrar/list, registrar/describe, subs/cache, subs/sample, machines/*, pair-dispatch, watch-*, etc.).
- Server holds a persistent nREPL connection across the session.
- Sentinel-based reconnect on app reload (per existing pattern).
- Deprecate the bash shims in favour of MCP tool calls; keep the shims for back-compat if appropriate.
- Update SKILL.md to teach Claude the MCP tool calls.
- Document the install + connect flow.

## Implementation language

Node + nrepl-client npm package OR babashka + bencode. Pick whichever has cleaner MCP server tooling. Probably Node — MCP SDK is JS-flavoured.

## Bottlenecks expected to dissolve

- First-op latency drops from cold-bash-babashka-nREPL (~700ms) to single nREPL message (~10-50ms).
- Subsequent ops in the same session are persistent-connection-fast (~5-20ms).
- Eliminates Windows bash quirks.

## Bottlenecks NOT addressed by this bead alone

- Runtime auto-injection (rf2-???-pair2-preloads).
- Round-trip count per task (rf2-???-pair2-snapshot-mega-op).
- Polling vs streaming (rf2-???-pair2-streaming-subs).
These are the 3 sub-beads filed alongside.

## Disposition

P2 — pre-1.0 candidate since pair2's iteration speed gates productive use. Direction set.

title: Make re-frame-pair2 faster to iterate with (parent)
description: Mike (2026-05-10): pair2's iteration speed is gated by transport overhead + non-structural inefficiencies. Four sub-beads compose: (1) MCP server speaking nREPL natively (structural shift, prerequisite for the others); (2) auto-injected runtime via shadow-cljs :preloads (no per-session inject cost); (3) coarser snapshot mega-op (cuts round-trip count); (4) streaming subscription channel for trace + epoch events. Each is independently valuable; (1) unblocks (2)-(4).

title: Conformance README could either point to the published JSON corpus or soften the claim
description:
Suggestion to consider:

`spec/conformance/README.md` tells non-EDN implementors that a JSON-translated conformance corpus is also published, but the spec tree does not say where that artefact lives or how it is generated.

Evidence:
- `spec/conformance/README.md:28` says: "A JSON-translated corpus is also published for hosts where EDN is too friction; the JSON form is mechanically derived from the EDN source so the two never drift."
- There is no adjacent path, download location, generation command, or publication note in `spec/conformance/README.md`.
- The `spec/conformance/` directory only contains `README.md` and `fixtures/`, so a reader staying inside `/spec` has no way to find the promised JSON form.

Why it matters:
For non-Clojure implementors, this reads as a completeness promise the corpus does not cash out locally. The gap is not the absence of JSON itself; the gap is that the spec does not tell the implementor where the canonical JSON artefact is or how to reproduce it.

Suggested improvement:
- If the JSON corpus exists, add its location and the generation/publication contract.
- If it is planned rather than shipped, reword the sentence to say so explicitly.
- Prefer a single source of truth: either a checked-in/generated path or a documented release artefact, not an implied external somewhere.

title: Conformance README could explicitly document the two fixture modes now in the corpus
description:
Suggestion to consider:

`spec/conformance/README.md` still introduces fixtures as if the corpus has one primary shape: dispatch-driven frame fixtures with top-level `:fixture/dispatches` and `:fixture/expect`. The live corpus now has a second substantial mode: pure/direct-call fixtures driven by `:fixture/calls` with call-local expectations.

Evidence:
- `spec/conformance/README.md:35-52` shows only the dispatch-style map with top-level `:fixture/dispatches` and `:fixture/expect`.
- `spec/conformance/README.md:150-153` mentions `:fixture/calls` only later in the lifecycle walkthrough, after the reader has already been taught the top-level shape.
- Many fixtures now use `:fixture/calls` instead of `:fixture/expect`, for example `spec/conformance/fixtures/machine-transition.edn`, `spec/conformance/fixtures/routing-match-url.edn`, and `spec/conformance/fixtures/ssr-render-to-string.edn`.
- The README's comparison paragraph at `spec/conformance/README.md:153` refers to `:expected-fx-emitted`, while the documented dispatch-style example and live fixtures use `:effects-routed`.

Why it matters:
The conformance README is the implementor's entry point. A host author reading only that document can easily build the wrong harness abstraction, especially for machine, routing, and SSR pure-call fixtures.

Suggested improvement:
- Split the fixture-format section into two explicit modes: dispatch fixtures and pure/direct-call fixtures.
- Add one worked example for each mode.
- Normalise the expectation-key vocabulary in the README (`:effects-routed` vs `:expected-fx-emitted`) so the prose matches the corpus.

title: Consider aligning re-frame-pair2 watch modes and predicate docs with the actual implementation
description:
Why this suggestion exists:
The `watch/*` surface does not currently match its documented contract in two ways.

First, window mode and count mode are not actually separate. In `skills/re-frame-pair2/scripts/ops.clj:467-495`, `watch-op` always assigns a default `count-n` of 5 and checks both the elapsed-time and emitted-count stop conditions for every non-stream watch. That means `scripts/watch-epochs.sh --window-ms 30000` can stop after five matches instead of running for the full 30 seconds promised in `skills/re-frame-pair2/SKILL.md:151`.

Second, `--custom` is advertised to users but not implemented. `SKILL.md:156` lists it as a supported predicate and `SKILL.md:306` uses it in the machine-transition recipe, but `skills/re-frame-pair2/scripts/ops.clj:444-450` exits immediately with `:not-yet-supported` when the flag is passed.

Suggestions to consider:
- Make `--window-ms` and `--count` truly distinct modes, or set the inactive mode's limit to unbounded so `watch/window` behaves as documented.
- Either implement `--custom` or remove it from user-facing docs and examples until it exists.
- Add direct shim/integration coverage for watch mode termination semantics and for any supported predicate flags.

title: Make browser test harness resilient to occupied port 8021
description:
Why this issue exists:
implementation/scripts/serve-and-run-browser-tests.cjs hard-codes `const PORT = 8021` and assumes that port is available. In a workspace where another local repo or prior test server is already listening on 8021, `http-server` exits with `EADDRINUSE`, but the harness still waits through the readiness timeout before failing. That makes `npm run test:browser` brittle across concurrent local sessions and slows diagnosis.

What needs to be done:
- Allow the browser test port to be injected via env var and/or choose a free port dynamically.
- Detect child-process early exit, especially `EADDRINUSE`, and fail fast with a direct error instead of timing out after the readiness loop.
- Thread the selected port through to `BROWSER_TEST_URL` so the Playwright runner remains unchanged.

Notes:
Observed from this repo on 2026-05-10: `npm run test:browser` failed because another `http-server` process from a sibling repo was already bound to 8021, not because of a product regression.

title: Spec 005 gap: per-:invoke wall-clock :timeout-ms (spans retries)
description: From findings/boot-as-statemachine-dash8-rf8.md gap M3. Spec 014 :rf.http/managed per-attempt timeout governs ONE HTTP attempt; if request retries N times the wall-clock can grow well past any sensible bound. Boot machine wants 'this whole step (including retries) times out at 30s'; no first-class way today. Workarounds: (a) sibling :timer machine racing the work; (b) embed timeout in retry math. Both imperfect. Direction: add :timeout-ms to :invoke spec. When spawned machine doesn't terminate within the wall-clock window, runtime cancels it and transitions parent via :on-timeout. Trace event :rf.machine.invoke/timed-out fires on expiry. Open question: does :on-timeout receive partial-progress context? Probably no (timeout = state unknown); document. Disposition: P2 pre-1.0 candidate. Composes with M1 (spawn-and-join also wants :timeout-ms) and M2 (timeout fires same cancellation cascade).

title: Spec 005 gap: :invoke cancellation contract for in-flight :rf.http/managed requests
description: From findings/boot-as-statemachine-dash8-rf8.md gap M2. When a parent machine cancels a spawned child mid-execution, what happens to in-flight :rf.http/managed requests the child kicked off? Spec 005 + spec 014 do not explicitly cover the cross-feature cancellation contract. Direction: spawned actor destruction MUST abort its in-flight HTTP requests; trace event :rf.http/aborted-on-actor-destroy fires. Spawned-actor-id propagates through :rf.http/managed dispatch envelope; frame's HTTP request registry filters by spawned-actor-id on destroy; late-bind hook from machines into http to clear actor's requests. Open question: non-actor sources (request dispatched directly from event handler with no spawned-actor parent) — no cancellation. Probably fine; confirm in spec wording. Disposition: P2 pre-1.0 candidate; cancellation contract affects every boot machine correctness.

title: Spec 005 gap: first-class spawn-and-join (parallel-region state-machines)
description: From findings/boot-as-statemachine-dash8-rf8.md gap M1. Both Dash8 and rf8 boot sequences are parallel-region state machines expressed via day8.re-frame.async-flow-fx :seen-all-of? / :seen-any-of? predicate DSL. Dash8 fans out 7 dispatches and joins on a 7-event set. The spawn-and-join pattern dominates; spec 005 has no first-class expression for it. Today authors fake it with siblings + counter + :always — mechanical, verbose. Direction: add :invoke-all (or extend :invoke) with :join-condition :all/:any/:n-of/predicate-fn. Trace events for join lifecycle. Cancel-on-decision semantics need design refinement (cancel siblings on first :all-complete or :any-failed). Disposition: P2 pre-1.0 candidate since boot-as-state-machine is flagship use case.

title: Spec corpus: scope multi-host portability to JS-cross-compile + React (drop Python/Ruby/Go; drop non-VDOM substrates)
description: Decision (Mike, 2026-05-10): re-frame2 commits to React + VDOM. Non-VDOM substrates (Svelte, Solid, vanilla DOM, Replicant, Lit) are out of scope for forward-looking discussion. Multi-host portability scopes to languages that cross-compile to JS and work with React in the browser — examples: ClojureScript (reference), TypeScript, Melange / ReScript / Reason, Fable (F#), Squint, Scala.js, Kotlin/JS, PureScript. DROP from spec language: Python, Ruby, Kotlin (non-JS), native Rust, Go, server-side languages. Sweep spec corpus to align: 000-Vision (the host-profile matrix, the §Pattern footnote, Goal 8 'language-agnostic'), 001-Registration:137, 002-Frames:426/440/649, 004-Views:13/51, 006-ReactiveSubstrate:61/117/897, 010-Schemas:242 (Python hashlib parenthetical), 014-HTTPRequests:24, Construction-Prompts:16, Conventions:180. Reword 'host language' → 'JS-cross-compile language' or similar where appropriate. Replace non-React substrate mentions (Vue/Solid/Svelte) with React-flavoured cross-compile examples (Melange-React, PureScript-React, etc.). Pre-alpha; no migration concern. Blocked-by rf2-agql to avoid spec/006 + Conventions conflict.
notes:
---

## Decisions (Mike, 2026-05-10) — open questions from findings/substrate-contract-portability.md

### Q1: Drop 'any reactive substrate' wording in spec/006? — YES

The preamble's 'any reactive substrate' framing is retired. Spec 006 explicitly commits to React + VDOM. The substrate-agnostic wording stays only where it accurately describes the contract that DOES generalise (the universal reactive-container contract — entries 1-5 + 9 of the 9-fn surface). The render-side (entries 6-8) is React-shaped and the spec should say so.

Concrete sweep targets in 006:
- §What re-frame2 separates — currently 'reactive / rendering substrate', narrow to 'React + VDOM substrate'.
- §What the adapter owns — entries 6-8 (subscribe-tracking, render, dispose-frame!) should call out the React+VDOM assumption.
- The boundary description — the universal-vs-React-shaped split should be explicit.

### Q2: 8-language list canonical — YES

Confirmed: ClojureScript, TypeScript, Melange / ReScript / Reason, Fable (F#), Squint, Scala.js, PureScript, Kotlin/JS.

Sweep removes Python, Ruby, Kotlin (non-JS), Go, native Rust, server-side languages from spec corpus.

### Q3: Shared React-context primitive — KEEP in core, document per-language port equivalent

The `re-frame.adapter.context/frame-context` lives in the core artefact (CLJS-only file, JVM build doesn't load it). Consistent with the React commitment.

For non-CLJS language ports (TS, Fable, Melange etc.), each port handles its own React.createContext equivalent. Document this contract pre-1.0:

- spec/006 §Frame propagation — note that the React-context primitive is host-language-specific. CLJS reference uses cljs-bean-style React.createContext; TS port uses native React.createContext; Fable/Scala.js/PureScript ports use their respective React-binding's context primitive.
- The contract is 'each port supplies a React-Context value carrying the current frame-id; views read it via the port's hooks-equivalent.' The mechanism varies; the contract doesn't.
- spec/006 should NOT prescribe the JS implementation details — that's port discretion.

## Sweep work scope (revised)

In addition to dropping non-React-VDOM substrates and non-cross-compile languages, the sweep:

1. Drops 'any reactive substrate' framing from spec/006 preamble + commit text.
2. Adds explicit React + VDOM commitment language to 006 §Reactive substrate.
3. Adopts the 8-language list as the canonical multi-host portability target.
4. Documents per-language React-context-primitive in spec/006 §Frame propagation (no relocation; clarification only).
5. Sweeps Python/Ruby/Kotlin-non-JS/Go/native-Rust references from corpus.

title: Explicit (rf/init! adapter-map) — drop no-arg form + drop register-default-adapter! (replaces rf2-84po model)
description: Decision (Mike, 2026-05-10): explicit > implicit, bundle-size argument. Current rf2-84po (PR #165) ships side-effect register-default-adapter! on substrate-ns load — implicit and bundle-bloat. Redesign: (1) drop no-arg (rf/init!) — raises :rf.error/no-adapter-specified instead; (2) drop register-default-adapter! and the default-adapter registry entirely; (3) each adapter ns (re-frame.adapter.reagent / .uix / .helix / .ssr per Option A naming) exports an 'adapter' var (the adapter map); (4) consumer requires the ns + passes the var: (rf/init! reagent/adapter); (5) SSR gets its own JVM-side adapter following same pattern (Option (b) from earlier discussion — symmetry with browser substrates). Sweep all examples (counter, login, todomvc, realworld, ssr, etc.) to use explicit form. Spec edits: 006 §init!, API.md, 011-SSR §init flow, MIGRATION.md additive entry. Drops :rf.error/multiple-default-adapters and :rf.error/no-adapter-registered (only :rf.error/no-adapter-specified survives). Blocked-by adapter-canonical naming bead (must pick before; depends on which ns to require).

title: Adapter-canonical naming: rename implementation/substrates/ → implementation/adapters/ + re-frame.substrate.* → re-frame.adapter.* + spec sweep
description: Decision (Mike, 2026-05-10): Option A from naming-discussion. Substrate = abstract contract (spec 006); adapter = each implementation (Reagent, UIx, Helix, SSR). After rf2-zha9 lands: (1) rename implementation/substrates/ → implementation/adapters/; (2) rename CLJS namespaces re-frame.substrate.<name> → re-frame.adapter.<name> across reagent/uix/helix; (3) sweep spec corpus to use 'adapter' for implementations, 'substrate' only for the abstract contract. Maven artefact names (day8/re-frame-2-reagent etc.) unchanged. Pre-1.0 — no back-compat. Touches: implementation/{deps.edn,shadow-cljs.edn}, .github/workflows, .github/scripts/verify-version-lockstep.sh, docs/release-process.md, all spec docs, examples that require the substrate ns, MIGRATION.md additive entry. Comparable scope to rf2-zha9 + a namespace rename. Blocked-by rf2-zha9.

title: Reorganise implementation/ — group substrate adapters under implementation/substrates/
description: Move implementation/{reagent,uix,helix}/ → implementation/substrates/{reagent,uix,helix}/. Direction-set per Mike (2026-05-10). Touches: each substrate's deps.edn (paths inside artefact unchanged); implementation/deps.edn :local/root references; implementation/shadow-cljs.edn source paths; implementation/core/deps.edn test deps; .github/workflows/{test,release}.yml per-artefact paths; .github/scripts/verify-version-lockstep.sh ARTEFACTS list; docs/release-process.md; spec/Conventions.md §Packaging conventions; spec/Ownership.md; spec/MIGRATION.md (additive entry M-N for the move); spec doc cross-refs that mention 'implementation/reagent/' etc. Maven artefact names (day8/re-frame-2-reagent etc.) stay unchanged. Pre-1.0; no back-compat. Comparable scope to rf2-kx74 examples reorg.

title: Flesh out docs/guide/ — 3 priority new chapters + 3 chapter updates (post-session catch-up)
description: Expand docs/guide/ to cover this session's substantial additions. Direction-set scope ladder: (1) update existing chapters 02 / 04 / 05 to reflect multi-substrate world + init! Option 2 + frame-provider + spawn-registry + :rf.machine rename; (2) write 3 new priority chapters: Testing, Devtools/pair-tools, Routing. Stop after 6 deliverables and report; remaining (Schemas, Flows, Production, From-xstate, ch 08/09 updates) can be a follow-up dispatch. Source material: spec/* docs + findings/* + recent PR bodies (PRs 134-179).

title: reg-view + frame-provider context resolution is dead code: subscribe never consults React context, and Reagent stringifies the Provider's value prop
description: spec/002 §Reading the frame from React context defines a 3-tier resolution chain (dynamic var → React context → :rf/default) for subscribe/dispatch. The CLJS implementation has TWO current-frame fns: re-frame.frame/current-frame (CLJS+JVM shared, dynamic var only) and re-frame.views/current-frame (CLJS, full chain including React context). subscribe (re-frame.subs.cljc) calls frame/current-frame — the React-context tier is silently bypassed. Discovered while implementing rf2-d3k3 (plain-fn warning) — the warning's contract assumes subscribe route to :rf/default for plain fns; in practice ALL fns under a non-default frame-provider also route to :rf/default because subscribe never consults the React context. Compounding issue: Reagent's convert-prop-value (reagent.impl.template) stringifies named values when passed as React props, so even if subscribe consulted (.-context cmp) on a reg-view-wrapped component, ctx would be a string (not a keyword) and views/current-frame's (when (keyword? ctx) ctx) check returns nil. Two fixes likely needed: (1) wire subscribe (and dispatch's frame default) to consult re-frame.views/current-frame on CLJS via late-bind so the React-context tier becomes live; (2) views/current-frame must accept stringified-keyword shape from Reagent's prop conversion (mirror the workaround rf2-d3k3 added in read-react-context-frame). Found by browser-runner test in cross_spec_cljs_test/plain-fn-under-non-default-frame: _currentValue on the React Context object is the string "tenant-plain-fn-warn" not the keyword :tenant-plain-fn-warn. Note: the existing reg-view + frame-provider integration is not currently exercised by any cross-namespace browser-runner test that asserts subscribe routes to the surrounding frame — that's why this has stayed hidden.

title: spec/010-Schemas.md re-enable-validation-in-prod escape hatches contradict C-000.35 strict elision
description: From rf2-wq0a (PR #169) drift survey. spec/010-Schemas.md has 3 sites that imply a re-enable-validation-in-prod escape hatch: L92 (reg-app-schema row 'prod (when re-enabled): log and proceed'), L94 ('post-handler app-db failures roll back in dev' implying a prod branch), L118 (per-step recovery 'Prod (when validation re-enabled)...'). C-000.35 (just landed in 000-Vision) ratifies strict elision: 'production elision MUST NOT alter the value of a frame's state nor the order or identity of dispatched events.' These 010 escape hatches contradict C-000.35. The legitimate production-side validation surface is the boundary-validation interceptor at 010 L130-148; that should be the canonical home for any prod validation, not the dev-time validate-* fns being opted back in. Direction: align 010 to C-000.35 — either drop the re-enabling language entirely OR redirect 'prod validation' references to point at the boundary-interceptor. Mike's call which framing is clearer.

title: Cover machine guard / action 3-arity escape hatch (rf2-hbt1 follow-up)
description: From rf2-o423 test coverage audit (top-3 #3 + most surprising finding). The 3-arity guard/action escape hatch is canonically documented in 'implementation/machines/src/re_frame/machines.cljc:78-118' with a runtime arglist-inspection dispatcher ('declares-3-arity?'). Documentation explicitly flags variadics (e.g. (constantly ...)) as a footgun. **Not a single test in the suite uses 3-arity guards or actions.** Every machine test is 2-arity. A 'declares-3-arity?' refactor — especially around variadics — would be invisible to the 1413-assertion test suite. Required tests: (a) register a machine with a 3-arity guard, exercise transitions, assert the third arg (the spec's expected ':data'/'event'/extra binding) reaches the guard correctly. (b) Same for 3-arity ':action'. (c) Variadic edge cases: '(constantly true)' as guard (variadic-fn case mentioned in docstring as footgun). Live tests, not just JVM unit. P2 because it is silent coverage drift on a documented public surface.
close_reason: Resolved via PR #156 — 3-arity guard/action escape hatch coverage merged (commit ea271fc). Real-bug follow-up rf2-l04j was filed during this work and has also been addressed via PR #159.

title: Late-bind missing-artefact error path coverage for all 6 per-feature splits
description: From rf2-o423 test coverage audit (top-3 #2). All per-feature splits (schemas/machines/routing/flows/http/ssr) raise a documented error when a consumer calls a re-exported surface but the artefact is absent from the classpath (e.g. ':rf.error/flows-artefact-missing'). NONE of the splits have a test asserting this error contract. The contract is currently only documented in prose; broken absent-handling would not be caught in CI. Test shape: per-artefact, simulate the artefact-absent classpath state, call the re-exported surface, assert the documented error is raised with the documented payload shape. Probably implemented as a JVM test that ad-hoc strips the artefact's deps from classpath OR a CLJS test that registers the late-bind hook as nil and exercises the surface. Per-artefact: schemas, machines, routing, flows, http, ssr. P2 because the absent-artefact contract is the pre-beta promise to consumers picking artefacts a la carte.
close_reason: Resolved via PR #148 — late-bind missing-artefact error path coverage for all 6 per-feature splits (commit ef06f1d).

title: Permanent bundle-isolation CI gate for per-feature splits (counter as canonical no-feature app)
description: From rf2-o423 test coverage audit (findings/test-coverage-audit-2026-05-09.md, top-3 #1). Each per-feature split (schemas / machines / routing / flows / http / ssr / epoch-when-landed) was verified at PR-time with a one-shot bundle-isolation grep against the counter example bundle. None has a permanent CI test asserting the isolation contract. A future change accidentally re-importing a split-out namespace into core would not be caught. Counter is the canonical no-feature app — it imports zero per-feature artefacts. CI test should: (1) shadow-cljs release counter, (2) grep the produced main.js for each artefact's namespace markers, (3) assert 0 hits per artefact (or a stable allow-list of consumer-side preset-map keywords). Lives ideally as a step in test.yml gating the topological deploy DAG. P2 because bundle-size guarantees are a load-bearing pre-1.0 promise.
close_reason: Resolved via PR #149 — permanent bundle-isolation CI gate via counter (commits 53b783b, f86d0e4).

title: Suggestion: review stale nil cache path when subscribing before a sub is first registered
description:
Suggestion for further agent review on suitability and possible action.

Review finding:
A subscribe against an unregistered sub appears to cache a reaction with  ody-fn nil. If the sub is then registered for the first time, the registrar takes the first-registration path rather than the replacement path, so the existing cached entry is not invalidated. An early subscriber can therefore remain stuck on 
il until the cache slot is cleared or disposed.

Observed surface:
- implementation/core/src/re_frame/subs.cljc: compute-and-cache! emits :rf.error/no-such-sub when lookup fails but still stores a cache entry.
- implementation/core/src/re_frame/registrar.cljc: first-time egister! emits :rf.registry/handler-registered but does not notify replacement hooks.
- implementation/core/src/re_frame/subs.cljc: subscribe prefers cache hits, so the stale entry can survive the later registration.

Why this may merit action:
This looks like a boot-order / lazy-load correctness edge case. It could surface if code subscribes before optional namespaces have loaded their registrations.

Suggested review questions:
- Should missing-subscribe attempts avoid caching altogether?
- Alternatively, should first registration of a :sub invalidate any pre-existing cache entries keyed by that sub id?
- What regression test best captures the sequence: subscribe missing sub -> register sub -> subscribe again?

Possible action, if the review agrees:
Prevent caching on missing-sub lookup or add an explicit invalidation path on first registration, then cover the sequence with a focused regression test.
close_reason: Resolved via PR #161 — sub-cache stale nil regression fixed via skip-cache-write for no-such-sub miss + tests + spec invariants (commits 745e9e3, 18c4256, aa4f425).

title: Suggestion: review whether clear-sub should invalidate materialized subscription cache entries
description:
Suggestion for further agent review on suitability and possible action.

Review finding:
f/clear-sub currently unregisters the registrar entry but does not invalidate already-materialized per-frame subscription cache entries. Because subscribe serves cache hits before consulting the registrar, a cleared sub can continue returning stale values until the cache is explicitly cleared or naturally disposed.

Observed surface:
- implementation/core/src/re_frame/subs.cljc: clear-sub delegates directly to registrar removal.
- implementation/core/src/re_frame/subs.cljc: subscribe returns cached :reaction on hit before re-checking the registrar.
- implementation/core/src/re_frame/registrar.cljc: unregister/clear-kind do not trigger the sub replacement hook used by re-registration invalidation.

Why this may merit action:
This looks like a correctness gap for a public API surface re-exported from e-frame.core, and it can also affect tooling or hot-reload flows that rely on clearing subs.

Suggested review questions:
- Is clear-sub intended to guarantee that subsequent reads behave as though the sub is gone immediately?
- Should unregister / clear-kind for :sub also evict matching cache entries across frames, analogous to the existing re-registration hook?
- Are dedicated tests needed for clear-sub id and clear-sub with no args against pinned cache entries?

Possible action, if the review agrees:
Invalidate matching sub-cache entries across frames when subs are cleared, and add regression coverage for stale-cache reads after clear.
close_reason: Resolved via PR #161 — clear-sub pin-behaviour pinned via tests + spec invariants alongside rf2-l9u5 (commits 18c4256, aa4f425).

title: Conventions :rf/route vs corpus-wide :route for the route slice — Conventions is the outlier
description:
Audit rf2-smpt finding F4, ranked top-1 most load-bearing.

Quote (audit-conventions table F4 line 56): 'Conventions says the route slice lives at :rf/route. 012-Routing canonically says :route (no rf/ prefix), API.md's framework-sub list shows :route, Runtime-Architecture.md:124 says ":rf/machines, :route". Conventions is the outlier; the rest of the corpus uses :route. This is the single highest-impact cross-spec inconsistency in the file.'

Audit §4 Top-1 (line 79): 'Conventions.md is alone in saying the route slice lives at :rf/route. Every other relevant spec (012, API, Runtime-Architecture) says :route. Conventions is supposed to be the canonical home for reserved app-db keys; if Conventions disagrees with 012, then either 012 is wrong about the runtime contract, or the migration agent is going to flag legitimate user code as a collision against a key that doesn't actually exist.'

Cross-spec inconsistency per audit-criterion #2: Conventions §Reserved app-db keys table (line 99) lists :rf/route; 012-Routing.md:225,933 + API.md:112 + Runtime-Architecture.md:124 use :route. A migration agent following Conventions would flag user code that writes to :route as legitimate, and ignore it as collision against :rf/route — exactly backwards.

Resolution per audit R-S3: 'Decide :route vs :rf/route. Spec corpus consensus is :route; Conventions is the outlier. Two-character edit (s/:rf\/route/:route/g in the Reserved app-db keys table cell). Then re-check the §Reserved namespaces :rf.route/* row and the §Reserved sub-ids list — [:rf/route] appears as a sub-id alongside [:rf.route/id] etc., which itself is muddled (sub-id is :route, family-prefix is :rf.route/*).'
notes:
2026-05-09 — investigation by rf2-ljw6 (halt-and-report)

CORPUS DISAGREEMENT confirmed: the audit (rf2-smpt F4) claims "the rest of the corpus uses :route" but discovery shows the corpus is split, NOT consistent.

Route slice in app-db (the :rf/route vs :route question):
  Uses :route (no rf/ prefix):
    - 012-Routing.md:225,933 (canonical: "The runtime maintains a single slice in app-db under the :route key")
    - Spec-Schemas.md:549 ("The shape of app-db's :route slice")
    - Runtime-Architecture.md:124 (":rf/machines, :route")
    - API.md:134 (":route" framework sub returns "the full :route slice")
    - Cross-Spec-Interactions.md:81 ("the resolved :route slice")
  Uses :rf/route (with prefix):
    - Conventions.md:108 (the table cell flagged by audit)
    - 002-Frames.md:69 ("[:rf/route] — the route slice for url-bound frames")
    - 011-SSR.md:67 (":rf/route (carried inside :rf/app-db under the :route slice)" — internally contradicts itself)
    - MIGRATION.md:771-772 — explicit v1 → v2 rename rows: "[:route] (framework sub) → [:rf/route]" and "app-db [:route] (slice key) → app-db [:rf/route]"
    - Conventions.md:286 (substrate-adapter table — :rf/route reg-sub family)
    - Conventions.md:315 (rationale paragraph — :rf/route reg-sub family)

MIGRATION.md is particularly load-bearing: it asserts the v2 target IS :rf/route. So either MIGRATION is wrong (and Conventions+002+MIGRATION need updating) OR 012/Spec-Schemas/Runtime-Architecture/API.md are wrong (and they need updating).

Per the rf2-ljw6 brief's "if the corpus disagrees, HALT" rule, no spec edits made. Branch rf2-ljw6-conventions-route-fix created (clean), worktree torn down.

Sibling discrepancies (F22, F15) — corpus IS consistent, but withheld pending Mike's D1 call to keep the §Reserved namespaces table change as one logical sweep:

D2 (F22): Conventions:22 says "[:rf.machine <id>]" for the framework sub-id; corpus consensus is :rf/machine (single-segment kw): 005-StateMachines.md:1546 "(rf/reg-sub :rf/machine ...)"; API.md "(rf/sub-machine ...)" sugar over [:rf/machine ...]; 002-Frames.md:1105.

D3 (F15): Conventions:24 lists ":rf.route/url-changed" in the routing-events list; corpus consensus is :rf/url-changed: 012-Routing.md:32,301,531,595,712,847,926; API.md:124; Construction-Prompts.md:745,830,834,841; Conventions.md:286 (substrate-adapter row, internally contradicts the line-24 cell).

Recommendation: settle D1 first (Mike's call: prefix or no prefix on the route slice key), then a single sweep can fix D1+D2+D3 plus the spec(s) that are actually wrong.

title: Tool-Pair surface behaviour against destroyed frames is unspecified (epoch-history, restore-epoch, get-frame-db, register-epoch-cb)
description:
Audit rf2-ehpu finding F6, ranked top-2 most load-bearing.

Quote (audit-tool-pair line 28): 'Tool-Pair commits consumers to the pattern: "walks the epoch history of a frame, snapshot state at each point, restore an earlier state." It does NOT say what (rf/epoch-history frame-id), (rf/restore-epoch frame-id epoch-id), (rf/register-epoch-cb), or (rf/get-frame-db frame-id) do when called against a destroyed frame. 002 §destroy-frame defines :reason :frame-destroyed for dispatch/subscribe but does not extend the contract to the Tool-Pair query surface. A pair tool watching epochs while the user calls (rf/destroy-frame …) has no spec-defined behaviour to handle: nil? throw? empty vec? :rf.error/no-such-handler (kind :frame)? Tool-Pair must close this — it's the most likely "registration race" failure mode.'

Why load-bearing (audit-tool-pair §3 Top 2): re-frame2's multi-frame architecture makes frame churn ordinary — story tools mount and unmount frames freely; pair tools probe ephemeral test frames. A pair tool consuming epoch-history is guaranteed to encounter destroyed-frame races over enough wall-clock. The spec's silence forces consumers to discover behaviour empirically and likely diverge across implementations.

Pair2 corroboration (audit §5): runtime.cljs pair-dispatch-sync! (line 443+) has explicit failure-mode branching for 'no epoch recorded after dispatch' — depth=0, frame destroyed, interop/debug-enabled? false. Pair2 inferred them from the surface and built defensive logic that should have been specified.

Resolution per audit R-M (medium): add a §Tool-Pair surface against destroyed frames subsection; recommended shape — read APIs return nil (or empty vec for collection-shaped returns), mutating APIs throw :rf.error/no-such-handler (kind :frame), listeners stop receiving epoch records from destroyed frame on next emit boundary.

title: Tool-Pair source-coord attribute: "opaque + parseable" + missing schema + 3-spec format drift
description:
Audit rf2-ehpu finding F1, ranked top-1 most load-bearing.

Quote (audit-tool-pair line 23): 'Tool-Pair §Source-mapping says the attribute value is "the contract (an opaque string the tool parses to recover :ns / :line / :file — schema in [Spec-Schemas])". Three faults in one sentence: (a) "contract" + "opaque" is self-cancelling — either it's parseable (contract) or it's not (opaque); pair2's STATUS.md surfaces the same tension and resolves it pragmatically against the consumer's interest; (b) it lists :file as recoverable from the attribute, but Spec 006 §Attribute value format defines the format as <ns>:<sym>:<line>:<col> with no :file component (the <sym> is the registry handler-id, NOT the file); (c) the cited Spec-Schemas schema does not exist (grep source-coord against Spec-Schemas.md returns zero matches).'

Why load-bearing (audit-tool-pair §3): pair2's STATUS.md explicitly resolves the tension by parsing pragmatically and warning consumers it might break; intersects multiple specs (Tool-Pair, 006, Spec-Schemas) with three different shapes; every pair-shaped consumer that wants click-back-to-source — which is most of them — WILL parse the attribute. Cross-spec inconsistency report (audit-tool-pair §4 row 1): Tool-Pair line 116 says :ns/:line/:file recoverable; Spec 006 §Attribute value format says <ns>:<sym>:<line>:<col> (no :file). Severity: HIGH.

F9 also bundled (audit-tool-pair line 31): 'Tool-Pair line 54 promises :ns/:line/:file/:column on every registration's metadata. Spec 001 §Source-coordinate capture lists only :ns/:line/:file (no :column). Spec 006 §Capture mechanism captures :line/:column from (meta &form). Consumer reading 001's table writes a click-back resolver against (handler-meta) without expecting :column.' Same root cause: source-coord shape inconsistent across 001/006/Tool-Pair/Spec-Schemas.

Resolution per audit R-M (medium): pick (a) commit the format as part of the public contract — declare 4-segment shape in Tool-Pair, add Spec-Schemas entry for :rf/source-coord-attr, kill 'opaque' framing; OR (b) commit a parsing helper (rf/parse-source-coord attr-string). Either way, sync recovery list with Spec 006's actual format, and reconcile :column claim.

title: Spec 009 claims "each error category has a registered Malli :tags schema" but Spec-Schemas registers only 2 of ~25
description:
Audit rf2-ra02 finding F-9.

Quote (audit-009 line 53): 'Per-category :tags schemas are described as "registered as a Malli schema" (line 482) but only TWO are shown — HandlerExceptionTags and SchemaValidationTags (line 491–509) — under a "Conceptual; the actual registration mechanism is implementation-specific" caveat. Spec-Schemas does NOT register schemas for the other ~25 categories. Either (a) every category needs its :tags schema in Spec-Schemas (the natural home for them), or (b) 009 should drop the "each category has a registered Malli schema" claim and just say the example shape is illustrative. The spec promises "consumers can validate / branch on the payload safely" (line 430) but a consumer trying to validate, say, :rf.error/no-such-sub against a registered schema today would not find one.'

Audit confidence note (audit-009 line 208): 'I confirmed Spec-Schemas does not register them by searching for the category names directly. I did NOT check the implementation tree to see if register-app-schema! is called for each category at boot. If it is, the spec is just under-documented; if it isn't, this is a real coverage gap.'

Schema-coverage gap per audit-criterion #4: documented payload (the per-category :tags shape) has no entry in Spec-Schemas.

Resolution per audit R-M3: decide if 'every category has a registered Malli schema' is pattern requirement, CLJS-reference requirement, or aspirational. If pattern-level, Spec-Schemas needs the ~25 schemas. If CLJS-only, narrow 009 line 482's claim.
close_reason: Resolved via PR #154 — ~65 missing :tags schemas registered in Spec-Schemas (commit a6b8ea8).

title: Spec 005 has two precedence lists for transition resolution that disagree
description:
Audit rf2-goxn finding #9.

Quote (audit-005 line 24): 'Two precedence lists for transition resolution disagree. L211 (§Wildcard transitions): "state-local explicit → state-local :* → top-level explicit → top-level :*". L760 (§Transition resolution): "leaf up to root… explicit, :*, parent explicit, parent :*…". Real disagreement on whether a parent's :* fires before walking up to grandparent's explicit.'

Internal contradiction per audit-criterion: spec A says X, spec B says Y, both can't be right; an AI implementor following one would build something that diverges from the other.

Resolution per audit §5 medium: 'Reconcile the two precedence lists at L211 and L760. One canonical order (deepest explicit → deepest wildcard → walk up → root explicit → root wildcard) — remove the other.'

title: Spec 009 trace category coverage gap: ~25 emitted categories absent from §Error categories / §op-type vocabulary tables
description:
Audit rf2-ra02 findings F-2, F-3, F-5, F-6, F-7 plus rf2-8syd F13 and rf2-goxn #7, #8 — bundled because they all describe the same root cause: 009's §Error categories table and §op-type vocabulary section are incomplete relative to §Where trace emission lives and to actual runtime emit sites. 009 §Error contract opens (line 407) by claiming 'This section is the authoritative model for re-frame2's error taxonomy … the single source of truth for category names, payload shapes, and recovery defaults.' That claim is materially false today.

ra02 F-2 (most load-bearing): ~25 missing categories the runtime emits but 009 does not tabulate — :rf.error/no-such-fx (fx.cljc), :rf.error/frame-destroyed (router.cljc), :rf.error/flow-eval-exception (router.cljc), :rf.warning/route-shadowed-by-equal-score (routing.cljc), all :rf.http/* and :rf.warning/decode-defaulted from http_managed.cljc, plus EVERY :rf.error/machine-* category enumerated in spec/005 (machine-action-wrote-db, machine-grammar-not-in-v1, machine-unresolved-guard, machine-unresolved-action, machine-unhandled-event, machine-state-not-in-definition, machine-snapshot-version-mismatch, machine-raise-depth-exceeded, machine-always-depth-exceeded, machine-always-self-loop, machine-compound-state-missing-initial). The 'five distinct namespace prefixes' claim at line 434 is genuinely six once 014 is folded in (:rf.http/ is unmentioned).

ra02 F-3: Six :rf.epoch/restore-* categories (Tool-Pair lines 79–87) are not registered in 009's table. Tool-Pair canonicalises them; 009 also claims taxonomy authority. The two specs disagree on which is canonical.

ra02 F-5: :rf.machine/spawned and :rf.machine/destroyed (009 line 222 emit) are not in 009's §op-type vocabulary section, which lists only :rf.machine.lifecycle/created / destroyed. The same destroy event fires from two emit sites under two different operation names.

ra02 F-6: :rf.machine/system-id-bound and :rf.machine/system-id-released (Spec 005 §1203–1204) absent from 009. rf2-suue added the :rf.error/system-id-collision row but missed the symmetric -bound/-released events.

ra02 F-7: :rf.machine.microstep/transition (documented in Spec 005 §887–892, registered in Spec-Schemas §195/§202) absent from 009 §op-type AND §Where trace emission lives.

8syd F13: :rf.frame/drain-aborted mentioned only in 002 line 935 + 009 line 224, but not in 009 §Error categories table or Spec-Schemas. An AI implementor cannot tell op-type, :tags, or :recovery.

goxn #7: 005 lists :rf.machine/raised (L1960) — never emitted. 009 lists :rf.machine.lifecycle/destroyed — never emitted. Drop or wire.

goxn #8: machines.cljc:501 emits :rf.machine.timer/skipped-on-server but no spec lists it.

Resolution per ra02 R-M1: audit every emit site in §Where trace emission lives against the §Error categories table and §op-type vocabulary; file the missing rows. Mechanical work, not a design decision. Note: rf2-2s1o landed :rf.flow/* rows but did not address the broader gap.
close_reason: Resolved via PR #154 — Spec 009 trace category coverage sweep (~50 missing categories added) (commit 013c48f).

title: Spec 002 reg-frame metadata grammar drifts from Spec-Schemas / Spec 009 (ghost keys + enum drift)
description:
Audit rf2-8syd findings F1, F4, plus rf2-ra02 F-10 — three independent inline drifts from Spec-Schemas as canonical source.

F1 (audit-002 line 23): The reg-frame canonical grammar list does NOT include :on-error or :platform. Both are referenced as preset expansion keys (002 line 232–233) and :on-error is referenced again at 002 line 927. The :rf/frame-meta schema (Spec-Schemas line 896–897) carries both. 009 §Error-handler policy says :on-error is registered per-frame via reg-frame metadata (per [002-Frames §:on-error]) — but 002 has no §:on-error section. Quote: 'The reg-frame canonical grammar lists :doc :on-create :on-destroy :fx-overrides :interceptors :drain-depth :ns :line :file but does NOT list :on-error or :platform.' Resolution: 002 is incomplete; both other specs are correct. Add the keys to the canonical grammar.

F4 (audit-002 line 26): :source enum values disagree across three locations. 002 line 330 lists 5 values (:ui :timer :http :repl :machine); 002 line 366 lists 6 (adds :ssr-hydration); Spec-Schemas :rf/dispatch-envelope (line 94) ships 8 (:ui :timer :http :machine :repl :ssr-hydration :test :other). Resolution: replace 002's inline enumerations with a pointer to :rf/dispatch-envelope.

ra02 F-10 (audit-009): :operation is <kw-or-vec> per 009 §Core fields line 25, but :keyword per Spec-Schemas §:rf/trace-event line 183. Pick one (Spec-Schemas matches every consumer pattern).

Common root cause: 002 and 009 inline grammars/enums for fields whose canonical schemas live in Spec-Schemas, and the duplications drift. Fix: replace inline lists with pointers to Spec-Schemas, or lock the order and widen.
close_reason: Resolved via PR #155 — Spec 002 reg-frame metadata grammar aligned via cross-reference fixes (commit 567ba79).

title: Add :flow op-type + :rf.flow/* trace vocabulary to spec 009 + flows instrumentation (toolpair audit #3)
description: Decision (Mike 2026-05-09): yes — file and implement. Required for re-frame-10x v2 flow panel. Spec edits: 009 add :flow op-type; define :rf.flow/registered, :rf.flow/computed, :rf.flow/skip (value-equal recompute suppression per rf2-719e), :rf.flow/cleared, :rf.flow/failed. spec 013 (verify filename) new §Flow tracing cross-reference. Implementation: flows.cljc emits via (re-frame.trace/emit! :flow :rf.flow/<event> payload) — same pattern as machines.cljc:896. Tool-Pair.md: consumers register-trace-cb! filtering by :flow op-type. Tests: each lifecycle event fires; :rf.flow/skip semantics; conformance fixture flow-lifecycle-emits-traces (host-agnostic EDN). Sequencing: spec 009 also edited by rf2-suue and Performance API impl — different sections, merges cleanly.
close_reason: Resolved via PR #138 — :flow op-type + :rf.flow/* trace vocabulary shipped (commits 1770068, e2f0c2d, 653739c).

title: Performance API instrumentation for prod timing — behind a compile-time flag (toolpair audit #2)
description: Decision (Mike 2026-05-09): replace v1 span-shape (spec 009 already drops it) with browser Performance API. Debug builds: skip (browsers too noisy). Prod builds: behind a goog-define flag, default OFF, Closure DCE elides when off. No 10x v1 back-compat (v2 is rewrite). Documented in: spec/009-Instrumentation.md (new §Performance instrumentation), spec/Tool-Pair.md (consumption surface), spec/API.md (configure flag). Mechanism: (goog-define ^boolean re-frame.performance/enabled? false) + closure-defines toggle. Initial scope marks/measures: event dispatch, sub recompute, fx execution, render. Naming rf:event:<id>, rf:sub:<id>, rf:fx:<key>, rf:render:<frame-id>. Tests: helper round-trip; bundle-grep counter 0 calls under :advanced+flag=false; presence under flag=true; browser smoke for measure entries. Sequencing: spec 009 also edited by rf2-suue (small additive row) and the :flow op-type bead — different sections, merges cleanly.
close_reason: Resolved via PR #139 — Performance API instrumentation behind compile-time flag (commits 97bd915, 8ec6950, 263a0ff, 9227143).

title: Source-coord instrumentation for state machines (guards / actions / entry-exit / on-spawn / reg-machine site)
description: Gap (per Mike verification 2026-05-09): reg-machine is a defn, not a macro (machines.cljc:878). No source-coord stamping. Mirror rf2-z7f7 view pattern: turn reg-machine into a macro, capture &form metadata, walk machine spec at expansion to stamp :ns/:line/:column on each guard/action/entry/exit/on-spawn reference. Production-elision gated on goog.DEBUG. Spec edits: 005 §reg-machine + new §Source-coord stamping, 006 cross-ref, Tool-Pair.md, API.md. Tests: stamped meta returns correct coords; bundle-grep counter 0 under :advanced+DEBUG=false. Sequencing: rf2-suue is in flight editing machines.cljc; this bead may rebase against it (different sections, should merge cleanly).
close_reason: Resolved via PR #140 — source-coord stamping for guards/actions/entry-exit/on-spawn/reg-machine site (commits 7da95f6, d1db82d, 2794781, 6a1ca75).

title: Audit spec vs implementation for actionable drift
description: Audit the current /spec against /implementation and record each actionable difference as a possible necessary change. This bead is the audit container; once the comparison is complete, its description/notes will be updated with the concrete drift items, phrased as implementor-facing possible changes rather than mandatory fixes.
notes:
Audit result: filed child beads rf2-4o1c.1 through rf2-4o1c.5 for concrete spec/runtime drift found during the comparison.

New child beads:
- rf2-4o1c.1 - public machine lifecycle fx ids (`:rf.machine/spawn` / `:rf.machine/destroy`) vs implementation's registered ids (`:spawn` / `:destroy-machine`)
- rf2-4o1c.2 - dynamic machine spawn/destroy semantics promised by Spec 005 vs current trace-only runtime behaviour
- rf2-4o1c.3 - fragment-only routing trace op name drift (`:route.url/fragment-changed` vs `:rf.route/url-changed`)
- rf2-4o1c.4 - head-mismatch trace category drift (`:rf.warning/head-mismatch` vs `:rf.ssr/hydration-mismatch` + `:failing-id`)
- rf2-4o1c.5 - head hydration payload keys (`:rf/head`, `:rf/head-hash`) ignored by current `:rf/hydrate` runtime

Already tracked elsewhere; not duplicated here:
- rf2-0gfo - SSR nav-on-server prose vs `:rf.fx/skipped-on-platform`
- rf2-y509 - route-not-found / error-projector contradiction
close_reason: Audit completed; actionable drift filed as child beads.

title: Audit recent feature work for sufficient test coverage (pre-beta verification)
description:
## Scope

Walk PRs merged over the last couple of days and verify each added behaviour has sufficient test coverage. Pre-beta integrity check — gaps surface as follow-up beads to file before v0.0.1.beta release.

## What "sufficient" means here

- New behaviour has a happy-path test.
- Edge cases the spec calls out are tested (boundary conditions, error paths, lifecycle).
- Production-elision contracts are sentinel-tested if the surface is dev-only.
- Host-agnostic surfaces have conformance fixtures (EDN under `spec/conformance/` or wherever the project pattern lives).
- The test isn't just a smoke test of "doesn't crash" — it asserts the documented contract.

## Recent work to audit (chronological, last ~2 days)

### Per-feature artefact splits (rf2-5vjj Strategy B)
- **rf2-p7va schemas split** — first per-feature template.
- **rf2-xbtj machines split** (PR #129) — out-of-core machinery; bundle-isolation grep was 0 (with the strict pattern). Fixed a latent `:spawn` shadowing bug.
- **rf2-k682 routing split** (PR #130) — biggest single-PR bundle drop (−15.6 KB).
- **rf2-tfw3 flows split** (PR #132) — 2 unavoidable consumer-side keyword strings remained.
- **rf2-5kpd HTTP split** (PR #133) — bundle dropped −14.8 KB on counter; managed-http-counter has 12 distinct `:rf.http/*` keywords (full failure taxonomy).

For each split: are there permanent tests verifying:
1. Bundle-isolation grep stays clean (no regression if a future change accidentally re-imports the split-out namespace into core)?
2. Late-bind hooks raise the documented error when the artefact is absent from the classpath?
3. Late-bind hooks resolve correctly when the artefact IS on the classpath?

### Functional features
- **rf2-d0pi auto-inject** (Form-1 views auto-receive `dispatch` / `subscribe` arg). Tests covering: Form-1 happy path, Form-2 wrapping behaviour (does Form-2 still get auto-inject? per Reagent v2 — see feedback memory), Form-3 / not-supported error path?
- **rf2-sixo frame-provider Path 1** (React context for frame propagation). Tests covering: nested component frame inheritance, default-frame fallback, missing-context error path?
- **rf2-yl9n Form 2 canonical** + **rf2-heo2 Form 2 + view canonical, no get-view**. Tests covering the canonical pattern; non-canonical patterns rejected at expansion?
