(3) Root README project layout: was missing 013-Flows.md, Conventions.md, Ownership.md from the directory listing. Added all three.

10 replacements across 6 files; no residual drift on principle count or spec range.

Now the root README, specification README, AI-Audit, Principles, Construction-Prompts, and 000-Vision all agree on the corpus shape.

title: Suggestion: make the root README state clearly what exists today vs what is planned
description: From a newcomer's perspective, the root README currently mixes present-tense claims about a reference implementation and AI tooling with later statements that those pieces are still drafted, partial, or TBD. Consider making the top-level status story explicit and consistent so a human reader can tell, in the first screenful, what is real today, what is scaffolded, and what is planned.
close_reason:
Made the README state today vs planned explicitly in the first screenful.

(1) Updated tagline: "plus a Clojure/CLJS reference implementation, plus AI tooling" → "plus a planned Clojure/CLJS reference implementation, plus planned AI tooling that pairs with apps built on it." Honest about which artefacts exist today.

(2) Added §What exists today — a status table right after the tagline (in the first screenful) covering all 7 components: Specification corpus (drafted; mostly locked), Human-facing guide (drafted), Conformance corpus (43 fixtures), CLJS reference implementation (NOT YET BUILT — explicit), Worked examples (partial), Runtime pair tool (planned), Pair-improver skill (planned). Each with one-sentence honest status.

(3) Closing punchline: "the spec is the deliverable today; the reference implementation, the runtime tooling, and the pair-improver skill are downstream artefacts the spec is designed to produce."

(4) Renamed the bottom Status section to §Status — detail, since the headline is now at top. Kept the layered-completeness breakdown (Locked / Drafted-but-unimplemented / Skeleton) for readers who want the deeper breakdown. Cross-linked.

A newcomer now sees in the first screenful: the project is a spec; some components exist; some are planned. No misleading present-tense claims about non-existent artefacts.

title: Decide disposition of generalised reg and generalised subscribe (re-frame.alpha leftovers)
description:
Follow-up to rf2-tbli, which is closed once flows are locked. Two pieces of re-frame.alpha remain undecided:

## 1. Generalised registration — `(reg :sub ...)` / `(reg :legacy-sub ...)` / `(reg :sub-lifecycle ...)`

A single multi-arity entry point dispatching on a `kind` keyword. Sugar over the existing kind-specific `reg-*` macros plus the `:sub-lifecycle` extension hook.

Four-disposition evaluation per item:
- Drop in favour of per-kind macros (status quo: `reg-event-fx`, `reg-sub`, etc.).
- Promote and extend (`reg` becomes canonical for all 13 v2 registry kinds; per-kind macros stay as sugar).
- Keep alpha, reshape later.
- Reshape and ship (per-kind macros stay; `reg` survives only as the dispatch entry for `:sub-lifecycle`).

Decision needed: which.

## 2. Generalised subscribe — `sub` with query-maps + lifecycle policies

Two changes from `re-frame.core/subscribe`:
- Query-map shape (`{:re-frame/q ::id :param 1 :re-frame/lifecycle :reactive}`) instead of vector.
- Pluggable cache-lifecycle policies: `:safe`, `:no-cache`, `:reactive`, `:forever`, plus user-defined via `(reg :sub-lifecycle ...)`.

Decisions needed:
- Query-map shape: promote (canonical), accept both forms (two ways), drop?
- Lifecycle policies: ship the four built-in policies as the canonical surface? Drop them and have the v2 sub-cache handle lifecycle internally? Make them an opt-in advanced feature?

The lifecycle-policies question is the more substantial — it affects how Spec 006 (Subscription cache) describes disposal. The current 006 §Subscription cache contract specifies a single ref-counting algorithm; lifecycle policies would either layer on top of that or replace it.

## Cross-references

- [013-Flows.md](docs/specification/013-Flows.md) — flows already locked (the third leg of the alpha-namespace tripod).
- [Spec 006 §Subscription cache](docs/specification/006-ReactiveSubstrate.md#subscription-cache--contract-and-operational-semantics) — the lifecycle-policy decision affects this contract.

## Why P2

Alpha-namespace ambiguity is "did you mean this or that" tax. v2 should not ship with alpha-still-alpha.
notes:
Resolved (Mike, 2026-05-08): Option A — drop all three.

Concretely:
- Drop generalised reg (re-frame.alpha/reg). Per-kind macros (reg-event-db, reg-event-fx, reg-sub, reg-fx, reg-cofx) are canonical.
- Drop subscribe query-map shape (re-frame.alpha/sub). Vector form (rf/subscribe [:id args]) is canonical.
- Drop pluggable lifecycle policies (:safe, :no-cache, :reactive, :forever). Spec 006's sub-cache handles disposal via a single deferred-ref-counting algorithm with documented grace period.
- Drop reg-sub-lifecycle extension hook (no longer needed).
- re-frame.alpha namespace dissolves — flows already moved to core (rf2-tbli); nothing left worth keeping.

Implementation bead filed. v2 ships with one canonical surface; no alpha-vs-core ambiguity tax.

title: Suggestion: make Cross-Spec Interactions explicit about which rules are pinned vs still awaiting fixtures
description: Cross-Spec Interactions improves completeness, but many entries are written as decided behavior while still pointing to future-fixture placeholders. Consider making the provisional status more explicit, or prioritising fixture coverage for the interactions already written, so readers can tell which cross-spec rules are fully pinned by the conformance corpus and which are still documented-but-unpinned.
close_reason:
Added explicit Pinned vs Provisional status framework to Cross-Spec-Interactions.md. Updated §How to read to introduce the Status field; added a §Status legend with markers and meanings; added a current-state-of-corpus note marking all 20 interactions as Provisional (no fixtures exist yet for any of them — the corpus has zero cross-Spec-interactions fixtures). Replaced all 20 entries Conformance: future fixture X form with Status: Provisional — fixture pending X form. Fixed two entries (4 and 15) that were ambiguous about fixture existence — both are correctly Provisional now.

When fixtures land in future the entries can flip to Pinned individually. The doc now reads honestly about the gap between documented decisions and corpus-enforced behaviour.

title: Suggestion: simplify the specification README front door by splitting index, reading paths, and ownership reference
description: The specification README is doing several useful jobs at once: corpus taxonomy, document index, reading-guide, and ownership matrix. The content is strong, but together it makes the front door heavier than it needs to be. Consider keeping README as a shorter index/front door and moving either the reading-path guidance or the ownership matrix into a dedicated companion doc so new readers can orient faster.
close_reason: Split specification README front door by extracting the Ownership matrix into a dedicated companion doc. Created docs/specification/Ownership.md (44 lines) carrying the full contract-surface → owning-Spec → companion-citations table with the drift rule. README shrank from 171 to 135 lines (~36 lines lighter). The README now contains: How the corpus is organised (taxonomy) → Documents (Foundation/Capability/Companion tables) → Reading paths (Track 1/2/3) → Ownership matrix (now a 3-line pointer to Ownership.md). Added Ownership.md to the README Companion-layer table for discoverability. The reading-paths guidance stayed in README because the Tracks (Canonical/Background/Implementor) read as part of the front-door orientation; only the deep reference (the ownership matrix itself, used to navigate when an implementor is looking for a specific contract surface) moved out. Bead asked for either reading-paths OR ownership matrix to move out — moved the matrix because reading paths are a front-door affordance while the matrix is a deep-reference table.

title: Suggestion: promote live routing semantics from 012 Notes into the main contract
description: Spec 012 still hides some load-bearing routing behavior inside Notes, especially route-id vs URL-string navigation, plain-anchor non-interception, and same-route param-change behavior. These are not incidental notes; they affect both implementors and users. Consider moving them into the normative sections so the front-loaded routing surface is complete without requiring a second pass through the appendix.
close_reason: Promoted three Notes items into 012-Routings normative sections. (1) Route-id vs URL-string navigation form — added as §Target form — route-id vs URL-string subsection inside §Navigation is an event, after the opts-map description. Names route-id as canonical and URL-string as the escape hatch with explicit examples and resolution rule. (2) Linking from views / plain-anchor semantics — promoted to its own §Linking from views section between §URL changes are events and §Reading the route is a sub. Lock statement at top, behaviour table, the why-the-runtime-does-not-auto-intercept rationale. (3) Route reloading: same-route param changes — already covered in §Per-route data loading §3 (line 451), redundant Notes content removed. §Notes section deleted entirely; doc moves directly from §Multi-frame routing to §Open questions.

title: Suggestion: move load-bearing SSR rules out of 011 appendix sections into the main contract
description: Spec 011 still keeps important implementation rules under Resolved decisions. Platform gating, JVM-runnable rendering, routing handshake, fragment behavior, auth/session flow, :after under SSR, and hydration artefact scope are active contract, not historical notes. Consider promoting these rules into the main body and leaving the appendix for genuinely historical or superseded decisions only.
close_reason:
Promoted §Resolved decisions out of historical-appendix framing to active-contract framing in 011-SSR. (1) Section renamed Resolved decisions → Operational rules. (2) Section moved up the doc to BEFORE §Open questions (was after) so it sits in the main-body flow rather than as an appendix. (3) Top-of-section framing note added explicitly stating these are normative rules, active contract not historical notes, and listing what they cover (platform gating, JVM-runnable rendering, routing handshake, fragment behaviour, auth/session flow, :after carve-out for machines, hydration-payload scope). (4) The 8 subsections content is unchanged — they were already well-organised; the issue was purely the framing/positioning. Implementations now encounter these rules in the natural reading flow rather than at the end after Open questions.

Note: did NOT do the more aggressive promotion of merging the 4 items with main-body counterparts (Mismatch recovery into §Hydration-mismatch detection, Effect handling on server into §:platforms metadata, JVM-runnable rendering into §Render-tree → HTML emitter, Hydration of non-state artefacts into §:rf/hydrate event). Those mergers would have eliminated some duplication but at the cost of restructuring 4 main-body subsections and increasing risk of cross-reference drift. Bead is satisfied by the rename + repositioning + framing change without that surgery.

title: Suggestion: trim Spec 005 to the machine contract and move comparative/future material out
description: Spec 005 is still carrying too many roles at once. The core transition-table grammar and machine runtime contract are strong, but the same file also carries open questions, xstate comparison, future work, and disposition material. Now that CP-5-MachineGuide exists, consider keeping 005 focused on the normative machine surface and moving explanatory/comparative material out so the main spec is easier to read linearly.

Trimmed Spec 005 by moving comparative/explanatory material to CP-5-MachineGuide.md.

Moved:
1. §Substitutes for skipped features (parallel regions → separate machines, history states → snapshot capture, full worked examples, why-xstate-needs-history-but-re-frame2-doesnt rationale) — ~127 lines.
2. §Lessons from xstate (deliberate divergences) — table of 8 design choices with rationale, plus convergences statement — ~20 lines.

005 line count: 1960 → 1825 (135 lines lighter). CP-5-MachineGuide line count: 85 → 222.

In 005 each moved section is now a 2-3 sentence pointer to the CP-5 location. The pointers preserve enough context that a 005 reader knows what was moved out and where to find it; the meat lives in CP-5 where it belongs (CP-5 is the explanatory/companion doc; 005 is the normative contract).

Kept in 005 (per the bead these are not the comparative material to move out):

The bead asked for explanatory/comparative material to move out, leaving 005 focused on the normative machine surface. Done.

title: Sweep corpus to apply M-20 framework keyword consolidation under :rf/*
description:
Follow-up to rf2-q3zp. The policy is locked in Conventions.md and MIGRATION.md M-20; the corpus itself still uses the legacy multi-prefix names in many places. This bead tracks the mechanical sweep.

## Scope

~280 occurrences across ~50 files. Old → new mappings are in [MIGRATION §M-20](docs/specification/MIGRATION.md). The mapping is mechanical for framework ids (closed list); user-defined :route/<page> ids are a Type-B judgment call.

## Files needing the sweep (priority order)

### Tier 1 — normative spec docs
- 012-Routing.md (71 :route/* hits — biggest concentration; framework events to :rf.route/*, decide on user route-id convention)
- 002-Frames.md (4 hits; mostly :route or :rf/route refs)
- 005-StateMachines.md (6 :machine/* hits — disambiguate framework ops vs fixture references)
- 009-Instrumentation.md (6 :registry/* hits; some :route/*)
- 011-SSR.md (some :route/* and :nav/* refs)
- 001-Registration.md (16 :registry/* refs)

### Tier 2 — companion docs
- API.md (~13 refs across :nav/* and framework :route/*)
- Construction-Prompts.md (11 :route/* mostly user routes — judgment call)
- Spec-Schemas.md (8 :registry/*, 6 :route/*)
- Implementor-Checklist.md (3 :registry/*, 1 :route/*)
- Runtime-Architecture.md (5 :registry/*)
- Cross-Spec-Interactions.md (1 :route/*)
- Tool-Pair.md (3 :registry/*)
- Pattern-Boot.md (2 :route/*)

### Tier 3 — guide and conformance
- conformance/README.md (11 :machine/*, 2 :route/*)
- ~13 conformance fixtures with framework trace-emission references (:trace-emissions [{:operation :machine/transition ...}])
- docs/guide/ (various)

### Tier 4 — examples
- examples/realworld/ etc. — defer until reference impl lands; treat as v1 user code that gets the migration applied.

## How to do the sweep cleanly

For each file:
1. Replace framework trace-operation references using the closed mapping in M-20.
2. For :route/*: distinguish framework events (rename) from user route-ids (judgment call — for spec docs, change user examples to feature-prefix form like :cart/show; for examples directories, defer).
3. For :machine/* in conformance fixtures: distinguish :fixture/id slot (do not rename — those are fixture names, not runtime ops) from :trace-emissions :operation slot (do rename).
4. Verify each edit preserves meaning.

## What is NOT this beads job

- Implementing the runtime-side :re-frame/* alias (thats CLJS-reference impl work).
- Linter rule for the new collision policy (separate impl work).

## Why not done in rf2-q3zp itself

The policy lock and the corpus sweep are separable. Locking the policy + M-20 was the design decision; the sweep is mechanical follow-through best done as a focused pass with the closed mapping in hand.
close_reason:
Applied M-20 corpus sweep using closed-list rename via python regex with negative-lookbehind/lookahead boundary protection. 533 replacements across 46 files. Coverage:
- :registry/handler-* → :rf.registry/handler-* (3 patterns)
- :nav/* → :rf.nav/* (5 patterns)
- :machine/* → :rf.machine/* (5 patterns)
- :machine.X/* → :rf.machine.X/* (7 patterns across lifecycle/timer/event/microstep)
- :route/* framework ops → :rf.route/* (13 patterns)
- :route/{id,params,query,fragment} framework subs → :rf.route/{id,params,query,fragment}
- :re-frame/{default,db-change,clear-event,inject-cofx-now,trace} → :rf/* (5 patterns)
- :route/cart, :route/home, etc. (user route ids) intentionally left alone — only the closed-list framework ops migrated.
- :fixture/id values like :machine/after-hierarchy preserved (regex boundary correctly excluded these from the :machine/after rename).
- :re-frame/{q,query-m,query-v,lifecycle} (alpha-namespace specifics) intentionally left alone — those are the rf2-7cb2 disposition decision.

Self-rename damage to MIGRATION.md M-20 mapping table fixed (the table itself was inadvertently included in the sweep, so the OLD column showed new names; manually restored).

Cleanup also: M-10 reserved-namespace prose updated to reflect the new single-root scheme rather than listing the now-deprecated legacy prefixes as if they were canonical.

Not done in this sweep:
- :route standalone (the slice key) → :rf/route — left alone because of disambiguation cost (`:route` appears both as the registry-kind name in 001-Registration.md, where it stays bare, and as the app-db slice key in 011/012/etc., where it should rename). Conventions.md already shows `:rf/route` as the canonical slice key; the slice-rename across 011/012 prose is a small follow-up if needed.
- Fixture filenames not renamed — Conformance README convention is "filename mirrors :fixture/id with slash → hyphen"; some fixture-ids changed (e.g. :machine/transition → :rf.machine/transition would imply rf.machine-transition.edn). Filename rename deferred; existing files retain their names.

title: Review re-frame v1 README for ideas to add to re-frame2 human-readable docs
description:
Source: https://github.com/day8/re-frame/blob/master/README.md

The re-frame v1 README is the most-read entry point for re-frame and has been refined over years. It carries framing, examples, taglines, and explanatory metaphors that work well for human readers landing on the project for the first time. re-frame2 has shifted to an AI-first emphasis where the spec corpus carries the load-bearing content, but the human-facing docs (docs/guide/, top-level README, marketing-style framing) still benefit from the v1 README discipline.

Concrete output wanted: a survey pass over the v1 README that identifies which framings, metaphors, examples, and structural moves are worth lifting into re-frame2 human-readable surfaces — and where the v2 docs should diverge because the audience or thesis has changed.

Specific things to look for in the v1 README:
- Opening framing / tagline (the "what is re-frame in one sentence" line and the way it expands).
- The pattern explanation (six dominoes, etc.) — how much of that survives in v2 with frames + machines, and how the v2 framing lands for someone arriving fresh.
- Code examples used at the top — are the v2 equivalents (Counter, 7GUIs, Login) better or worse for the first-impression role?
- The README structure itself (sections, ordering, length) — what proportion of which content has the right shape.
- Acknowledgements, ecosystem links, badges — retain or rework.

Cross-reference targets in re-frame2:
- The top-level re-frame2 README (whatever it ultimately becomes).
- docs/guide/01-why-re-frame2.md, 03-events-state-cycle.md, 04-views-and-frames.md — the human-flavour pages that recently got their update pass.
- The taglines / one-line summaries in 000-Vision and Principles.md.

Distinguish carefully:
- Content that should be lifted as-is (or near-as-is) — proven framings.
- Content that should be reshaped — v1 idea, v2-appropriate execution.
- Content that should be dropped — superseded by v2 mechanics (e.g. v1 specifics that no longer apply).
- Content that needs new equivalents — v2-specific concepts (frames, machines, AI-first thesis) that v1 had no need to explain.

Why P2: the human-facing surface is consequential for adoption but the spec is the load-bearing artefact for the AI-first thesis. Worth doing as a focused pass once the spec corpus settles.
close_reason: Audited v1 README and rewrote re-frame2 README. Audit findings: v1 README does well at identity-setting (Pratchett axe quote, Outlasted JS churn pitch, Lisp anchor, React-is-the-V framing, brevity, visual identity); v2 README was structurally an engineering doc rather than a landing page. Rewrite preserves v2-specific content (three-layer AI-amenable surface, status section, directory tree, reading order) but reshapes for landing-page voice. New structure: logo placeholder → tagline → Pratchett axe quote → Why re-frame2 (4-paragraph thesis: events-are-causal, ~10y track record updated for v2, Lisp advantage, Reagent-is-the-V) → Whats new vs v1 (table) → AI-amenable surface → Documentation pointers → Project layout (demoted from lead) → Status → Reading order → Licence. Hand-off note: logo asset needs copying from v1 docs/images/logo/re-frame-colour.{png,svg}; license.txt to be added when v2 ships; ten-year reference will need updating to current year at release. Substantive thesis copy is set but Mike may want to iterate the voice.

title: Suggestion: decide whether all framework-owned re-frame keywords should live under :rf/*
description:
Open the question of whether every framework-owned keyword in re-frame2 should be namespaced under :rf/* (or another single reserved prefix), rather than mixing framework ids into multiple naming schemes. This includes events, effects, subs, trace categories, app-db keys, and any other runtime-owned ids.

The decision should weigh clarity, grepability, collision avoidance, migration cost from re-frame v1, compatibility with existing naming, and whether a single reserved namespace would make the spec and tooling easier to reason about. Concrete output wanted: one clear naming policy for framework-owned ids, including any deliberate exceptions.
close_reason:
Decision: consolidate to :rf/* as the single framework root namespace. Hierarchical sub-namespaces under it (:rf.error/*, :rf.warning/*, :rf.fx/*, :rf.frame/*, :rf.machine/*, :rf.registry/*, :rf.route/*, :rf.nav/*, :rf.ssr/*, :rf.server/*, :rf.epoch/*, :rf.assert/*, :rf.test/*, plus deeper hierarchy under :rf.machine.*). :re-frame/* survives only as a v1-compat alias for the small set that bridges (notably :re-frame/default → :rf/default); migration agent rewrites mechanically. User-defined route ids drop the :route/* convention and use feature prefix (:cart/show, :auth/login-page) like other user ids.

Implementation complete:
1. Conventions.md §Reserved namespaces — restructured the 14-row table into a single-root reserved set with hierarchical sub-namespace listing; explicit v1-compat alias section; user-defined-route-ids policy spelled out; discipline rules updated.
2. Conventions.md §Reserved app-db keys — :route renamed to :rf/route for full consistency with :rf/machines.
3. Conventions.md §Reserved sub-ids — [:route] → [:rf/route]; [:route/*] → [:rf.route/*]; [:rf/machine <id>] → [:rf.machine <id>].
4. MIGRATION.md M-20 — added as Type A (mechanical rename) with the full closed mapping table (~35 old→new pairs); covers :re-frame/*, :registry/*, :machine/*, :machine.X/*, :nav/*, :route/* framework events, [:route] sub renames, and the user-route-id Type-B judgment call.
5. Type-tag summary updated to include M-20 in Type A list.

Follow-up bead rf2-y683 filed for the actual ~280-occurrence corpus sweep — separated because policy lock and mechanical follow-through are different units of work; the closed mapping in M-20 is what the sweep applies.

title: Suggestion: revisit subscribe/dispatch argument shape — vector, map, or hybrid
description:
Open the question of what argument shape re-frame2 should prefer for dispatch and subscribe calls. The current re-frame style is positional/vector-based, e.g. [:event-1 arg] and [:sub-id arg]. An alternative is a map-based envelope such as dispatch {:rf/eid :event-1 :arg1 value}, or a hybrid where ids stay positional but richer inputs become named maps.

The decision should weigh clarity, AI amenability, inspectability, schema attachment, backwards compatibility with re-frame, ergonomics for simple cases, and whether positional vectors are too placeful once events/subs grow beyond trivial arities. Concrete output wanted: one preferred canonical form for dispatch/subscribe, plus the status of any alternates (supported, sugar, discouraged, or out of scope).
close_reason:
Decision: lock the hybrid call shape as canonical. [<id>] for trivial events/subs, [<id> <scalar>] for single-arg, [<id> <map>] for multi-arg. Multi-positional is tolerated indefinitely; linter nudges; existing v1 codebases run unchanged. Migration is opt-in per event-id, AI-driven, atomic (rewrites handler destructure + every call site for the id together). v1 unwrap interceptor pre-canonical (codebases using it are halfway migrated); ships unchanged in v2 as opt-in handler-side sugar. v1 trim-v dropped (its purpose was the multi-positional form v2 leaves behind).

Implementation:
1. New M-19 in MIGRATION.md — full migration rule with mechanics, failure modes, linter-nudge spec, v1 unwrap/trim-v handling, placeful-is-brittle rationale, Type-B opt-in classification.
2. New P9 Name over place principle in Principles.md — corpus-wide design value, applies to event payloads, effect maps, registration metadata, frame configs, machine snapshots, trace tags, hiccup attributes (single-value cases stay positional).
3. Cross-reference added to AI-Audit.md master principle table (P9; per-Spec scoring deferred until re-audit pass).
4. 002 §Routing — canonical-call-shape table added to the dispatch envelope section, pointing at Principles §Name over place and MIGRATION §M-19.
5. Construction-Prompts.md CP-1 (event handler) and CP-2 (subscription) — pre-flight note about call-shape convention; CP-1 templates updated to use {:keys [...]} destructure for multi-arg payloads.

Ranged across 5 files. Cross-references all linked.

title: Suggestion: revisit canonical view call syntax — [:counter args] vs [counter args]
description: Open the question of what the canonical view invocation form should be in re-frame2: explicit id/data style like [:counter args], or var/reference style like [counter args]. The decision should weigh clarity, inspectability, AI amenability, consistency with registered-view semantics, and fit with the current render-tree contract. Concrete output wanted: one preferred canonical form, plus the status of the other form (supported sugar, discouraged, or out of scope).
notes:
Resolved (Mike, 2026-05-08): Form 2 (Var-reference) is canonical.

Render-tree shape: [my-view args]. Native Reagent shape, no interception layer, compile-time safety, no HTML-tag collisions.

Form 1 ([:keyword args]) is NOT a render-tree form — keyword vectors at render time are HTML elements (Reagent's existing semantics). The keyword id is reserved for runtime lookup and introspection ((rf/view id), trace events, devtools, error frames, registry-only views via reg-view*).

Family asymmetry: render trees use Vars; runtime lookups use ids; reg-view bridges them (auto-defs Var + auto-derives id). Same pattern as the reg-view / reg-view* split (rf2-d0pi).

Spec-edit follow-up filed as a small bead (additions to 004-Views, Conventions, 001-Registration, Cross-Spec-Interactions). Land after rf2-d0pi merges to avoid double-touching files.

title: Suggestion: audit the conformance folder for correctness and fit
description: Check that docs/specification/conformance is correct as a corpus: fixture set, filenames, claimed scope, directory structure, references from the spec, and the relationship between the claimed contract surface and what the fixtures actually cover. Concrete output wanted: confirm whether the folder is in the right shape for the current spec, and identify any gaps, stale fixtures, misplaced material, or naming/organization issues.
close_reason: Audit complete. Findings: (1) 2 orphan fixtures (fx-db-first, fx-ordering-source-order) added to README §Fixtures table. (2) 23 fixtures missing :fixture/capabilities tag — all 23 tagged with appropriate capability sets (kept to existing in-use vocabulary except introducing :ssr/render-to-string for the render-to-string fixture). (3) dispatch-depth-limit.edn renamed to drain-depth-limit.edn (id :drain/depth-limit) to match spec terminology; cross-ref in always-depth-exceeded.edn updated; README table updated. Coverage gaps for the new contracts (hot-reload, sub-cache disposal, drain-loop levels, cross-spec interactions, reagent adapter) noted in bd memory rf2-conformance-audit-2026-05-07-43-fixtures for follow-up beads when CLJS implementation lands. P1 doc-hygiene pass complete; P2 coverage expansion deferred until reference implementation exists to verify against.

title: Suggestion: decide how the alpha namespace API fits into re-frame2, especially flows
description: There is still an unresolved surface-area question around APIs that currently live under an lpha namespace, especially the  lows API. The spec should decide whether these APIs become part of the stable re-frame2 surface, move into a companion/library layer, remain explicitly experimental, or are replaced by other locked mechanisms. Concrete output wanted: one clear placement and status story for lpha APIs, with  lows handled explicitly rather than implicitly.
close_reason: Duplicate of rf2-tbli, which carries the comprehensive analysis (per-item dispositions for flows, generalised registration, generalised subscribe, reg-global-interceptor; the four-disposition framework; files-to-update list; open questions for Mike). All work tracked under rf2-tbli going forward.

title: Drop reg-sub-raw — architecture covers every legitimate use case
description:
Locked decision: remove reg-sub-raw from re-frame2. The substrate now has explicit answers for every legitimate v1 use of reg-sub-raw; the remaining patterns are anti-patterns we want to remove anyway.

WHY reg-sub-raw existed in v1:
1. Reaction-tree rooted in something other than app-db (JS event stream, timer, external pub/sub).
2. Manual reaction lifecycle (explicit r/track!, r/dispose!).
3. Subscription that performs side effects (dirty anti-pattern).
4. Custom caching beyond the standard sub-cache.
5. Bridging non-Reagent reactive sources into re-frame.

WHY each is now covered by re-frame2 architecture:

1. Non-app-db sources → Pattern-AsyncEffect plus registered-fx. The external thing dispatches an event; the handler updates app-db; the sub reads app-db. The state-as-data discipline is preserved.

2. Manual reaction lifecycle → state machines. A machine state has entry / exit / data; reg-sub-raw lifecycle becomes machine state lifecycle.

3. Side effects from subs → use Pattern-AsyncEffect for the side effect, sub for reading the result. Dirty pattern; we WANT it gone.

4. Custom caching → 006 ReactiveSubstrate adapter handles caching at the substrate level; per-sub custom caching is rarely needed.

5. Bridging external reactive sources → 006 ReactiveSubstrate adapter contract handles this. A custom adapter brings the external source into the substrate; subs consume normally.

Plus: Goal 2 frame revertibility says all state lives in the frame value. reg-sub-raw subs that hold state outside the frame value violate the goal. Their state must move into app-db (per option 1 above) for revertibility.

WHAT DISAPPEARS:
- reg-sub-raw — removed
- The implicit reaction-lifecycle escape hatch

WHAT STAYS:
- reg-sub — unchanged; the canonical sub registration
- Standard sub-cache semantics
- Pattern-AsyncEffect for non-app-db reactive sources
- State machines for lifecycle-bearing reactive computations
- 006 adapter contract for bridging external reactivity

MIGRATION (new rule M-N):
- Type B (semantic flag) — agent identifies every reg-sub-raw call site and asks the user to choose the rewrite path. Static analysis can suggest the path based on what the raw body does:
  - If the body reads only app-db → mechanically convert to reg-sub.
  - If the body subscribes to a non-app-db source → user converts to a registered fx that dispatches events; the sub reads app-db.
  - If the body manages lifecycle → convert to a state machine.
  - If the body does side effects → flag as anti-pattern; the user moves the side effect to an event handler.

FILES TO UPDATE:
- API.md: remove reg-sub-raw row; document in Removed-not-shipped table.
- 001-Registration.md: drop :sub-raw from registry-kinds.
- 002-Frames.md: subscribe section confirms reg-sub is the only registration; cross-reference Pattern-AsyncEffect / state machines / adapter contract for what reg-sub-raw used to cover.
- 006-ReactiveSubstrate.md: adapter contract is the canonical bridging mechanism; reg-sub-raw was the v1 stopgap for what 006 now handles.
- MIGRATION.md: new rule with the four sub-cases above.
- 010-Schemas.md: any reference to :sub-raw schema dropped.
- Spec-Schemas.md: drop any :sub-raw entries.
- rf2-tbli alpha-disposition bead: not directly affected (reg-sub-raw is in re-frame.core not re-frame.alpha).

OPEN CALL:
What about v1 users with genuine non-stopgap uses of reg-sub-raw? The migration rule is Type B (flag for human review) which means the agent surfaces every site for the user to choose. If the survey reveals a category of legitimate use the architecture does not cover, file a follow-up bead before locking the removal.

PAIRS WITH:
- rf2-tbli (re-frame.alpha disposition) — same evaluation framework; this is the parallel decision for the main namespace.
- rf2-b87c (drop reg-global-interceptor) — also a v2 surface reduction.
- 006-ReactiveSubstrate adapter contract — replaces the reg-sub-raw stopgap.
- Pattern-AsyncEffect — handles non-app-db reactive sources.
- State machines (005) — handle lifecycle reactive computations.

WHY P2:
Real surface change; mechanical for most call sites; semantic flag for non-trivial ones. Not P1 because the migration is per-call-site review; not blocking other work.

Status: open. Ready to action — design locked. Run the v1-survey first to validate no genuine use case is uncovered.
close_reason: Removed reg-sub-raw; reg-sub is the only sub-registration form; added MIGRATION M-18 (Type B with four rewrite paths).

title: Drop reg-global-interceptor — frame-level interceptors is the canonical mechanism
description:
Locked decision: remove reg-global-interceptor and clear-global-interceptor from re-frame2. Frame-level :interceptors (declared in reg-frame metadata) is the canonical mechanism for "every event in this frame fires through this interceptor." There is no cross-frame interceptor concept in v2.

WHY:
1. Aligns with frame-as-isolated-actor. Goal 2 (frame-state revertibility) and strict-encapsulation push toward isolated runtime boundaries. Process-wide interceptors firing regardless of frame violate the isolation.
2. Single-frame apps get global semantics for free. Apps using only :re-frame/default declare reg-frame with :interceptors and get identical observable behaviour to v1.
3. Order-of-application becomes unambiguous. Two layers (frame-level wraps handler-level) instead of three.
4. Test frames stop being weird. Production audit-logger no longer fires on test-frame events.
5. Trace-listener API covers observer use cases. Audit logging, performance instrumentation, schema-validation-via-trace are observers, not behaviour-modifiers. register-trace-cb sees every dispatch across all frames without interceptor semantics.
6. The remaining case (cross-frame behaviour modifier) is rare and usually an architectural smell. The right fix is per-frame declaration or a trace listener.

WHAT DISAPPEARS:
- reg-global-interceptor — removed
- clear-global-interceptor — removed
- :interceptor registry kind — removed

WHAT STAYS:
- reg-frame :interceptors — the canonical global-within-this-frame mechanism
- handler-level :interceptors (per reg-event-fx) — unchanged
- register-trace-cb — the right tool for observer concerns

MIGRATION (new rule M-17):
- Type A single-frame: reg-global-interceptor X becomes added to reg-frame :re-frame/default :interceptors vector.
- Type B multi-frame: flag for human review. Right rewrite depends on intent — apply to each frame, convert to trace listener, or restrict to default frame.

FILES TO UPDATE:
- API.md: remove reg-global-interceptor / clear-global-interceptor rows; document in Removed-not-shipped table.
- 001-Registration.md: drop :interceptor from registry-kinds.
- 002-Frames.md: clarify frame-level :interceptors as canonical; note no cross-frame interceptor concept; for cross-frame observation use register-trace-cb.
- MIGRATION.md: new rule M-17 Type A or B as above; remove the preserved entry from What-stays-the-same.
- rf2-tbli pending alpha-disposition bead: mark this as the resolution for the global-interceptor item.

PAIRS WITH:
- rf2-tbli (re-frame.alpha disposition) — resolves one of the four alpha-item dispositions.
- 002-Frames § frame metadata — strengthens the role of frame-level :interceptors.
- 009-Instrumentation — register-trace-cb cited as canonical answer for cross-frame observer concerns.
- 000-Vision Goal 2 (frame revertibility) — drops a process-wide concept that violated isolation.

WHY P2: small change; design is locked; files-to-update list concrete. Not P1 because migration is mechanical for the common case.

Status: open. Ready to action.
close_reason: Removed reg-global-interceptor / clear-global-interceptor; frame-level :interceptors is canonical; added MIGRATION M-17 (Type A single-frame, Type B multi-frame).

title: Decide the disposition of re-frame.alpha namespace contents — including flows
description:
The `re-frame.alpha` namespace inherited from re-frame v1 contains several APIs whose status in re-frame2 is unresolved. Each carries the `, alpha` qualifier in API.md, signalling "may change" — but for v2 the framework needs to decide for each: promote to stable, keep alpha, drop, or reshape and ship. Flows are the most consequential of these.

## What is currently in re-frame.alpha (per API.md §"Alpha namespace")

1. **Generalised registration** — `reg :sub` / `reg :legacy-sub` / `reg :sub-lifecycle`. Allows registering subs through a single `reg` multi-arity instead of `reg-sub` / `reg-sub-raw`. Query-map shape on the registration side.

2. **Generalised subscribe** — `sub` (accepts query-map shape; `subscribe` is an alias). Map-shape is more extensible than query-vector but adds a second way to subscribe.

3. **Flows** — `reg-flow`, `flow<-`, `clear-flow`, `get-flow`, plus the `:flow` and `:live?` registered subs that read flow values. Flows are reactively-computed values with lifecycle hooks (init when first observed; cleanup when last observer leaves) — adjacent to subs but registered separately.

4. **`reg-global-interceptor`** — mentioned but separately tracked in the global-interceptor follow-up.

## The four dispositions per item

For each alpha item, the v2 decision is one of:

- **Promote to stable.** Drop the alpha qualifier; commit to the shape; document semantics fully.
- **Keep alpha.** Leave as-is with the "may change" signal; defer the commitment.
- **Drop.** Remove from v2 surface; users migrate to whatever subsumes it (or do without).
- **Reshape and ship.** The item is right in spirit but needs API tightening; reshape, lock, ship as stable.

## Per-item analysis

### Flows

The interesting case. v1 introduced flows because subs alone could not express:
- Reactive values with lifecycle (init, cleanup).
- Computed values that should be queryable independently of who first asked.
- Cross-cutting reactivity not anchored to a specific sub-key.

In v2, the substrate has expanded. Several mechanisms are adjacent:
- **State machines** (per [005-StateMachines](005-StateMachines.md)) provide lifecycle (states, entry/exit) for any reactively-observed value placed in `:data`.
- **`sub-machine`** ([005 §Subscribing to machines](005-StateMachines.md#subscribing-to-machines-via-sub-machine)) reads a machine snapshot reactively.
- **`:invoke`** declares child machines tied to parent state lifecycle.
- **Pattern-RemoteData** ([Pattern-RemoteData.md](Pattern-RemoteData.md)) provides the standard slice for async-data lifecycles.

Question: do machines + sub-machine + Pattern-RemoteData **subsume** flows? Or do flows fill a niche that those do not — non-machine reactive computations with lifecycle?

Plausible answers:

(a) **Flows are subsumed; drop.** Users writing flows in v1 migrate to a state machine (where lifecycle and reactivity compose cleanly). Pro: smaller surface, fewer concepts to learn, better composition. Con: existing flow users have to rewrite.

(b) **Flows are useful; promote.** Some reactive computations do not warrant a full state machine — flows are a lighter primitive. Promote to stable; document semantics; integrate with machines (machines can publish flows; flows can be inputs to subs).

(c) **Flows are useful but need reshaping; reshape and ship.** Flow API has rough edges from its alpha exploration; tighten the shape (especially around lifecycle, multi-frame interaction, and SSR semantics).

The spec needs to pick one. (a) is the simplest; (b)/(c) more conservative; (a)+(b)/(c) compromise possible if flows are reframed as a thin convenience layer over machines.

### Generalised registration (`reg :sub` / `reg :legacy-sub` / `reg :sub-lifecycle`)

These exist in v1 to unify the family `reg-sub` / `reg-sub-raw` / lifecycle variants under one entry point with kind discrimination. v2 has more registration kinds (`:event`, `:sub`, `:fx`, `:cofx`, `:machine`, `:route`, `:head`, `:error-projector`, etc.) — generalised `reg` could span all of them OR be retired in favour of per-kind macros.

Plausible answers:

(a) **Drop in favour of per-kind macros.** Per-kind macros (`reg-event-fx`, `reg-sub`, etc.) are clearer at the call site; generalised `reg` adds a layer.

(b) **Promote and extend.** Generalised `reg` becomes the canonical way to register all kinds; per-kind macros remain as sugar.

(c) **Keep alpha, reshape later.** Defer.

### Generalised subscribe (`sub` with query-map)

Query-map shape is more extensible than query-vector (`(sub {:re-frame/q :user/profile :user-id 42})` vs `(subscribe [:user/profile 42])`). Useful when subs grow past 2–3 args; query-vector positional ordering becomes a maintenance burden.

Plausible answers:

(a) **Promote.** Ship as the canonical form; deprecate query-vector for new code.

(b) **Promote both forms.** Query-vector and query-map both stable; users pick. Risk: two ways to do it.

(c) **Drop.** Query-vector is sufficient; users with complex args use a single map argument: `(subscribe [:user/profile {:user-id 42 :include-deleted? true}])`.

### `reg-global-interceptor`

Separate follow-up (see the global-interceptor disposition discussion). Likely outcome: promote out of alpha.

## Files to update when actioning

- API.md §"Alpha namespace" — restructure to reflect each items disposition.
- 002-Frames.md — if globals promoted, document semantics.
- A new spec section (or a Pattern doc) for flows if (b)/(c) above; or remove flow references if (a).
- MIGRATION.md — for any drop or reshape, document the migration path.

## Why this matters

The alpha namespace is "deferred decisions." Every deferred decision is potential confusion for an AI scaffolding from the spec ("should I use sub or subscribe? reg or reg-sub? flows or machines?"). A v2 release with alpha-still-alpha sends a "may change" signal that suppresses adoption. The four-way evaluation per item resolves this.

## Pairs with

- The global-interceptor disposition (separate but related — same evaluation framework).
- rf2-5pt (AI-implementable from the spec alone) — closes a class of "did you mean this or that" ambiguity.
- 002-Frames — flow semantics in a multi-frame world need explicit treatment if flows are kept.
- Pattern-RemoteData — adjacent to flows; resolution affects which is canonical.

## Open question for Mike

Per-item dispositions:

1. **Flows:** drop (subsumed by machines)? Promote? Reshape?
2. **Generalised registration:** drop (per-kind wins)? Promote? Defer?
3. **Generalised subscribe:** promote query-map? Both forms? Drop?
4. **`reg-global-interceptor`:** promote (per the global-interceptor follow-up).

Status: open. Decisions needed before v2 lock; cannot ship "preserved, alpha" indefinitely if v2 is supposed to be a settled contract.
close_reason:
Flows decision locked. The decisions:

1. Flows ship in v2 as a focused convenience capability — the v2 incarnation of v1 on-changes, registered in the runtime and runtime-toggleable via fx.
2. Mike framing: convenience for a small number of relatively small use-cases. Not to be overused. Not a sub replacement.
3. v1 alpha overgrowth (lifecycle state machine, topological sort by reified flow<-, per-flow fx, four lifecycle policies) is dropped. v2 keeps just: pure compute fn, input paths, output path, dirty-check, topological sort over static path-overlap.
4. Two reserved fx-ids: :rf.fx/reg-flow, :rf.fx/clear-flow.

Plus consequential std-interceptor decisions (per Mike note that v2 should still have a before/after pair):
- KEEP: inject-cofx, after, path, unwrap (Option C from the rename discussion — keep asymmetric naming, document the de facto pair).
- DROP: debug (subsumed by trace surface + 10x), trim-v (subsumed by M-19 map-payload form), on-changes (subsumed by flows), enrich (subsumed by flows + schemas).

Implementation:
1. New Spec 013-Flows.md (~250 lines): abstract, when-and-when-not-to-use, registration shape, drain integration, topological sort + cycle detection, dirty-check, dynamic toggle via fx, comparison to subs/machines/on-changes, migration from v1 alpha, conformance fixture plan, open questions, resolved decisions, cross-references. Restraint framing front-and-center per Mike note that flows are convenience for small use-cases.
2. 001-Registration.md — added :flow row to the canonical kind table.
3. Conventions.md — added :rf.fx/reg-flow and :rf.fx/clear-flow to reserved fx-ids.
4. README.md companion-docs / spec listing — added Spec 013.
5. API.md §Standard interceptors — restructured to show v2 surface (4 interceptors with phase + purpose) plus removed-in-v2 table.
6. MIGRATION.md M-21 — added covering all four std-interceptor drops with type tagging (debug + trim-v Type A; on-changes + enrich Type B because the rewrite path depends on body intent).
7. MIGRATION.md type-tag summary updated to include M-21 in both Type A and Type B lists.
8. MIGRATION.md §Standard interceptors line — updated to reflect dropped set.
9. 009-Instrumentation.md §Trace emit sites — updated to remove dropped interceptors.

Not yet done — follow-up rf2-7cb2 filed for the remaining alpha leftovers (generalised reg, generalised sub with query-maps + lifecycle policies). Those need separate decisions.

Cross-spec drift to clean up later (a corpus sweep is rf2-y683 territory): some doc-internal references still mention enrich, on-changes, etc. as preserved interceptors — surface area is small.

title: Document parameter-passing across pattern docs (chunk-size, URL, config, etc.)
description:
The pattern docs (Pattern-LongRunningWork, Pattern-WebSocket, Pattern-Boot) show machines with parameters in :data (chunk-size, URL, auth-token, etc.) but do not clearly demonstrate how those parameters are passed in. Readers will reach the worked examples and wonder "how do I parameterise this?" The mechanism exists; the docs do not surface it.

This is a documentation gap, not an architectural shortcoming. The fix is to demonstrate the three canonical mechanisms across the affected pattern docs.

## The three parameter-passing mechanisms

**1. Via the :start event payload (most flexible).**

The dispatched event carries opts; the :start action reads them. Works for any machine — singleton or spawned.

  ;; user-side
  (rf/dispatch [:compute/batch-job [:start input {:chunk-size 50}]])

  ;; action
  :start-job
  (fn [_ [_ input opts]]
    {:data {:total      (count input)
            :input      input
            :chunk-size (:chunk-size opts 100)
            ...}})

**2. Via the spawn-spec :data fn (when spawned from a parent).**

For dynamic actors, the spawn-spec :data slot accepts a fn closing over the parents snapshot. The parent already has the URL/token; the spawn-spec :data fn derives the childs initial data.

  :invoke {:machine-id :ws/socket
           :data       (fn [{:keys [data]} _]
                         {:url        (-> data :config :ws-url)
                          :auth-token (-> data :session :token)})}

**3. Via host config / frame metadata (boot-time-fixed values).**

The boot machine reads config from app-db; threads values into machine starts via event payload.

  :on {:hydrate {:target :ready
                 :action (fn [{:keys [data]} _]
                           {:fx [[:dispatch [:ws/socket [:connect
                                                         {:url (-> data :config :ws-url)}]]]]})}}

## Pattern-doc updates

**Pattern-LongRunningWork.md:** the worked example has :chunk-size 100 hardcoded as a default. Update to:
- Declare 100 in :data as the default.
- Have :start-job accept an opts map: (fn [_ [_ input opts]] ...).
- Show the dispatch as (rf/dispatch [:compute/batch-job [:start input {:chunk-size 50}]]).
- Add a brief §Parameters subsection at the end explaining when each of the three mechanisms applies.

**Pattern-WebSocket.md:** the worked example has :url declared in :data. Update to:
- Show the :connect action accepting opts: (fn [_ [_ {:keys [url auth-token]}]] ...).
- Show the dispatch with URL: (rf/dispatch [:ws/socket [:connect {:url ... :auth-token ...}]]).
- Add a §Parameters subsection.

**Pattern-Boot.md:** the worked example reads config implicitly. Update to:
- Show the :configuring state explicitly fetching config (Pattern-AsyncEffect instance).
- Show subsequent states reading config from :data and threading values into :invoke spawn-specs or dispatched events.
- Add a §Parameters subsection.

**Pattern-AsyncEffect.md (foundational):** add a §"Parameter passing across the boundary" subsection to Pattern-AsyncEffect explaining the three mechanisms generically; the specific pattern docs (RemoteData, WebSocket, Boot, LongRunningWork, Forms) cross-reference it.

## Anti-patterns to call out

- Hardcoding parameters in the machines :data without a way to override them at start-time (the current example error).
- Reading host globals from inside actions (closes over global state; non-testable; non-replayable).
- Threading parameters through a :spec metadata field at registration (registration-time vs run-time conflation; spec :data is initial values, not overrides).

## Files to update when actioning

- Pattern-AsyncEffect.md — new §Parameter passing across the boundary; canonical home of the three mechanisms.
- Pattern-LongRunningWork.md — update worked example; brief §Parameters cross-referencing AsyncEffect.
- Pattern-WebSocket.md — update worked example; brief §Parameters cross-referencing AsyncEffect.
- Pattern-Boot.md — update worked example; brief §Parameters cross-referencing AsyncEffect.
- Pattern-RemoteData.md — likely no change (RemoteData examples already pass URL/payload via events) but verify.
- Pattern-Forms.md — likely no change (form values are already in slice :draft) but verify.

## Why P2

Documentation clarity for the foundation pattern docs. Readers will hit this gap quickly because the worked examples are the first thing they read after the prose. Fixing it improves AI-scaffolding accuracy too — an AI scaffolding a new machine from these patterns reads the example as the canonical shape; if the example does not show parameter passing, the AI may produce hardcoded-defaults code.

## Pairs with

- rf2-boxi (Pattern-AsyncEffect — gets the new §Parameters subsection as the canonical home).
- rf2-un3y (Pattern-Boot).
- rf2-35gf (Pattern-WebSocket).
- rf2-07eb (Pattern-LongRunningWork — the bead that surfaced this gap).
- rf2-22zt (Pattern-RemoteData) and rf2-kw00 (Pattern-Forms) — verify, likely no change.

Status: open. Action across the three (or four) pattern docs in one focused pass.
close_reason: Parameter-passing documented in Pattern-AsyncEffect §Parameter passing across the boundary as the canonical home; brief §Parameters subsections added in Pattern-LongRunningWork, Pattern-WebSocket, Pattern-Boot with cross-references.

title: Suggestion: update the RealWorld auth example to match the machine-scoped resolution lock
description: examples/realworld/auth.cljs still says machine-scoped guard/action lookup falls back to the global registry. The current StateMachines spec says guard/action ids resolve machine-locally only. Because this file is a worked auth-machine example, that one sentence will teach the wrong rule. Update the prose to match the locked semantics, or make any intended escape hatch explicit elsewhere. See examples/realworld/auth.cljs:14-16.
close_reason: RealWorld auth.cljs: docstring corrected — guard/action keywords resolve machine-locally only; no global registry fallback.

title: Suggestion: correct the Circle Drawer dialog/undo sketch so it demonstrates the claimed pattern
description: examples/7guis/06_circle_drawer.cljs currently claims the adjust-radius dialog commits as one undoable step, but the code mutates circle radius continuously during :drawer/dialog-drag and :drawer/close-dialog only clears the dialog. That means the example does not actually preserve the pre-dialog snapshot it says it is demonstrating. The slider is also rendered with a constant :value 30 instead of the selected circle's current radius. Consider either storing draft radius separately until commit, or simplifying the prose so the sketch no longer teaches the wrong undo pattern. See examples/7guis/06_circle_drawer.cljs:78-120 and 156-173.
close_reason: Circle Drawer: dialog now stores :draft-radius separate from circle; commits on close; slider :value bound to draft. Edit dialog is now genuinely a single undoable step.

title: Suggestion: make the RealWorld routing example explicit about runtime-owned vs example-owned behavior
description: examples/realworld/routing.cljs currently mixes two stories: it says the Spec-012-locked default handler owns core behavior, but then it hand-registers :route/navigate, :route/handle-url-change, :route/continue, :route/cancel, and route-link itself. For a worked example that is fine, but the file should clearly distinguish 'framework contract being illustrated' from 'example-local shim/wiring' so readers do not infer the wrong ownership boundaries. See examples/realworld/routing.cljs:95-101, 130-165, 239-253.
close_reason: RealWorld routing.cljs: added explicit ownership-boundary docstring; FRAMEWORK-SHIPPED markers on :route/navigate, :route/handle-url-change, :route/continue, :route/cancel, :rf/route-link.

title: Suggestion: scope the RealWorld example claims to the implemented subset
description: The RealWorld README and top-level docstrings currently overstate completeness for a sketch. They describe routing/auth/core pieces as 'implemented' or 'full impl', say every locked routing feature is exercised, and describe SSR as part of the end-to-end exercise, while the app shell still has multiple route views commented out and several route hooks point at TODO namespaces. Tightening the claims to 'implemented subset' would make the example easier to trust. See examples/realworld/README.md:20,36,52,67; examples/realworld/core.cljs:11-22,30-35,134-141; examples/realworld/routing.cljs:49-74.
close_reason: RealWorld README + core.cljs: claims scoped to implemented subset (auth + articles + routing scaffold); routing.cljs row clarified; SSR called out as TODO with pointer to examples/ssr.

title: Suggestion: bring the SSR worked example up to the locked hydrate and mismatch contract
description: examples/ssr/core.cljc still bakes in older SSR semantics in a few load-bearing places. Its default :rf/hydrate handler merges server state into client state, while Spec 011 now says the default contract is replace-on-hydrate with merge only via an explicit override. The example also computes a loose hash in the payload rather than demonstrating the current structural render-hash marker contract. Because this file is the SSR teaching example, it should either track the current lock or clearly label these as simplified sketch choices. See examples/ssr/core.cljc:76-81, 132-137, 156-163.
close_reason: SSR example: :rf/hydrate now uses locked :replace-app-db (not merge); render-hash described as the data-rf-render-hash structural marker per Spec 011.

title: Suggestion: make the minimal routing example match the locked routing surface more closely
description: examples/routing/core.cljs still teaches a narrower, older routing shape: :route/link instead of the canonical :rf/route-link, direct dispatch to :route/navigate instead of :rf/url-requested, and a narrower :route/navigate contract than Spec 012 now describes. As a sketch it does not need every routing feature, but it should either present itself as a deliberately minimal subset or use the current standard runtime events so readers do not learn the wrong surface first. See examples/routing/core.cljs:77-80 and 153-161.
close_reason: Minimal routing example: route-link registered as :rf/route-link; clicks dispatch :rf/url-requested; :route/navigate signature opens to opts map; modifier-key passthrough in click handler; docstring scopes the example as a deliberately minimal subset.

title: Suggestion: align JVM-runnable claims in examples with actual host/file boundaries
description: Several examples and READMEs say their headless tests are JVM-runnable even though the code lives in .cljs namespaces and requires CLJS-only libraries. That is likely to mislead readers about what can actually run on Clojure/JVM. Consider either moving the testable/example-core parts to .cljc or softening the claims to 'headless' / 'browserless' unless the namespace is genuinely host-loadable. Examples: examples/login/core.cljs:14,256; examples/nine-states/core.cljs:40,571; examples/realworld/README.md:52; examples/realworld/ssr.cljs:27.
close_reason: Softened JVM-runnable claims to 'browserless' in login, nine-states, realworld README, realworld/ssr.cljs (all .cljs); flagged that JVM execution requires .cljc port.

title: Pattern-LongRunningWork — modernise the v1 "CPU hog" guidance
description:
The re-frame v1 doc "Solve the CPU hog problem" (https://day8.github.io/re-frame/Solve-the-CPU-hog-problem/) describes how to handle long-running CPU work without freezing the UI: chunk the work, re-dispatch between chunks, yield control to the browser. The doc is old; the toolkit re-frame2 ships is richer. Modernise as Pattern-LongRunningWork.

## What changed since v1

v1 toolkit was: chunked re-dispatch + app-db flags for cancel + `^:flush-dom` event metadata for one-shot DOM-flush. Re-frame2 ships:

- WebWorkers via Pattern-AsyncEffect (rf2-boxi) — for one-shot offload — and Pattern-WebSocket (rf2-35gf) for long-lived work.
- State machines with hierarchical states, `:always` (eventless transitions for batch progression), `:after` (delayed transitions for yielding), machine-scoped guards, `:invoke` for actor-spawn.
- Pattern-StaleDetection (rf2-035a) — epoch idiom for cancellation; supersedes the v1 `:abandonment-required` flag pattern.
- Frame-state revertibility (Goal 2) — undo of long computations is automatic if the chunk-processing logic preserves `app-db` value-equality.

The modernised pattern uses these.

## What goes in Pattern-LongRunningWork.md

### When to reach for this pattern

- A handler must compute something expensive on the main thread.
- The work cannot be fully offloaded (uses DOM-only APIs; needs incremental progress reporting; needs to read `app-db` slices that are awkward to serialise).

### Decision: offload or chunk?

Decision tree at the top of the doc:

1. Does the work involve I/O? → Use Pattern-AsyncEffect; no chunking needed (the host browser already yields during I/O).
2. Is it truly CPU-bound and can it be serialised in / out? → Use a Web Worker via Pattern-AsyncEffect (one-shot) or Pattern-WebSocket (long-lived). This is the preferred modern answer.
3. Must it run on the main thread? → Use the chunked state-machine pattern below.

### The canonical chunked-state-machine pattern (replaces v1 self-redispatch)

A `(reg-event-fx :compute/batch-job (create-machine-handler {...}))` with states:

- `:idle` — not running.
- `:processing` — actively computing one chunk; `:always` transition to `:checking-done` when chunk completes.
- `:checking-done` — `:always [{:guard :done? :target :complete} {:target :yielding}]`.
- `:yielding` — `:after 0 :processing` (yields to the browser; resumes on next tick).
- `:complete` — terminal.
- `:cancelled` — terminal; entered when user cancels.

The state-machine version:
- Replaces the manual `:dispatch [:count-to false ...]` re-entry with a state machine that explicitly models the chunked progression.
- Yields between chunks via `:after 0` (browser gets a tick).
- Stops on `:done?` guard (reaches terminal `:complete`).
- Handles cancel via a separate `:cancel` event that transitions to `:cancelled` regardless of current chunk.

### Cancellation (modernised)

v1 used an `:abandonment-required` app-db flag. Re-frame2 uses Pattern-StaleDetection: cancellation advances the machines epoch (entering `:cancelled` state); the in-flight chunk dispatch carries the pre-cancellation epoch; on receipt, the machine handler checks the epoch and aborts if mismatched (already in `:cancelled`).

In practice: `:cancel` event → transition to `:cancelled` (entry action emits `:rf.machine.timer/stale-after` if any timer is in flight); the next batchs `:always` check finds the machine in `:cancelled` and the transition fails (or skips entirely).

### Progress UI

The machines `:data` carries `{:processed 0 :total 1000 :elapsed-ms 0}`. A registered sub `(rf/sub-machine :compute/batch-job)` returns the snapshot; views render `(:processed data) / (:total data)` as a progress bar. No new mechanism.

### One-shot heavy work (replaces `^:flush-dom`)

If the work is a single block (not chunkable) and you want a "Working..." modal to appear before the block, the modern pattern is:

1. Dispatch event A: sets `:processing? true` in `app-db`.
2. Event A returns `{:fx [[:dispatch-later {:ms 0 :dispatch [:event-B]}]]}` — yields one tick.
3. Event B does the long work synchronously.

`:dispatch-later {:ms 0}` schedules via the host clock primitive (via `re-frame.interop`); the browser gets one render cycle between A and B.

The v1 `^:flush-dom` event metadata is removed; the modern equivalent is `:dispatch-later` with `{:ms 0}`. Document this in the migration notes if not already covered.

### Anti-patterns

- Computing in subscriptions (subs should be cheap; long compute belongs in events).
- Mutating `app-db` mid-handler with multiple `assoc`s expecting interleaved renders (re-frame batches per drain; only one render per drain).
- Manually managing chunk state in app-db when a state machine fits more cleanly.
- Forgetting cancellation entirely (Pattern-StaleDetection composes cleanly).
- Using `:always` cycles without bounded depth (the `:rf.error/machine-always-depth-exceeded` rule applies; batch-yielding via `:after 0` resets the depth).

### Composition

- Pattern-AsyncEffect — preferred for offloadable work.
- Pattern-WebSocket — preferred for long-lived computation in a worker.
- Pattern-StaleDetection — handles cancellation cleanly.
- State machines (005) — the canonical chunked pattern.
- `:after` (rf2-f6g5 closed) — yielding via `:after 0`.
- `:always` (rf2-9ln closed) — batch progression via eventless transitions.

## Files to update when actioning

- New: `docs/specification/Pattern-LongRunningWork.md` (Type: Pattern).
- README.md — companion-docs index.
- 005-StateMachines.md — cross-reference Pattern-LongRunningWork as a worked example of `:always` + `:after 0` for batch processing.
- Pattern-AsyncEffect.md — note "for non-offloadable CPU-bound work, see Pattern-LongRunningWork."
- MIGRATION.md — add note under "What stays the same" or as a migration rule that `^:flush-dom` event metadata is replaced by `:dispatch-later {:ms 0}`.

## Why P2

Common production problem; well-known v1 pattern; modernised version uses the substrate cleanly. Naming the pattern lets users find the modern answer instead of the 8-year-old article. Not P1 because apps work today via the v1 pattern; the doc is a discoverability win, not a correctness fix.

## Pairs with

- rf2-boxi (Pattern-AsyncEffect) — the offload alternative.
- rf2-35gf (Pattern-WebSocket) — long-lived offload.
- rf2-035a (Pattern-StaleDetection) — cancellation.
- rf2-f6g5 closed (`:after`) — yielding.
- rf2-9ln closed (`:always`) — batch progression.

Status: open. Action immediately per Mikes instruction.
close_reason: Pattern-LongRunningWork.md created. Modernises the v1 'CPU hog' guidance with: decision tree (offload to worker via Pattern-AsyncEffect vs chunk on main thread); canonical chunked-state-machine pattern using :always for batch progression and :after 0 for browser yielding; cancellation via state transition (composes with Pattern-StaleDetection); progress UI from machine :data via sub-machine; one-shot heavy-work pattern replacing v1's ^:flush-dom metadata with :dispatch-later {:ms 0}; anti-patterns; explicit migration table from v1. Cross-referenced from README, Pattern-AsyncEffect, 005, MIGRATION (added M-16 for ^:flush-dom removal).

title: Suggestion: separate reading paths into canonical, background, and implementor tracks
description: Cross-corpus structure review: the current README reading-order section still mixes external background rationale with the claimed normative spine. Consider splitting reading guidance into three explicit tracks: canonical contract reading, optional background rationale, and implementor-porting path. Real benefit: new readers know what is required versus helpful, and implementors can follow a minimal path without reconstructing corpus boundaries themselves.
close_reason: README's reading order rewritten as three explicit, non-overlapping tracks: Track 1 Canonical (the contract in normative reading order), Track 2 Background (rationale + principles + design history pointers), Track 3 Implementor (host-profile matrix → Implementor-Checklist → Spec-Schemas → error contract → SSR → Construction-Prompts → conformance corpus). Each track is sequenced explicitly; Track 3 wires up the porting path the corpus already supplies.

title: Suggestion: standardize a numbered-Spec template with fixed sections and trailing appendices
description: Cross-corpus structure review: the numbered specs are much closer now, but they still vary in where they place normative surface, CLJS-reference notes, rationale, open questions, and resolved decisions. Consider enforcing one template across 000-012: Abstract, Normative surface inventory, Core contract, CLJS reference notes, Cross-references, then appendices for Notes/Open questions/Resolved decisions. Real benefit: higher scanability, better AI parsing, and fewer hidden second sources of truth.
close_reason: Audited 000-012 against Header/Abstract/Normative content/Open questions/Resolved decisions/Cross-references template. Most specs already conformed (006 fully; 000/002/004/005/007/008/009 conformed bar a final Cross-references appendix, deliberately preserved as-is). Fixes applied to drifted specs: 011 Resolved decisions and Open questions ordering swapped; 001 Cross-references moved after Open questions; 010 'Non-Malli library support (RESOLVED)' lifted out of Open questions into a new Resolved decisions section.

title: Suggestion: add a corpus-wide ownership matrix for every contract surface
description: Cross-corpus structure review: several remaining issues are really ownership confusion, not missing content. Consider a single matrix near the README front that maps each surface area (frame model, registration metadata, route grammar, trace vocabulary, schema shapes, migration rules, pair-tool runtime, etc.) to one canonical owner doc and any non-owning projections. Real benefit: faster navigation, fewer duplicated definitions, and less drift between 001/002/004/API/Spec-Schemas/Tool-Pair.
close_reason: Added an 'Ownership matrix' section to README listing every contract surface against its owning Spec, with companion citations. Covers identity primitive, registration grammar, registry kinds, frames, dispatch envelope, effect-map shape, fx/cofx, drain, overrides, machines (foundation hooks vs full grammar split between 002 and 005), views, subs (002 + 006 split), state machine grammar, reactive substrate, stories, testing, trace events, error contract, schemas, SSR + :platforms + emitter, routing, runtime shapes, API signatures, migration rules, pair-tool, conventions, construction prompts, conformance corpus. README now states the drift rule: a second normative definition is a corpus bug.

title: Suggestion: reorganize docs/specification into three explicit layers
description: Cross-corpus structure review: the current two-bucket split still leaves Reference doing two jobs. Consider a three-layer structure: (1) normative contract docs, (2) derived reference and migration artefacts, and (3) companion patterns/tooling/audit docs. Real benefit: clearer reader expectations, clearer bucket semantics, and less ownership drift between numbered Specs, API/MIGRATION/Spec-Schemas, and the pattern/tooling docs.
close_reason: Restructured docs/specification/README.md to surface a three-layer organisation (Foundation: 000/001/002; Capability: 004-012; Companion: everything else). The two-bucket normative-vs-companion axis is preserved; the layer axis runs alongside it. README now has explicit per-layer tables and introductory prose explaining the layer concept.

title: Suggestion: split CP-5 machine appendix out of Construction-Prompts and stop restating canonical route grammar
description: Latest per-document pass: Construction-Prompts still turns CP-5 into a second machine spec, repeats shared pre-flight guidance despite promising deltas only, and restates canonical route grammar inline in CP-7 after pointing to the owning spec. Consider tightening it back into a build-facing prompt catalogue.
close_reason: Trimmed Construction-Prompts: extracted CP-5 deep-dive into CP-5-MachineGuide.md (inline-fn escape hatch, v1 grammar subset, parallel/history substitutes), converted CP-1/CP-2/CP-3/CP-4 pre-flight to delta-only, removed restated route grammar in CP-7.

title: Suggestion: make AI-Audit scope, legend, and gap references self-contained
description: Latest per-document pass: AI-Audit still crosses its own declared corpus boundary, uses n/a without defining it in the legend, duplicates some gap tracking, and forward-references G-* shorthands before defining them. Consider making the audit artifact fully self-contained and stable.
close_reason: Made AI-Audit self-contained: added §Scope (which docs are graded), defined n/a in legend, added forward-reference pointer for G-A through G-F, deduplicated per-spec gaps by linking to canonical G-letter.

title: Suggestion: make Pattern-StaleDetection self-contained and explicit about naming ownership
description: Latest per-document pass: Pattern-StaleDetection still does not settle whether it owns stale trace-event naming or delegates it, and its formal contract relies on undefined terms and CLJS-specific jargon. Consider making the contract self-contained and the naming authority explicit.
close_reason: Pattern-StaleDetection: added glossary, ownership section (initiating container owns epoch; destruction host-dependent), and trace-event naming ownership (pattern owns shape, features own strings).

title: Suggestion: reconcile Pattern-RemoteData attempt semantics, reset optionality, error rendering, and cancellation status
description: Latest per-document pass: Pattern-RemoteData still conflicts on how attempt works, whether reset is optional or required for conformance, whether error states may retain and render stale data, and whether cancellation is part of the contract or just a sketch. Consider tightening those points into one internally consistent convention.

title: Suggestion: lock canonical Pattern-Forms rules for failed-submit visibility, form-level errors, submit-error, and async validation
description: Latest per-document pass: Pattern-Forms still leaves several core form behaviors partially example-only or internally inconsistent, especially failed-submit error visibility, when form errors display, whether submit-error is standard, and how async validation fits the error model. Consider promoting those rules into the explicit canonical sections.

title: Suggestion: complete Tool-Pair consolidated contract and name restore failures explicitly
description: Latest per-document pass: Tool-Pair still says its top table is the consolidated contract while later sections add more surface, and its epoch-restore failure path is not fully named in canonical event vocabulary. Consider making the top-level contract truly complete and tightening the failure surface.
close_reason: Completed Tool-Pair contract: clarified consolidated-contract = existing-surface + time-travel + §How-AI-tools-attach is a view (not new commitments), expanded restore-failure modes to six named categories with tags (unknown frame/epoch, schema-mismatch, missing-handler, version-mismatch, during-drain).

title: Suggestion: rescope Spec-Schemas into runtime, public, and conformance contracts and keep v1 and post-v1 separate
description: Latest per-document pass: Spec-Schemas still mixes runtime-internal schemas, public input contracts, and conformance-only artefacts in one catalogue, and it still places post-v1 hydration fields inside a supposedly exact v1 payload shape. Consider separating those categories more explicitly.
close_reason: Rescoped Spec-Schemas: added Schema layers section (Runtime / Public / Conformance), per-schema Layer header on each entry, split hydration-payload into v1 + post-v1 schemas to keep v1 contract auditable.

title: Suggestion: regularize API.md tables for spec refs, configure keys, status taxonomy, and alpha coverage
description: Latest per-document pass: API.md still mixes unlike values in the Spec column, scatters configure-key documentation, uses status markers beyond its declared vocabulary, and treats re-frame.alpha in a different format from the rest of the reference. Consider normalizing those table conventions so API.md is a regular lookup surface again.

title: Suggestion: regularize MIGRATION rule identifiers and split M-11 into atomic rules

Consider normalizing the rule taxonomy and splitting the omnibus rule.

Consider finishing the index and taxonomy cleanup so the entrypoint is self-consistent.

title: Suggestion: specify the 010 schema digest algorithm precisely
description: Latest per-document pass: 010 still uses a loose canonicalisation description for schema digests even though the digest is meant to be cross-runtime reproducible. Consider defining exact serialisation, ordering, and hash representation so different hosts can implement it deterministically.
