Inputs, procedure (7 steps), output shape, rationale (why SHA-256 over FNV-1a), test-vector pointer, and non-schema-host fallback all locked.

title: Suggestion: close 010 contradictions in schema failure semantics, boundary validation, and non-Malli extension points
description: Latest per-document pass: 010 still conflicts on whether every schema failure aborts dispatch, leaves validate-at-boundary ambiguous against event :spec, and only gestures at a non-Malli validator hook. Consider locking those extension and failure rules explicitly.
close_reason: 010-Schemas: replaced 'Failure aborts the dispatch' summary with explicit per-step recovery semantics in the When-checked table (event/cofx skip handler, fx-args skip just the offending fx, app-db rolls back in dev); clarified validate-at-boundary reuses handler's :spec rather than introducing a parallel schema; added §Non-Malli validators — the validator-fn extension point locking set-schema-validator! as the substitution mechanism.

title: Suggestion: make 009 register-trace-cb, error-event shape, and production-elision semantics canonical
description: Latest per-document pass: 009 still splits the listener API signature across sections, contradicts itself on whether :frame is top-level or only inside :tags on error events, and describes JVM/production trace elision in incompatible ways. Consider tightening those three contracts in one place.
close_reason: 009: unified register-trace-cb signature with optional opts arg; moved :frame to top-level on error events (removed :tags duplicate); locked JVM elision via System property (CLJS-only compile-time elision)

title: Suggestion: clarify 008 helper surface and with-frame call shapes
description: Latest per-document pass: 008 still contradicts itself about whether it ships helper surface at all, and it still presents multiple with-frame syntaxes without a single canonical call shape. Consider making the actual helper inventory and call shapes explicit near the top of the spec.
close_reason: 008: canonicalised with-frame to single binding-vector form (id-only is sugar); locked re-frame.test helper inventory in one table (re-exports + dispatch-sequence + assert-state)

title: Suggestion: canonicalize 007 story ids, assertion namespace, variant shape, and loader lifecycle
description: Latest per-document pass: 007 still has conflicting story/variant id syntax, inconsistent assert event namespaces, an incomplete canonical variant contract around :extends, and contradictory loader semantics. Consider locking those four surfaces in one canonical contract block.
close_reason: 007: locked story/variant id grammar; canonicalised :rf.assert/* namespace; tightened :extends in variant table; documented four-phase loader lifecycle (loaders → events → render → play)

title: Suggestion: resolve 004 render-tree serialisability and carrier-shape ambiguity
description: Latest per-document pass: 004 still mixes a CLJS vector carrier with cross-host node-shape claims, and it also describes the render tree as serialisable while allowing runtime-only handler values in attrs. Consider tightening the contract so the conceptual shape, carrier choices, and serialisation boundary are stated separately and consistently.
close_reason: 004-Views: split render-tree contract into three subsections — conceptual node shape (host-independent), carrier (host-specific table including hiccup vs JSX vs tuple), and serialisation boundary (structure serialisable; function-valued attrs are not). Removes the CLJS-vector-as-cross-host-claim mix.

title: Suggestion: close 005 machine contract gaps around reserved fx, state-node grammar, and unhandled-event naming
description: Latest per-document pass: 005 still has concrete contract drift around destroy-machine as an effect surface, the complete state-node grammar, whether sub-events are locked or still open, and which unhandled-event name is canonical. Consider collapsing those into one authoritative grammar/effects section.
close_reason: 005-StateMachines: added :destroy-machine to the reserved-fx-ids section (closing the surface gap); expanded §State nodes to list the complete grammar (:on/:always/:after/:entry/:exit/:invoke/:initial/:states/:meta) with capability-gating note; canonicalised :rf.warning/machine-unhandled-event (was split across :machine.event/unhandled and :rf.warning/unhandled-event); promoted sub-event call-site shape from open question to RESOLVED.

title: Suggestion: move non-frame surfaces out of 002-Frames and consolidate reg-frame grammar
description: Latest per-document pass: 002 still acts as a second home for view behavior, machine contracts, and clock/interp APIs, while also spreading reg-frame grammar across multiple sections and leaving compute-sub undefined in one place. Consider reducing 002 back to frame-only surface plus one canonical reg-frame contract.
close_reason: 002-Frames: clarified registry-query ownership in 002 table preamble; replaced clock/interop section with pointer to 005 (where it now canonically lives); pointer-only compute-sub reference cites 008; renamed §reg-frame is atomic to mark canonical metadata grammar location.

title: Suggestion: clarify 001 ownership boundaries for machine-local declarations, metadata shape, and registrar query APIs
description: Latest per-document pass: 001 still leaves three ownership questions open inside the main registration spec: whether machine guards/actions are registry entries or machine-local declarations; whether metadata shape lives here or in Spec-Schemas; and whether the registrar query API is owned here or in 002. Consider consolidating those boundaries so 001 can serve as a single canonical owner.
close_reason: 001-Registration: added §Canonical ownership boundaries clarifying machine guards/actions are machine-local declarations (not registry kind), metadata-shape prose lives here while Malli schema lives in Spec-Schemas, registry query API contract is owned here with 002 cross-referencing for runtime-state queries.

title: Suggestion: clarify 000-Vision required-vs-optional shape description and unify frame summary
description: Latest per-document pass: 000 still contradicts itself on whether shape description is optional or pattern-required, and it gives multiple incompatible summaries of what a frame contains. Consider tightening those two foundation contracts so 000 has one authoritative statement for each.

title: Pattern-WebSocket — long-lived connection lifecycle as a state machine
description:
WebSockets do not fit Pattern-AsyncEffect (rf2-boxi); they are state-machine-shaped: long-lived connection with retry, backoff, server-pushed events, heartbeat, subscription management, message correlation, buffer/queue when disconnected, re-auth on reconnect. The natural canonical answer is a state machine that owns the connection lifecycle.

Document as Pattern-WebSocket.md, separate from Pattern-AsyncEffect.

The pattern composes the locked machine substrate: hierarchical states (rf2-3tk closed) for connection lifecycle; :after (rf2-f6g5 closed) for backoff timers; :always (rf2-9ln closed) for max-retries guards and queued-message flushing; machine-scoped :guards / :actions for per-machine logic; :invoke for declarative connection actors; Pattern-StaleDetection (rf2-035a) for stale replies from previous connection epochs.

Canonical states: :disconnected → :connecting → :authenticating → :connected (with sub-states :idle / :sending / :receiving) → :reconnecting (with :after backoff) → :failed (max retries exceeded). Hierarchical because :connected has internal sub-states (idle, sending, receiving, ready-to-receive); other top-level states do not need hierarchy.

What goes in Pattern-WebSocket.md: connection lifecycle state machine; reconnection with exponential backoff via :after; max-retries via :always with machine-scoped guard; authentication on (re)connect plus token refresh; subscription management (machine tracks subscribed topics in :data); message correlation for request-reply via in-flight requests map keyed by request-id; buffer/queue when disconnected, flushed on :connected entry; server-pushed events as dispatched events; heartbeat/keepalive via :after for pings; SSR: machine no-ops in SSR mode (mirrors :after no-op rule); composition with Pattern-StaleDetection.

Anti-patterns to call out: implementing reconnect logic in setTimeout from fx-handler; mutating app-db from on-message callback directly; per-message machine-spawn-and-destroy; treating WebSocket as Pattern-AsyncEffect.

Files to update when actioning: new docs/specification/Pattern-WebSocket.md (Type: Pattern); Pattern-AsyncEffect.md cross-reference 'for long-lived connections see Pattern-WebSocket'; 005-StateMachines.md cross-reference as worked example; README.md index.

Open calls: SSE / EventSource / WebRTC same pattern? Lean: yes; cover briefly. Subscription protocol: document canonical pattern (machine tracks topics in :data; entry to :connected re-subscribes); apps customise. Message format (EDN/JSON/MessagePack/Protobuf): out of pattern scope; host serialises.

Pairs with: rf2-boxi (Pattern-AsyncEffect, distinct but adjacent); rf2-035a (Pattern-StaleDetection, composes for stale replies); rf2-un3y (Pattern-Boot, may include 'establish WebSocket' as boot phase); rf2-3tk closed (hierarchical states); rf2-f6g5 closed (:after for backoff); rf2-9ln closed (:always for max-retries); rf2-13lo (Implementor Checklist Part 1 capability question yes/no); rf2-d78p (no-Suspense — loading state from connection lifecycle is explicit, not Suspense-style).

Why P2: common production need for real-time SPAs; named pattern saves every team from re-inventing the connection state machine. Pairs with rf2-boxi: AsyncEffect is universally needed; WebSocket is needed by the subset with real-time requirements.

Status: open. Action when there is appetite for another pattern doc.
close_reason: Pattern-WebSocket.md created: connection lifecycle state machine (disconnected/connecting/authenticating/connected[idle|sending|receiving]/reconnecting/failed), exponential backoff via :after, max-retries via :always, machine-scoped actions for resubscribe/flush-queue/correlation, server-pushed events as dispatched events, SSR no-op rule, anti-patterns. Cross-refs added in 005-StateMachines.md, README.md; SSE/WebRTC noted same shape.

title: Document the no-Suspense choice — loading state is explicit data, not implicit suspension
description:
React Suspense is a popular mechanism for declarative loading UI: a component "suspends" while async work runs; React shows a fallback; the component renders normally on resolve. Loading state is implicit in the suspended-component machinery.

Re-frame2 takes the opposite approach: **loading state is explicit data in app-db.** Pattern-RemoteData :status :loading; the Nine States example (rf2-orj9) demonstrates this canonically. Views read the state and branch on it.

This is a deliberate architectural choice worth calling out explicitly so developers from React (and other Suspense-using frameworks) understand the design.

## Why explicit-loading-state wins for re-frame2

The choice falls out of Single Store + substrate-agnosticism:

1. **Single Store invariant.** Loading state IS state; it lives in app-db with everything else. Goal 2 (revertibility) requires this — a frame is fully revertible only if all its state is in the value.
2. **Substrate-agnostic.** Suspense is React-specific. Re-frame2 supports plain-atom (JVM / headless tests / SSR), Reagent (CLJS), and future substrates per Spec 006. A Suspense-based approach would couple re-frame2 to React.
3. **Testability.** Headless tests of "what does this view render when loading?" are straightforward when loading is just data. Suspense requires a React-aware test renderer.
4. **Inspectability.** Tools can introspect app-db to see what is loading. Suspense state is not queryable from outside the React tree.
5. **SSR symmetry.** Loading state during SSR is just app-db state (loaded synchronously on server). Suspense streaming-SSR has complex boundary semantics that re-frame2 sidesteps.
6. **AI-amenability.** "Is this view loading?" is a sub query; AI can reason about it. Suspense throws-Promise machinery is harder to reason about.
7. **Cross-component coordination.** Sibling views can ask "is this data loaded?" via subs. With Suspense, sibling components do not naturally know about each others suspended state.
8. **Composes with state machines.** Loading is itself a state (a Pattern-RemoteData status); a state machine governs the transitions. Suspense does not compose with state machines — its a parallel mechanism.

## Trade-offs to acknowledge

- **More verbose at the call site.** A view reads the loading state and branches: (if loading? Loading-view Loaded-view). With Suspense, the view code is "synchronous"; the Suspense boundary handles the fallback elsewhere.
- **User must remember to surface loading state.** Pattern-RemoteData provides the slice; users must register the four standard events; AI scaffolding (CP-N for remote-data) makes this mechanical.

These costs are small; the wins above justify them.

## What to document

A short normative note explaining the choice, placed where developers from React will land. Best home: **004-Views.md** has a §"Why explicit loading state, not Suspense" subsection. Cross-references from:

- Pattern-RemoteData.md — the canonical pattern that exemplifies the explicit-loading approach.
- Pattern-AsyncEffect.md (rf2-boxi when actioned) — the foundational pattern; loading state is the visible side of "fx in flight."
- Nine States example (rf2-orj9 when actioned) — exhaustive demonstration.
- Optionally, 000-Vision.md as a Lessons-from-React-style note ("what we deliberately choose not to inherit").

## Anti-patterns to call out

- Hiding loading state in component-local React state (defeats single-store, inspectability).
- Building a Suspense-equivalent in re-frame2 (e.g., events that "throw" until data arrives) — opposite of the spec.
- Forgetting to surface loading state at all (silent loading; users wait for "done" with no UI feedback).

## Composition with patterns

- Pattern-RemoteData :status field is the canonical place loading state lives.
- Pattern-Boot makes the boot phase visible via :rf/boot {:phase :authenticating}.
- Nine States exhaustively renders all loading states.
- State machines naturally have :loading as a state with :on transitions.

All of these compose cleanly because they share the explicit-state approach. A Suspense-based machinery would collide with all of them.

## Where this fits in the bead family

This bead is **documentation** of an architectural choice that is already implicit. Nothing new is built; the pattern is already there. The bead is to make the choice **discoverable and defensible** — a developer reading the spec sees it called out; an AI scaffolding views knows the rule (loading state is explicit; do not invent Suspense-style abstraction).

## Files to update when actioning

- 004-Views.md: new §"Loading state is explicit, not implicit" subsection (~30 lines). Cross-references Pattern-RemoteData and Nine States.
- Pattern-RemoteData.md: add a one-line cross-reference to the new 004 section.
- README.md: optional brief mention if there is a "design choices" section (otherwise none).
- Optionally: a short note in 000-Vision §What we retain / what we change.

## Why P2

Documentation of a real choice that affects what an AI scaffolding views might assume. AIs trained on React data may try to suggest Suspense-style abstractions; the doc is the corrective. Also: critical for the "developer-from-React onboarding" experience, which is a sizeable portion of re-frame2 newcomers.

Not P1 because the architecture works without the explicit doc — the lack of Suspense is not a bug; just an unstated choice. P3 would be too low; this is the kind of thing that gets misunderstood without explicit calling-out.

## Pairs with

- rf2-boxi (Pattern-AsyncEffect) — explicit loading state is the visible side of in-flight async.
- rf2-orj9 (Nine States) — the canonical demonstration.
- Pattern-RemoteData.md — the canonical slice for loading state.
- Goal 2 in 000-Vision (Frame state revertibility) — explicit state is required for revertibility.

Status: open. Action when 004-Views.md is being touched, or as a small standalone pass.
close_reason: Added 004-Views §Loading state is explicit, not implicit (after the render-tree contract), covering the choice, eight wins, trade-offs, and anti-patterns; cross-referenced from Pattern-RemoteData.

title: Pattern-Boot — application boot as a chained-async sequence (state-machine canonical form)
description:
Real SPAs have a multi-step initialisation sequence with dependencies: read config → authenticate / restore session → load user profile / feature flags → hydrate from localStorage → resolve routing → connect real-time services → mount UI. Each step depends on the previous; failure at any step is usually fatal or specifically recoverable; the user wants to see progress.

Today re-frame2 implies the answer is "chain via dispatched events" — :app/init dispatches :auth/restore-session which on success dispatches :user/load-profile which on success dispatches :app/ready. Workable but unstructured; the boot sequence itself is invisible across N unrelated event handlers.

This pattern doc would name the canonical answer.

## What makes booting distinctive

Vs the generic async pattern (Pattern-AsyncEffect):

- **Sequential dependencies.** Step N+1 depends on step N. Cannot parallelise (mostly).
- **Failure semantics differ.** A failed step usually halts boot; the user sees an error page, not a partial app. Some failures are recoverable with retry; others are fatal.
- **Visible progress.** Users want to see "Loading profile..." then "Connecting..." then "Almost ready..." The boot UI is its own thing distinct from the running-app UI.
- **One-shot semantics.** Booting is conceptually run once per app load. Re-booting is unusual but should work (e.g., session expired → re-authenticate → resume).
- **SSR has a parallel concern.** Server-side init via :rf/server-init covers part of this; client-side booting handles the post-hydration steps.
- **Hot-reload concerns.** In dev, boot should not re-run on every code reload; only on initial app load. The boot machine probably commits to "post-boot" terminal-state and stays there.

## The state-machine canonical form

A boot state machine with explicit states for each phase:

  States: :configuring → :authenticating → :loading-profile → :hydrating → :routing → :ready
  Plus error states: :auth-failed, :profile-failed, :network-error, :fatal-error
  Plus retry states: :retrying-auth, :retrying-profile

Each state uses :invoke to spawn the async work; transitions on success/failure; entry actions update progress UI; the whole sequence is one inspectable, testable, traceable state machine.

This fits re-frame2 perfectly given recent locks: machines + :invoke + hierarchical states + machine-scoped guards/actions + epoch-based stale detection (Pattern-StaleDetection).

## Simpler form for trivial boot

If the boot is just one or two steps (e.g., simple apps that just need to read config and mount), a state machine is overkill. Pattern-Boot should also document the chained-events form:

  (rf/reg-event-fx :app/init
    (fn [_ _]
      {:fx [[:dispatch [:config/load]]]}))

  (rf/reg-event-fx :config/load
    (fn [_ _]
      {:fx [[:http {:url "/config" :on-success [:config/loaded]
                                    :on-error   [:app/init-failed]}]]}))

  (rf/reg-event-fx :config/loaded
    (fn [_ [_ config]]
      {:db (assoc db :config config)
       :fx [[:dispatch [:app/ready]]]}))

The pattern doc shows the spectrum: simple chained events for trivial boots; state machines when the boot graph has more than ~3 steps, error states, retries, or visible progress.

## What goes in Pattern-Boot.md

1. The boot problem stated: chained async + visible progress + sequential dependencies + one-shot.
2. The simple chained-events form for trivial boots (worked example: 2-step boot).
3. The state-machine canonical form (worked example: realistic 6-step boot — config, auth, profile, hydrate, route, ready).
4. Standard boot states and their meaning.
5. Error handling: per-step error transitions; user-facing recovery; fatal-error UX.
6. Boot UI: how to render progress from app-db; standard slice :rf/boot {:phase :authenticating :progress 0.4 :error nil}.
7. SSR composition: server-side :rf/server-init runs the server-equivalent of boot; client-side boot picks up from the hydrated app-db; document the handoff.
8. Hot-reload: boot machine commits to :ready state and stays; re-evaluating handlers does not re-boot.
9. Re-boot semantics: explicit dispatch :app/re-boot returns to :configuring; rare but useful for session-expired flows.
10. Anti-patterns:
    - Putting boot logic in view :on-mount lifecycle (ties boot to view; non-headless-testable).
    - Booting via top-level side effects at namespace load (no error handling; not deterministic).
    - Mixing boot logic with running-app logic (boot states should be terminal-state-distinct).

## Composition with other patterns

- **Pattern-AsyncEffect (rf2-boxi):** each boot step is an instance of the generic async pattern.
- **Pattern-StaleDetection:** if the user navigates or reloads during boot, in-flight replies need stale-detection. Boot state machines naturally provide the epoch via state transitions; stale replies are ignored when the boot has advanced or completed.
- **Pattern-RemoteData:** profile / config / feature-flag fetches are concrete instances; the boot machine drives them.
- **Pattern-Forms:** if boot includes a "set up your account" step, forms compose at that state.

## Where it lives

`docs/specification/Pattern-Boot.md` — companion to existing Pattern docs.

## Files to update when actioning

- New: `docs/specification/Pattern-Boot.md` (Type: Pattern).
- 002-Frames.md §Frame metadata: cross-reference Pattern-Boot from the :on-create discussion.
- 011-SSR.md: cross-reference Pattern-Boot for the client-side post-hydration boot sequence; describe the SSR/CSR boot handoff.
- README.md: index Pattern-Boot.
- Construction-Prompts.md: optional CP for scaffolding boot machines.

## Open calls

1. **Boot recovery.** What is the canonical user-facing recovery story? Per-step retry button? Full reload? Configurable per-app? Lean: document a default (per-step retry where possible, full reload for fatal errors); apps customise.

2. **SSR-side init pattern.** :rf/server-init in 011 is the existing surface; should Pattern-Boot also cover this side, or is it a client-side-only pattern? Lean: document both, with the handoff (server-side runs steps 1-N; client picks up from N+1 after hydration).

3. **Hot-reload re-boot rule.** Does dev-mode hot-reload re-run boot? Lean: no — boot is one-shot per app load; hot-reload preserves the :ready state. Document.

4. **Boot vs initial-route resolution.** If routing depends on auth (a "must-be-logged-in" route), is route resolution part of boot or after boot? Lean: route resolution is a boot step (state :routing); the route-on-mount happens within boot, not after.

## Why P2

Foundational pattern for any non-trivial SPA. Most production apps re-invent this; naming it makes the canonical answer discoverable. Not P1 because apps work without it (just unstructured), but the pattern doc is high-value for AI scaffolding (rf2-5pt) — an AI scaffolding a new app immediately wants the boot pattern.

## Pairs with

- rf2-boxi (Pattern-AsyncEffect) — boot is a sequence of these.
- Pattern-StaleDetection — composes for boot-time staleness.
- Pattern-RemoteData — concrete instances of boot steps.
- 002-Frames :on-create — entry point for boot.
- 011-SSR :rf/server-init — server-side boot equivalent.
- rf2-13lo (Implementors Checklist) — boot is part of the pattern surface every host must support.
- rf2-kq2z (RealWorld worked example) — RealWorld has its own boot sequence; the pattern would inform the implementation.

Status: open. Action when there is appetite for another foundational pattern doc.
close_reason: Pattern-Boot.md created: chained-events simple form + six-state boot machine (configuring/authenticating/loading-profile/hydrating/routing/ready) with retry-with-backoff via :after, machine-scoped guards/actions, boot UI subs, SSR handoff, hot-reload one-shot rule, re-boot semantics, anti-patterns. Indexed in README.md.

title: Pattern-AsyncEffect — name the canonical "external work + dispatched reply" pattern
description:
Re-frame2 implicitly relies on a single generic async-effect pattern: register an fx, the fx posts work to an external system, the external system replies asynchronously, a listener dispatches a re-frame event with the result, the event handler updates state. This is the foundational shape for all async work in re-frame.

Today the pattern is implicit knowledge. Document it explicitly as a Pattern doc parallel to Pattern-RemoteData, Pattern-Forms, Pattern-StaleDetection.

## The pattern

Six-step shape (each instance fills in concrete steps):

1. Register an fx for the external work.
2. The event handler returns :fx [[:my/external-thing args]].
3. The fx-handler posts work to the external system (HTTP, postMessage, IndexedDB, etc.).
4. The external system replies asynchronously.
5. A listener (registered at boot or per-call) translates the reply into a re-frame dispatch.
6. The dispatched event handler updates state from the result.

Each instance differs only in the concrete external system; the *shape* is invariant.

## Why this is worth naming

- AI scaffolding: an AI generating an integration with a new external system (a SaaS API, a worker, a Bluetooth device, anything async) reaches for the named pattern instead of inventing the shape.
- Implementor guidance: porting re-frame2 to another host is easier when the canonical async surface is named and documented; the host knows what fxs to provide.
- Composition with Pattern-StaleDetection: stale-detection is what you add when the dispatcher may have moved on by the time the reply arrives. The generic pattern + the stale-detection pattern compose into the specific cases (Pattern-RemoteData, navigation epoch, :after).
- Composition with Pattern-RemoteData: remote-data is the specific case of this pattern with a known reply shape (success/error/loaded-at). Naming the generic pattern lets RemoteData cite it cleanly.

## Concrete instances (each is a Pattern doc or convention)

- HTTP requests (Pattern-RemoteData specifies this explicitly)
- Workers (postMessage in/out)
- WebSockets (open/send/receive — though continuous, individual messages fit the pattern)
- IndexedDB queries
- File system / OPFS operations
- Crypto / WebAuthn operations
- Service Worker messaging
- Native bridges in mobile-shell apps (React Native, Capacitor, Tauri)
- AI / LLM API calls
- Geolocation / device sensor queries
- Background sync APIs

All of these instantiate the same shape. Re-frame2 does not need a separate fx primitive per case; users register their own fxs that fit the pattern.

## What goes in Pattern-AsyncEffect.md

A short doc covering:

1. The six-step shape (above).
2. A worked example using HTTP (cite Pattern-RemoteData for the lifecycle slice).
3. A worked example using workers (cite the worker-as-fx framing).
4. The composition with Pattern-StaleDetection (when reply arrives but dispatcher has moved on, suppress).
5. Anti-patterns:
   - Mutating app-db from inside the fx-handler (the fx-handler is a side-effect; results must come back via dispatched events).
   - Omitting :on-success / :on-error (callers must declare the reply event explicitly; no implicit dispatching).
   - Passing closures across the boundary (events must serialise; closures dont).
6. Cross-references to:
   - Pattern-RemoteData (specific case)
   - Pattern-StaleDetection (composition for staleness)
   - 002-Frames §Side effects (where the fx mechanism lives)

## Where it lives

`docs/specification/Pattern-AsyncEffect.md` — companion to existing Pattern docs.

Alternatively (less preferred): a §section within Pattern-RemoteData renamed to acknowledge the broader pattern. Trade-off: conflates two things; loses the modular reuse from other Pattern docs.

Lean: standalone Pattern-AsyncEffect.md as the foundational doc; specific applications (Pattern-RemoteData, future Pattern-Worker if needed) cite it.

## Files to update when actioning

- New: `docs/specification/Pattern-AsyncEffect.md` (Type: Pattern).
- Pattern-RemoteData.md: add a note at the top — "Pattern-RemoteData is the specific case of [Pattern-AsyncEffect](Pattern-AsyncEffect.md) for HTTP requests with the standard 5-key lifecycle slice."
- Pattern-StaleDetection.md: add a note — "When the dispatcher of a Pattern-AsyncEffect interaction may have moved on by the time the reply arrives, compose with this pattern."
- 002-Frames.md §Side effects (or similar): add a one-line cross-reference to Pattern-AsyncEffect for the canonical async shape.
- README.md: add Pattern-AsyncEffect to the companion-docs index.
- Construction-Prompts.md: optional — a CP for scaffolding async-effect integrations.

## Pairs with

- Pattern-RemoteData.md (specific HTTP case)
- Pattern-StaleDetection.md (composition for stale-suppression)
- rf2-13lo (Implementors Checklist) — Pattern-AsyncEffect is part of the foundation surface every implementation must support.

## Why P2

Foundational documentation; every async-shape feature in re-frame2 depends on this implicit knowledge. Naming it makes the pattern teachable, AI-scaffoldable, and consistent across implementations. Not P1 because the pattern works whether or not its named — current users figure it out from Pattern-RemoteData and the existing fx mechanism.

Status: open. Action when worker / future async beads come up; the pattern doc fills in foundational ground that those depend on.
close_reason: Pattern-AsyncEffect.md created: six-step shape, HTTP worked example, anti-patterns, composition with RemoteData/StaleDetection/WebSocket/Boot. Cross-refs added in Pattern-RemoteData.md, Pattern-StaleDetection.md, 002-Frames.md, README.md.

title: Suggestion: unify duplicated handler-body DSL definitions and normalize Spec-Schemas entries
description:
Why this suggestion exists
- Spec-Schemas.md currently defines the handler-body DSL twice with mismatched shapes, which directly undermines its role as a schema catalogue.
- Many entries also mix schema shape with design rationale, migration advice, and lifecycle commentary.

Suggested change
- Keep one canonical handler-body DSL definition and make the later section reference it.
- Normalize entries to shape + minimal field notes + cross-ref.
- Reduce front/back matter to a short how to read this catalogue note and move longer rationale elsewhere.

Real benefit
- Restores the file’s credibility as a reference catalogue.
- Removes direct implementer ambiguity from competing schema definitions.
- Improves lookup speed and reduces formatting/rendering risk.

title: Suggestion: keep Construction-Prompts build-facing and dedupe common scaffolding mechanics
description:
Why this suggestion exists

- It also repeats preflight/checklist mechanics across multiple CP sections while simultaneously describing itself as a planned catalogue.

Suggested change

- Remove or compress agent workflow / maintenance narratives.
- Either declare the file the canonical consolidated prompt set, or extract a shared preflight/checklist preamble and keep each CP to its deltas.

Real benefit
- Faster use as a construction artefact.
- Cleaner boundary between scaffolding and specification.
- Lower maintenance drift from repeated scaffolding boilerplate.
close_reason: Added a shared pre-flight preamble at the top so each CP carries kind-specific delta only. Removed the agent-workflow narrative ('How an AI uses these', 'What construction prompts do not cover') — those describe agent process rather than build mechanics.

title: Suggestion: keep AI-Audit evidentiary by removing roadmap-style next actions and live-design commentary
description:
Why this suggestion exists
- AI-Audit.md currently moves beyond audit findings into prioritized next actions and live design-state commentary.
- That makes it read partly like planning material instead of a durable audit record.

Suggested change
- Keep per-spec findings and, if useful, one synthesis layer.
- Remove or relocate prioritized work queues and transient wording such as ecent edit, 	racked elsewhere, or similar live-status phrases.

Real benefit
- Preserves the file as a stable audit artefact.
- Reduces duplication with issue tracking and planning.
- Makes the audit easier to trust and cite.
close_reason: Removed the 'Recommended next actions, in priority order' roadmap section. Compressed 'Headline finding' to a one-paragraph evidentiary summary. Stripped 'recent edit' / 'tracked elsewhere' live-state phrases from per-Spec gap notes.

title: Suggestion: tighten Tool-Pair.md into a precise runtime contract and move open questions out
description:
Why this suggestion exists
- Tool-Pair.md spends substantial space on philosophy/non-goals and ends with open questions and lean recommendations, which weakens it as a tool/runtime contract.
- Its time-travel surface is also described in prose without a fully explicit record/ordering/failure contract.

Suggested change
- Foreground committed runtime surface only.
- Move open questions and broader philosophy to a separate design note.
- Tighten time-travel sections into an explicit contract block with record schema, ordering guarantees, and restore/error behavior.

Real benefit
- Makes the document safer for tool authors to implement against.
- Reduces ambiguity around the pair-tool runtime boundary.
- Improves scanability by keeping callable/observable contract ahead of commentary.
close_reason: Tightened time-travel into an explicit contract block: recording, ordering, bounded history, query, restore, restore failure modes, restore caveat, production elision. Moved 'Open questions' out into a non-normative 'Design notes' appendix below cross-references.

title: Suggestion: tighten pattern-doc role boundaries across Pattern-RemoteData, Pattern-Forms, and Pattern-StaleDetection
description:
Why this suggestion exists
- The pattern docs are useful, but several sections read as hidden normative spec, design diary, or future-library planning instead of reusable conventions.
- Examples: exact event names treated as canonical API in Pattern-RemoteData, requirement-style future checklist and ambiguous dirty? rule in Pattern-Forms, and runtime-level formal wording in Pattern-StaleDetection.

Suggested change
- Keep these docs explicitly in the pattern/convention lane.
- Separate canonical roles from concrete CLJS naming/examples.
- Move future/roadmap/checklist material into appendices or separate implementation notes.
- Resolve any remaining pattern-level ambiguities such as dirty? semantics by either locking one rule or marking alternatives explicitly.

Real benefit
- Prevents support docs from becoming hidden sources of normative behavior.
- Makes the pattern catalogue easier to reuse across domains and hosts.
- Reduces ambiguity between spec requirement and ecommended convention.
close_reason: Added explicit 'Role' sections to all three pattern docs clarifying that exact event names are illustrative, the canonical content is the slice shape and lifecycle structure. Pattern-Forms: locked the dirty? rule (compared against :submitted when non-nil, otherwise defaults — single rule, no alternatives). Moved future/scaffolding-macro material into a clearer conformance-checklist section.

title: Suggestion: keep Conventions.md convention-only by removing API restatements and process spillover
description:

ormative specifications with reference/support docs like API.md and MIGRATION.md, and it leaves the
close_reason: Clarified the docs/specification/README taxonomy: explicit two-bucket classification (normative vs companion) with how API.md/MIGRATION.md fit. Added a corpus boundary statement that every doc declares its bucket via the Type header.

ormative principles from informative rationale/essays.

description:

Locked direction: server is authoritative; client app-db is replaced on hydrate.

title: Lock :fx ordering and atomicity — :db first, :fx entries in source order
description:
Lock the contract that the current 002-Frames.md drain semantics implies but doesn't yet state explicitly:

## The locked rules

When an event handler returns {:db ... :fx [[a 1] [b 2] [c 3]]}:

1. **:db is always the first side effect (when present).** The snapshot transitions atomically in one step before any :fx entry processes.
2. **:fx entries are processed in source order.** [a 1] runs before [b 2] runs before [c 3]. Users can rely on this ordering.
3. **Each :fx entry is processed serially before the next.** No interleaving. The fx-handler for entry N completes before entry N+1 begins.
4. **Subscriptions observe the post-:db state.** When the first :fx entry (e.g., :dispatch or :http) fires, app-db has already transitioned. Sub-cache invalidation has happened.

## Why state this explicitly

The drain semantics already imply ordering, but the contract isn't crisply stated for users. Without an explicit lock:
- Users can't rely on patterns like {:db <new-state> :fx [[:dispatch [:notify-of-state-change]]]} where the dispatched handler reads the new state.
- Implementations (re-frame-cljs, re-frame-js, etc.) may differ on whether :fx is sequential or concurrent.
- AI tools writing fx-emitting handlers don't know whether they can chain :fx entries.

## Atomicity

From the *external observer's* perspective:
- The :db transition is atomic — no observer sees a half-written app-db.
- The :fx entries are processed serially after the snapshot commit.
- Each fx-handler runs in the post-:db state.

From the *event handler's* perspective:
- The handler returns once with the full effects map.
- Sequencing of :fx entries is deterministic.
- The handler doesn't observe the side effects firing; it just declares them.

## How this composes with the dispatch queue

When :fx entries include :dispatch, the dispatched events are appended to the runtime queue in source order. Since the runtime queue is FIFO (per drain semantics), this preserves the source-order property all the way down the chain.

When :fx entries include :dispatch-later, the setTimeout-equivalent calls happen in source order, but the actual delivery depends on delays (each scheduled independently).

## Composition with machines

Machine action effects (:data + :fx) follow the same rule:
1. :data merges first (via :db write at the machine's path).
2. :fx entries process in source order (per the existing Level 1 drain semantics).
3. Within :fx, reserved fx-ids (:raise, :spawn) are recognised and routed; other entries pass through to the standard fx pipeline.

This is already what 005-StateMachines §Drain semantics §Level 1 says; this bead consolidates the rule cross-spec.

## Open design call: error-during-fx semantics

If :fx [[:a] [:b] [:c]] and the fx-handler for [:a] throws, what happens to [:b] and [:c]?

Three options:
1. **Halt the chain.** Subsequent fx are skipped; the error is traced; the snapshot commit is preserved.
2. **Continue the chain.** Subsequent fx run regardless; each error is traced independently.
3. **Halt for some categories; continue for others.** Critical fx (e.g., :db) always halt; user fx may continue.

**Lean: option 2 (continue the chain).** Reasoning:
- :fx entries are by design independent. Failing :a shouldn't prevent :b from running.
- Error tracing per fx gives debugging visibility.
- Halting on first error makes ordering ALSO mean dependency, which conflates two things.
- An fx that genuinely depends on a prior fx succeeding should be lifted to an event-handler chain (use :dispatch, observe the result via cofx).

But this is a real call worth flagging.

## Files to update when actioning

- 002-Frames.md: new §:fx ordering and atomicity guarantees; document the four locked rules; cross-reference to drain semantics.
- API.md: add a callout under :fx in the effect-map shape table; reference the §:fx ordering section.
- Spec-Schemas.md: add a normative note in the :rf/effect-map schema description.
- 005-StateMachines.md: cross-reference from §Actions / §Drain semantics §Level 1 — same rules apply at the machine layer.
- conformance/fixtures/: add 1-2 fixtures: fx-ordering-source-order.edn (verify :fx entries fire in declared order), fx-db-first.edn (verify :db is observable to the first :fx entry).

## Why P2

Foundational correctness. Apps already rely on this implicitly; making it explicit strengthens the contract for cross-impl conformance and AI-implementability. Not P1 because today's re-frame already behaves this way — it's a documentation/lock pass, not a behaviour change.

## Pairs with

- 002-Frames.md drain semantics (the existing place this rule lives implicitly).
- 005-StateMachines.md drain semantics (Level 1 — within a single action's effect map; same rule).
- rf2-5pt (AI implementability) — the contract becomes mechanical for cross-impl conformance.
- rf2-aspv (P1 HTTP response contract) — :rf/response is part of :db; ordering applies.

Status: open. Design largely settled (matches existing implicit behaviour); one open call (error-during-fx semantics) to resolve at action time. Estimated effort: small focused agent pass — mostly documentation lock, no behaviour change.
close_reason: Locked the four :fx ordering/atomicity rules in 002 (new section), Spec-Schemas (normative note on :rf/effect-map), and 005 (cross-ref from Drain §Level 1); added fx-db-first.edn and fx-ordering-source-order.edn fixtures; resolved error-during-fx open call as continue-the-chain with per-error tracing.

title: Suggestion: resolve 002-Frames :preset status and trim duplicate resolved-decision prose
description:
Why this suggestion exists
- 002 currently treats :preset as both normative API and still-open design, which creates a direct spec ambiguity.
- The document also restates major frame rules again in Resolved decisions, leaving two maintenance surfaces for the same contract.

Suggested change
- Decide whether :preset is locked or provisional and make the whole section reflect that single status.
- Reduce Resolved decisions to a brief decision index or fold still-normative rules back into the earlier contract sections.

Real benefit
- Removes ambiguity about what an implementation must ship now.
- Reduces contradiction risk inside one of the load-bearing foundation specs.
- Improves scanability of the core frame contract.

title: Suggestion: reorder 005-StateMachines around snapshot and grammar semantics first
description:
Why this suggestion exists
- 005 introduces query/introspection surfaces before it finishes defining the machine snapshot and transition grammar, which makes the document read like a guided essay instead of an implementation reference.
- Important rules such as machine-local guard/action resolution are also repeated across multiple sections.

Suggested change
- Restructure 005 as: snapshot contract, grammar, transition semantics, then query/introspection APIs, then non-normative notes.
- Consolidate repeated machine-local resolution rules into one authoritative normative section.

Real benefit
- Reduces reader backtracking in the largest and most complex spec.
- Makes the core machine contract easier to implement directly.
- Lowers drift risk from repeated rule statements.

title: Suggestion: clarify 006-ReactiveSubstrate contract categories and exact required surface

Why this suggestion exists

- Host examples and reference-host notes are also interleaved with the normative adapter surface.

Suggested change

- Separate Normative contract, Operational semantics, and Reference-host notes.

Real benefit

- Removes immediate ambiguity about what every implementation must provide.
- Cleans up one of the key host-portability specs.

title: Suggestion: resolve 007-Stories data-only variant contract vs fn-valued examples
description:
Why this suggestion exists
- 007 says variants are serialisable artefacts with no fn-valued slots, but later examples place functions in variant fields such as :args->events, :loaders, and decorator payloads.

Suggested change
- Pick one contract: either variants are strictly data-only, or function-valued escape hatches are part of the supported model.
- Rewrite the examples and field descriptions so they match the chosen contract exactly.

Real benefit
- Restores a single implementable artefact shape for validators, registrars, and tooling.
- Prevents downstream tools from guessing which examples are real and which are illustrative.

title: Suggestion: reconcile 009-Instrumentation error taxonomy into one authoritative model
description:
Why this suggestion exists
- 009 currently presents two incompatible error models: early sections treat :op-type as categories like :error/handler, while the later error contract makes :op-type a coarse severity and moves categories into :operation.
- That is a genuine spec ambiguity for implementers.

Suggested change
- Keep one authoritative error-model subsection.
- Align or remove the earlier :op-type taxonomy so every reference to trace errors uses the same contract.
- Move non-normative wording guidance and vendor-integration advice out of the main contract flow.

Real benefit
- Eliminates an implementation-level ambiguity in a cross-cutting spec.
- Makes the trace/error surface safer to consume as a contract.
- Improves scanability by reducing spec-vs-guidance mixing.

title: Suggestion: make 001-Registration single-source for metadata grammar and registry model

Why this suggestion exists

Suggested change

title: Suggestion: collapse 000-Vision's migration/back-compat story into one canonical section
description:
Why this suggestion exists
- The mechanical-upgrade story is one of 000's most important constraints, but it is currently spread across Hard constraints (C1), Scope, Tensions and resolutions, Backwards compatibility, and the closing Migration pointer.

- Keep one canonical compatibility section in 000 that states the rule: design may change, but every breaking change must be mechanically migratable or explicitly flagged for human review.

Real benefit

- Reduces the chance that migration policy drifts between 000 and MIGRATION.

title: Suggestion: make one canonical pattern-vs-reference summary in 000-Vision
description:
Why this suggestion exists
- 000 repeats the same pattern/reference split several times in different forms: Optional capabilities, What the pattern does NOT over-commit to, Host-profile matrix, The reference implementation table, and parts of Scope.

Suggested change

Real benefit

- Cuts re-reading and lowers the chance of contradictory edits across multiple summaries.
- Makes the front half of 000 denser and easier to navigate.

title: Suggestion: trim 000-Vision to foundation-only and move downstream mini-spec content out
description:
Why this suggestion exists
- 000-Vision currently reads well through the abstract, pattern, constraints, goals, and scope, but then turns into a mini-spec bundle for Frames, Views, Features, Registration, Schemas, Tooling, and Migration.
- Examples: docs/specification/000-Vision.md:538-714 duplicates or previews load-bearing design already owned by 002, 004, 001, 010, and MIGRATION.

- Treat Frames / Views / Registration / Schemas / Tooling / Migration details as owned elsewhere.

Real benefit

- Makes the load-bearing decisions in 000 easier to identify and cite.

title: Server error sanitisation boundary — public projection of SSR errors
description:
React Router explicitly sanitises server errors before they reach the browser in production. The current re-frame2 spec has structured errors and mismatch tracing (009), but does NOT clearly separate:

- Internal/server diagnostic detail (stack traces, exception data, internal codes) → trace surface only.
- Public/client-safe error detail → response payload.

Without this boundary, SSR error pages can leak stack traces, secrets in exception messages, and internal codes to clients.

## Concrete benefit

- No stack traces or secrets leaked to the client.
- Predictable 404/401/500 output.
- Cleaner monitoring and error reporting (full detail in traces; sanitised in response).
- A stable public error shape that SSR fallback templates can rely on.

## Fit with re-frame2

The right move is **not** to invent React-style error boundaries in the SSR core. It's to define a **public projection** of server errors that sits between the structured trace event (rich, internal) and the client-bound response (clean, public).

This fits 009's structured-error direction naturally: the internal :rf.error/* event is preserved in the trace; a sanitisation step produces the public projection for the response.

## Mechanism

Keep the full internal error trace event (per 009). Add a public error shape:

  {:status     500
   :code       :internal-error
   :message    "Something went wrong"
   :retryable? false}

Mapping rule:

1. SSR rendering / setup errors emit the full structured trace event (existing).
2. Before serialising anything to the client (or rendering an error page), the runtime maps the internal error to a public error shape via a registered projector.
3. Known control-flow errors preserve more detail:
   - :404 not found
   - :redirect
   - :validation-failure
   - :unauthorised
4. Unknown exceptions become a generic 500 in production by default. In dev, the public shape can include more detail (configurable via :ssr config).

## Customisation

Two plausible mechanisms:

1. **Registered projector.** (rf/reg-error-projector :myapp/public-error (fn [trace-event] public-shape)). Single registered projector consulted on each server error.
2. **Config-driven.** (rf/configure :ssr {:public-error-id :myapp/public-error}) names the projector to use.

**Lean: both.** A reg-* fn for the projector itself; a configure call to declare which one is active. Allows per-host customisation while keeping the registry-first pattern.

## Default projector

re-frame2 ships a default projector that:
- Maps known categories (:rf.error/no-such-handler → 404 in routing context, etc.).
- Generic 500 with code :internal-error and message "Something went wrong" for unknowns.
- In dev mode (configurable), includes :details with the original trace event.

Users who want different rules register their own projector and configure :ssr to use it.

## Open design calls

1. **Where in the pipeline does sanitisation happen?** Before HTML response render (inside the SSR pipeline) or at the host-adapter boundary (in the wrapper code)? Lean: before render. The HTML response IS the public projection; rendering an error page reads the public shape.

2. **Dev vs prod default behaviour.** Dev shows full detail; prod shows generic. Configurable via :ssr config or by the projector itself reading a build-time flag.

3. **Mapping for known errors.** :rf.error/no-such-handler in routing context → 404. :rf.error/schema-validation-failure → 400. Document the canonical mapping in the default projector.

4. **What about errors from within views (render-time)?** View exceptions during SSR also need projection. Same mechanism applies; document.

## Files to update when actioning

- 011-SSR.md: new §Server error projection; document the public error shape; document the projector mechanism; document the default projector; document dev vs prod behaviour.
- 009-Instrumentation.md: cross-reference the public-projection step from the structured-error contract; clarify that internal trace events are not leaked to the client.
- Spec-Schemas.md: add :rf/public-error schema; document the trace-event → public-error projection contract.
- API.md: add reg-error-projector row; add :ssr config keys for projector configuration; add :rf.error/sanitised-on-projection trace event for cases where the runtime had to fall back to the generic error.
- 001-Registration.md: add :error-projector registry kind.
- conformance/fixtures/: 2 fixtures: ssr-error-sanitisation.edn (handler throws; verify response contains generic 500 with no stack trace; verify trace contains full detail), ssr-error-known-mapping.edn (routing 404; verify response is 404 status with appropriate public shape).

## Why P2 (not P1)

Security/operational hygiene. Apps without it 'work' but leak diagnostic detail. P2 because:
- The trace surface already exists; this is a new use of existing data.
- Default projector covers most cases; users only register custom ones for specialised needs.
- Production-mode default is the safe default (generic 500); the worst-case behaviour without this bead is information leakage, not broken pages.

## Pairs with

- 009-Instrumentation.md structured error contract (the source of internal trace events).
- rf2-aspv (P1 HTTP response contract) — public error projection populates :status / :headers in the response.
- rf2-5pt (AI implementability) — closes a security best-practice gap that production SSR needs.

Status: open. Design proposed; four open calls flagged. Estimated effort: medium focused agent pass.
close_reason: Specced server error sanitisation boundary: :rf/public-error shape, reg-error-projector registry kind, default projector with canonical mapping, before-render placement, dev/prod gating, view-time exception handling, sanitised-on-projection fallback, two conformance fixtures.

title: First-class head/meta contract for SSR — derived-from-state head model
description:
The current 011-SSR.md spec is strong on body rendering but says nothing substantive about head metadata (title, meta tags, canonical URLs, Open Graph, Twitter cards, structured data, etc.). All admired SSR frameworks (Next.js, Nuxt, React Router) make this a first-class concern.

For SSR specifically, head tags belong in the server-rendered HTML (so crawlers see them on first byte), not as a later browser mutation. This is the central reason head/meta needs SSR-level treatment.

## Concrete benefit

- Correct initial <title> and meta tags.
- Canonical URLs for SEO.
- Robots directives.
- Open Graph / Twitter cards (link-unfurling on social platforms).
- Structured data (JSON-LD).
- Tooling can inspect the head model without scraping templates.

## Fit with re-frame2

Fits cleanly **only if treated as data produced from state**, not as an imperative DOM API.

Bad fit (don't do):
- Mutate the document head from inside components ('useHead'-style imperative API).

Good fit (do):
- A pure head model derived from app-db, route, and props.
- Computed during render; serialised as HTML head; client recomputes/confirms on hydration.

Preserves testability, portability, AI-amenability.

## Mechanism

Standard head model:

  {:title "Article title"
   :meta  [{:name "description" :content "..."}
           {:property "og:title" :content "..."}]
   :link  [{:rel "canonical" :href "https://example.com/articles/123"}]
   :html-attrs {}
   :body-attrs {}}

Three plausible exposure surfaces (pick one):

1. **Route metadata** — :head on a route is a pure function or sub-id deriving the head from app-db + route + params.
2. **Registered head function** — (rf/reg-head id ?metadata f) where f is pure like a view; (rf/render-head head-id) emits the head map.
3. **Pure helper over root view** — (rf/render-head frame-id root-view-id props).

**Lean: registered head function** + route :head metadata that names which registered head to use. Keeps head policy enumerable through (handlers :head); separates head/SEO logic from body rendering; pure and testable.

## Default flow

1. SSR request renders the body view.
2. Runtime resolves the active route's :head metadata to a registered head id.
3. (rf/render-head head-id frame-id) returns the head model.
4. Server emits <head>...</head> from the model.
5. Client on hydration recomputes the head from current state (no DOM scraping).
6. Mismatch detection: client compares its computed head to the rendered head; emits :rf.warning/head-mismatch if different.

Optional: tooling exposes (rf/active-head) to query the current head model without re-rendering.

## Open design calls

1. **Default head when no route declares :head.** Sensible default: title from frame metadata, content-type meta, charset meta. Or strict: registration must declare a head; missing emits :rf.error/no-head-registered.

2. **Composition across routes.** Should nested routes compose head fragments (parent + child)? Or is it always a single :head per route? Lean: single :head per route to start; composition is a follow-up if real cases emerge.

3. **Head mismatch handling.** Same as body-render mismatch: detect and warn; client re-renders to its computed head. Or: server head wins (don't override on hydration). Lean: detect-and-warn but client re-renders (consistency with body-mismatch handling).

4. **JSON-LD / structured data shape.** A flat :scripts vector? Or a dedicated :json-ld field? Lean: include in :script or a dedicated :json-ld field for clarity.

## Files to update when actioning

- 011-SSR.md: new §Head/meta contract; document the head model shape; document the registered-head mechanism; document hydration recomputation; document mismatch detection.
- Spec-Schemas.md: add :rf/head-model schema (title, meta, link, html-attrs, body-attrs, scripts, json-ld).
- API.md: add reg-head row; add render-head row; add :rf.warning/head-mismatch warning category.
- 001-Registration.md: add :head registry kind to the per-kind list; document the metadata shape for registered heads.
- conformance/fixtures/: 2 fixtures: ssr-head-emits.edn (verify <title> and meta tags appear in rendered HTML), ssr-head-hydration.edn (verify client-computed head matches server-rendered head).

## Why P2 (not P1)

Important but not correctness-blocking. Apps without explicit head support work; they just don't render correct SEO/social metadata server-side. Worth shipping a standard answer for production use.

## Pairs with

- 011 existing render-to-string story (head rendering is a sibling concern).
- 012 routing — :head is a route-metadata key.
- rf2-5pt (AI implementability) — closes a real-world feature most apps need.
- rf2-aspv (P1 HTTP response contract) — head-tags include status-aware bits like canonical URLs that may depend on the response status.

Status: open. Design proposed; four open calls flagged. Estimated effort: medium focused agent pass.
close_reason: Specced derived-from-state head/meta contract: :rf/head-model schema, reg-head registry kind, route :head metadata pointer, render-head/active-head, single-head-per-route in v1, head-mismatch detection, two conformance fixtures.

title: re-frame2 ships pair-required infra in-tree — eliminate re-frame-pair's dependency on re-frame-10x
description:
Today re-frame-pair (the nREPL-attached AI/REPL companion) requires re-frame-10x to be installed in the host application. The dependency exists because pair leans on infrastructure that historically lives in 10x — trace buffering, epoch history per drain-completion, source-coord query, frame-snapshot introspection.

re-frame2 should ship that infrastructure in-tree as part of the framework's standard surface, so that:

1. **re-frame-pair becomes a thin REPL/nREPL consumer of re-frame2's standard tooling primitives** — no 10x dependency required.
2. **A new re-frame-10x can be rewritten as a thin UI on top of re-frame2's standard tooling primitives** — sharing the substrate with pair instead of providing its own.
3. **Future AI tools (pair-improver per rf2-ps4, custom debug panels, story tools per 007) consume the same standard surface.**

The principle: re-frame2 owns the *infrastructure* (data shapes, query APIs, retention policies); 10x and pair own the *presentation/orchestration* on top.

## What infra moves in-tree

Most of the surface is already specified in Tool-Pair.md and 009-Instrumentation.md as v1-required. This bead is to verify the surface is *complete* and *integrated* enough that an external consumer like re-frame-pair (with no 10x dependency) can do its job.

Specific surface re-frame-pair needs:

1. **Trace buffer.** Last N trace events retained in-runtime, queryable. Today 10x keeps this; should be a runtime primitive in re-frame2 (in-tree, dev-only, configurable depth).
2. **Epoch history.** Per-frame snapshots at drain-completion boundaries. Specced in Tool-Pair §Epoch history; verify the implementation surface is complete.
3. **Source-coord query.** `(handler-meta :event :foo)` returns `{:ns :line :file :doc :source?}`. Already specced; verify surface.
4. **Public registrar query API.** `(handlers :event)`, `(machines)`, `(machine-meta)`. Already specced.
5. **Frame metadata introspection.** `(frame-ids)`, `(frame-meta frame-id)`. Already specced.
6. **Snapshot snapshot.** `(snapshot-of frame-id)` or `(get-frame-db frame-id)`. Already specced.
7. **Sub-cache introspection.** `(sub-cache frame-id)` for understanding what's currently subscribed. Already specced (CLJS-only).
8. **Hot-swap interface.** Re-register handlers at runtime; pair uses this for AI-driven exploration. Already specced via standard reg-* API.
9. **fx-stub mechanism.** Per-call override of fx for tests/AI experimentation. Already specced via `:fx-overrides` in dispatch opts.

## What needs verification (action items)

1. **Audit Tool-Pair.md against current spec.** Is every item Tool-Pair claims as v1-required actually specified in the per-feature specs? Cross-check.
2. **Audit re-frame-pair's actual usage of 10x.** Identify every place pair imports from 10x's namespaces; verify each has a re-frame2 in-tree equivalent.
3. **Trace buffer:** confirm the spec mandates a built-in trace buffer in dev builds. 009 §Subscription/consumption documents register-trace-cb / remove-trace-cb but doesn't explicitly require a built-in retain-N-events buffer. Add this if missing.
4. **Epoch history config:** verify `(rf/configure :epoch-history {:depth N})` is in API.md and is the canonical configuration surface for pair-style consumers.
5. **Production elision:** confirm the entire pair-required surface is dev-only — no production-build cost. Tool-Pair already states this; verify.
6. **Document the consumption pattern.** A short §'How AI tools attach' note in Tool-Pair.md or a new Companion doc that says: 'consumers register as trace listeners + query the registrar + read epoch history; that's the entire surface; no 10x dependency required.'

## Knock-on for re-frame-10x

When this lands, re-frame-10x v2 can be rewritten as:
- A registered trace listener.
- A renderer of the in-tree epoch history.
- A query consumer of the registrar.
- A UI on top — *not* a dependency.

That decouples 10x from pair entirely. They can share the substrate without one depending on the other.

## Pairs with

- Tool-Pair.md (the spec for pair-shaped tools — defines the contract).
- 009-Instrumentation.md (trace surface — needs to host the trace buffer in-tree).
- rf2-ps4 (re-frame-pair-improver skill, deferred — same family of tooling).
- rf2-13lo (Implementor Checklist — the pair-required surface should be in the Tool-Pair section of the Part 2 checklist).
- rf2-edi (006 revertibility audit — adapter shouldn't hold tool-relevant state outside the frame value, otherwise pair sees inconsistent state).

## Why P2

Real product impact: re-frame-pair's current 10x dependency is a friction point for users who want pair-style AI assistance without a 10x panel. Making re-frame2 self-sufficient for pair-shape tools removes the friction and enables the multi-impl story (re-frame-js, re-frame-py would each ship their own pair-required surface, not depend on a 10x-equivalent existing).

Action when the spec is closer to lock and the per-feature specs (009, Tool-Pair) have stabilised. Until then, this bead serves as the consolidating reminder that pair-required infra is a *framework concern*, not a 10x concern.

Status: open. P2 — affects the multi-impl story and the pair tooling experience.
close_reason: Audited pair-required surface against current spec. 8 of 9 items already specified (epoch history, source coords, registrar query API, frame metadata, snapshot-of, sub-cache, hot-swap, fx-stub). Gap: 009 explicitly disclaimed a long-term trace buffer ('tools maintain their own'). Added normative §Retain-N trace ring buffer (dev-only) to 009 specifying (rf/trace-buffer), (rf/clear-trace-buffer!), (rf/configure :trace-buffer {:depth N}); default 200 events; production-elided. Added matching API.md rows. Added §How AI tools attach to Tool-Pair.md documenting the consumption pattern: trace listener + trace buffer + epoch history + registrar query — no 10x dependency. Pair-required infra is now wholly in re-frame2's tree.

title: First-class navigation blocking — pending-nav protocol
description:
Real product need: unsaved forms, interrupted checkouts, destructive multi-step workflows. Angular, Vue Router, and TanStack Router all support blocking or confirming navigation. Current 012-Routing.md spec answer is 'use interceptors' — workable but too implicit for such a common case.

Make navigation blocking a first-class **named event/state protocol** instead of a magic component hook. This fits re-frame2 perfectly: pending-nav lives in app-db; UI renders confirm dialogs from ordinary subscriptions; user choices are dispatched normal events. All testable.

## Concrete benefit

- Real and common product need is met without re-frame2 forcing users to invent the pattern.
- Testable end-to-end via dispatched events; no special test machinery needed.
- Aligns with re-frame2 ethos: explicit state, named events, no implicit hooks.

## Mechanism

A standard pending-navigation state and three named events:

  [:route/navigation-blocked pending-nav]    ;; emitted by runtime when leave-guard rejects
  [:route/continue pending-nav-id]            ;; user dispatches to proceed
  [:route/cancel   pending-nav-id]            ;; user dispatches to abort

Route metadata can declare leave guards:

  (rf/reg-route :editor/article
    {:can-leave [:editor/can-leave?]              ;; sub-id; returns boolean
     :path "/editor/articles/:id"
     ...})

Or a frame-level policy can hook :rf/url-requested.

## Default flow

1. :rf/url-requested fires with the new URL.
2. Runtime evaluates leave guards for the *current* route (subscribes to :can-leave sub or runs a registered guard fn).
3. If clear → proceed normally (the new URL becomes active).
4. If blocked → store the intended navigation in app-db under :rf/pending-navigation; emit :route/navigation-blocked.
5. UI renders a confirmation dialog from (subscribe [:rf/pending-navigation]).
6. User chooses: dispatch [:route/continue id] or [:route/cancel id].
7. Runtime applies the choice — proceeds or stays.

## Pending-nav slot in app-db

  {:rf/pending-navigation
   {:id "pn-7"
    :requested-by-event [:rf/url-requested ...]
    :requested-url "/editor/articles/42"
    :reason "Form has unsaved changes"
    :rejecting-route :editor/article
    :rejecting-guard :editor/can-leave?}}

Cleared on :route/continue (with the navigation completing) or :route/cancel (without).

## Files to update when actioning

- 012-Routing.md: new §'Navigation blocking — pending-nav protocol'; document the three events, the slot shape, the leave-guard mechanism.
- Spec-Schemas.md: add :rf/pending-navigation schema; extend :rf/route-metadata with :can-leave field.
- conformance/fixtures/: new fixture route-navigation-blocked.edn — register a route with :can-leave; trigger nav while guard returns false; verify :rf/pending-navigation is set; verify URL did not change; dispatch :route/continue; verify URL changed.
- API.md: list the three new events under standard route events; list :can-leave route-metadata key.

## Why this shape (not a hook-based router)

The hook-based version (e.g., React Router useBlocker) is convenient but tied to component lifecycle. Re-frame2 strengths are explicit state and dispatched events; this design preserves them. Slightly more verbose at the call site; far more testable.

## Why P2 (not P1)

Feature completeness, not correctness. Apps without nav-blocking work; apps that need it currently have to invent the pattern via interceptors. Worth shipping a standard answer.

## Pairs with

- rf2-u54v (P1, nav epoch) — navigation blocking happens *before* the new nav-token is allocated; tokens are for committed navigations.
- 012-Routing existing route-metadata structure.

Status: open. Design locked; ready to action. Estimated effort: medium focused agent pass.
close_reason: Added 012-Routing.md §Navigation blocking — pending-nav protocol; :rf/pending-navigation slot + schema; :can-leave route metadata; three named events :route/navigation-blocked / :route/continue / :route/cancel; trace event :route/navigation-blocked; conformance fixture route-navigation-blocked.edn.

title: Fragment support in route slice — bring #fragment into the routing contract
description:
The current 012-Routing.md spec leaves #fragment outside the routing-level contract; views are told to read window.location.hash directly. This pushes browser-ambient state back into view code, which fights the rest of the design (everything else is explicit data flowing through events).

## Concrete benefit

- Anchor navigation becomes SSR-safe and testable.
- No direct browser read needed in view code.
- Scroll behavior integrates with fragments cleanly.
- Docs pages, settings tabs, anchored sections become portable across hosts.

## Mechanism

Extend the route slice with :fragment:

  {:id       :route/docs
   :params   {:page "routing"}
   :query    {}
   :fragment "scroll-restoration"}

Extend the helpers:
- (rf/match-url url) includes :fragment in its return.
- (rf/route-url route-id path-params query-params fragment?) emits the URL with #fragment.
- :route/navigate accepts {:fragment "section-2"} in opts or in target maps.

Update :nav/scroll semantics:
- If a fragment is present and the strategy is compatible (default :top), attempt element scroll first.
- Otherwise fall back to existing strategies (:top / :restore / :preserve).

## Subtlety: fragment-only changes do NOT re-fire :on-match

Same route + same params + different fragment → the route slice updates :fragment and emits :rf/url-changed, but :on-match (the loaded-data dispatch) is NOT re-fired. Loaded data is unchanged when only the fragment changed.

Document this rule explicitly: :on-match fires when path or query changes; fragment-only changes update the slice and emit :rf/url-changed for views to react.

## Files to update when actioning

- 012-Routing.md: §Route slice — add :fragment field; §match-url / route-url — extend signatures; §:nav/scroll — fragment-aware behaviour; new §'Fragment-only navigation' callout for the :on-match exception rule.
- Spec-Schemas.md: extend :rf/route-slice with :fragment (string-or-nil).
- conformance/fixtures/: new fixture route-fragment-change.edn — navigate to URL with fragment; verify slice contains fragment; navigate to same path with different fragment; verify :on-match does NOT re-fire; verify :rf/url-changed does fire.
- 011-SSR.md: confirm SSR parses fragment from request URL when applicable (mostly relevant for static-site generators with anchored navigation).

## Why P2 (not P1)

Polish more than correctness. Nothing is broken without fragment support — views can read window.location.hash and live. But it pushes a class of state out of the deterministic, testable, explicit data flow that the rest of the spec maintains. Worth fixing.

## Pairs with

- rf2-61r0 (P1, route ranking) — fragments are matched after route resolution; ranking applies to path/query, not fragment.
- 012-Routing existing scroll-restoration spec.

Status: open. Design locked; ready to action. Estimated effort: small focused agent pass.
close_reason: Added 012-Routing.md §Fragments; :fragment in :rf/route-slice; match-url/route-url extended to carry fragment; :route/fragment sub; fragment-only changes do NOT re-fire :on-match (emit :route/url-changed trace); 011-SSR.md §Fragments under SSR; conformance fixture route-fragment-change.edn.

title: Delayed :after transitions — formalisation
description:
:after is a delayed transition — fires after a specified time delay, no event needed. xstate/SCXML term: delayed transition.

## The pattern

A state with :after declares a map of milliseconds → transition specs. Each entry runs an independent timer; on expiry, the corresponding transition fires (subject to guards). Exiting the state advances an epoch counter; in-flight timers compare epochs on receipt and ignore stale firings.

```
{:loading {:after {5000  :timeout
                   30000 :hard-error}
           :on    {:loaded :ready
                   :failed :error}}}
```

## Locked design calls

1. **Cancellation: epoch-based stale-detection.** No new fx primitive (no :cancel-dispatch-later). Each entry increments an epoch counter in the machines :data; the scheduled :dispatch-later carries the epoch at scheduling time. On receipt, machine handler compares; mismatch → silently ignored, emits :machine.timer/stale-after trace.

2. **SSR behavior: :after no-ops.** SSR mode disables timer scheduling at the entry action; in-flight timers are not created. Document the rule: :after is a client-side affordance; SSR renders the static current state without timer effects.

3. **Clock abstraction: lives in the existing re-frame.interop layer.** re-frame already has a clj/cljs-split interop layer (preserved in re-frame2 per project memory). The clock primitive is a natural addition there:

  - re-frame.interop.now-ms (host-clock current time)
  - re-frame.interop.schedule-after! (host-clock setTimeout-equivalent)
  - re-frame.interop.cancel-scheduled! (best-effort cancellation, optional)

  Tests swap the interop layer using existing test fixtures; no new clock-configuration API is needed at the framework level.

4. **Value shape: admit transition-specs and fn-valued delays.**
  - Keyword-target sugar: `{5000 :timeout}` → `{5000 {:target :timeout}}`.
  - Full transition spec: `{5000 {:guard :still-loading? :target :timeout :action :log-timeout}}`.
  - Fn-valued delays: `{(fn [snap] (* 1000 (:retry-count data))) :retry}` for parameterised timing.

5. **Trace events:** :machine.timer/scheduled, :machine.timer/fired, :machine.timer/stale-after.

6. **Hierarchy interaction:** :after on a parent state remains active while in any child. Multiple :after timers can be in flight simultaneously across hierarchy levels. Exiting a level advances that levels epoch; pending timers from that level go stale.

7. **Multiple :after per state:** all timers run independently; whichever fires first (and matches its guard) triggers its transition. The transitions exit advances the epoch; other in-flight timers go stale.

## Drain semantics interaction

:after fires through the standard event pipeline (it dispatches a future event via :dispatch-later). When the event lands, drain semantics applies normally:
  - Per Level 3, the machine handler receives the synthetic ::after-elapsed event.
  - Epoch check happens before transition lookup.
  - On match: transition fires per Level 2 ordering (exit / action / entry cascade).
  - :raise queue and :always check happen as normal after the transition.

No new microstep loop. :after is just a deferred event source.

## What :after does NOT include

- Recurring timers — :after fires once per state entry. For polling, the user re-enters the state (e.g., :fetching → :waiting → :fetching).
- Wall-clock delays — :after is relative-to-entry, not "fire at 9:00 AM tomorrow."
- Pause / resume — no built-in pause; users pause by transitioning out.

## Schema additions

:rf/state-node gains optional :after slot:
```
[:after {:optional true}
 [:map-of
  [:or pos-int? fn?]              ;; ms or (fn [snap] ms)
  [:or :keyword                    ;; sugar: target keyword
       [:map                       ;; full transition spec
        [:guard  {:optional true} :rf/guard-ref]
        [:target {:optional true} :rf/transition-target]
        [:action {:optional true} :rf/action-ref]
        [:meta   {:optional true} :map]]]]]
```

## Conformance fixtures (minimum 3)

1. after-single-delay.edn — simple delay; verify timer fires; transition occurs after configured ms.
2. after-stale-detection.edn — real event arrives first; timer fires; epoch mismatch; ignored with :machine.timer/stale-after trace.
3. after-hierarchy.edn — :after on parent state; entering child doesnt cancel; timer remains active across child transitions; exits parent → epoch advances → timer stale.

All three use the test-clock pattern (advance time deterministically via re-frame.interop test fixtures).

## Files to update when actioning

- 005-StateMachines.md: new §"Delayed :after transitions" near §Eventless :always; update §Capability matrix — flip :after from work-in-progress to specified; update §Drain semantics gotchas with new gotcha (most common: forgetting epoch increment on entry).
- 002-Frames.md (or re-frame.interop section): document the clock primitives.
- 011-SSR.md: document :after no-op rule under SSR.
- Spec-Schemas.md: add :after slot to :rf/state-node per above; new error category :rf.warning/no-clock-configured (warning, not error); new trace events.
- conformance/fixtures/: add the 3 new fixtures; ensure test-clock fixture pattern is established.
- conformance/README.md: list new fixtures.

## Pairs with

- rf2-a83 (Spec-Schemas additions for v1 WIP capabilities — :after schema closes the last piece).
- rf2-3tk (closed; hierarchical states — :after interacts with hierarchical entry/exit).
- rf2-9ln (closed; :always — sibling delayed/eventless transition; same drain interaction).

Status: open. Design is locked; ready to action via a focused agent pass like :always.
close_reason: Delayed :after specified: epoch-based stale detection, SSR no-op, clock primitives in re-frame.interop, transition-spec value shape with fn-valued delays admitted, three trace events, three conformance fixtures, no-clock warning category. Substantive content in 005 §Delayed :after transitions.
