title: re-frame-pair2: streaming subscription channel for trace + epoch events
description:
Today watch-epochs polls (the 'pull-mode only' state per pair2's STATUS.md). Streaming via nREPL :out (or sidecar WebSocket) lets Claude observe events as they fire, no polling.

## Scope

- pair2 runtime registers a trace-cb / epoch-cb that pushes events to a stream.
- MCP server receives the stream and surfaces events to Claude as notifications (or as readable lines from a long-running tool call).
- Claude consumes the stream via Monitor-tool-shaped reads (one event per line).
- Replaces the current polling loop in watch-epochs.sh.

## Disposition

P2-P3 — the most architecturally involved of the four sub-beads. Composes with rf2-5b8e (MCP holds the stream) but is the largest piece individually. Blocked-by rf2-5b8e + ideally also rf2-???-snapshot (so streamed events join cleanly with snapshot reads of state).

title: Cross-Spec Interactions could reconcile its status vocabulary with the entries it now contains
description:
Suggestion to consider:

`spec/Cross-Spec-Interactions.md` defines a two-state status model (`Pinned` and `Provisional`), but the document now contains a third status marker (`Locked`) without explaining it.

Evidence:
- `spec/Cross-Spec-Interactions.md:18-29` says each interaction's status is either `Pinned` or `Provisional`, and the status legend only defines those two markers.
- `spec/Cross-Spec-Interactions.md:223` marks interaction 21 as `Locked`.
- `spec/Cross-Spec-Interactions.md:29` also describes the corpus state as "The remaining 13 stay Provisional," which only makes sense if the `Locked` entry is treated as outside the documented model.

Why it matters:
This is a small but visible clarity leak in one of the corpus's orientation docs. A reader cannot tell whether `Locked` means "normatively settled but not fixture-backed," "not a cross-spec interaction in the same sense," or simply a doc-only note.

Suggested improvement:
- Either add `Locked` to the status legend with a precise meaning, or restate interaction 21 using the existing vocabulary.
- If interaction 21 is intentionally a family-note rather than a fixture-trackable interaction, consider moving it to a short appendix so the main status model stays clean.

title: Refresh spec index and AI audit scaffolding after Spec 014 landed
description:
Suggestion to consider:

The corpus-navigation docs still describe the numbered spec set as if it ends at 013, and the AI audit still scopes itself to the pre-014 corpus. That makes the spec's own scaffolding look older than the normative set it is supposed to help readers navigate.

Evidence:
- `spec/README.md:12` says the contract is defined by numbered Specs `(000–013)` even though the same file now lists `014-HTTPRequests.md` as a capability spec.
- `spec/README.md:23` says "The numbering 000–013 has one gap" while the actual numbered set in the directory is 000–014 with 003 reserved.
- `spec/AI-Audit.md:4` scopes the audit to numbered Specs `(000–013)`.
- `spec/AI-Audit.md:9-12` and `spec/AI-Audit.md:51+` omit a stand-alone scoring pass for Spec 014, and the audit's per-Spec coverage is also still selective in a way that no longer matches the current corpus shape.

Why it matters:
These docs are the reader's orientation layer. When they lag the live spec set, it raises the question of whether 014 and the newer companion material have actually been integrated into the corpus-thinking or merely appended.

Suggested improvement:
- Sweep the index/audit scaffolding docs so they describe the live numbered set consistently.
- Either add Spec 014 to the AI audit properly, or explicitly mark the audit as historical / partial until a re-audit lands.
- If selective per-Spec scoring remains intentional, state that policy plainly rather than implying whole-corpus coverage.

title: Independent research + refinement of re-frame-2-story SOTA features (compares vs rf2-m6tu)
description:
Follow-up to rf2-m6tu. Different framing: 'what features would make re-frame-2-story state-of-the-art?' rather than 'what should it minimally support?'

Two-phase work for one agent:

## Phase 1 — Independent research (no peeking at rf2-m6tu)

Agent does its own survey of component-development tools focused on the SOTA question:
- What's the highest-bar capability set? Not 'minimum viable.'
- Which tools (Storybook + non-Storybook) ship features that feel best-in-class?
- Cross-tool: what's been borrowed, refined, surpassed?
- Storybook addons (https://storybook.js.org/addons) — which are state-of-the-art? a11y? play / interactions / test? composition? designs? viewport with breakpoints? args + controls type-generation?
- Beyond Storybook: Histoire's vue-specific magic, Ladle's perf claims, React Cosmos's fixture model, Bit's component-as-package, Lookbook's Rails-flavoured ergonomics.
- The answer should reach for SOTA, not the average.

Agent writes its independent answer to a scratch file: findings/re-frame-2-story-sota-draft.md (or holds in agent memory).

## Phase 2 — Comparison + refinement

After own answer is written:
- Read findings/re-frame-2-story-feature-set.md (the rf2-m6tu output).
- Compare:
  - Where do they agree? (Confidence: both reached the same conclusion independently.)
  - Where does rf2-m6tu under-claim vs SOTA? (Add to refined doc.)
  - Where does rf2-m6tu over-claim vs realistic? (Question.)
  - Where does the independent SOTA framing surface features rf2-m6tu missed entirely?
- Refine the rf2-m6tu document in place: findings/re-frame-2-story-feature-set.md gets the synthesis.
- Drop the scratch draft after merging if used.

The refined doc should:
- Carry forward rf2-m6tu's organisation (don't restructure for its own sake).
- Add SOTA features the original missed.
- Surface tensions where SOTA-pursuit conflicts with re-frame2's ethos (e.g. 'Storybook composition is SOTA but conflicts with our spec-first / single-source-of-truth ethos — recommend skip').
- Preserve attribution: 'Refined 2026-MM-DD against independent SOTA research; the following sections were strengthened: ...'

## Why two-phase

Pre-loading the agent with rf2-m6tu's findings biases the research. Mike wants the independent eyes first — different agent, different framing prompt, no shared context — to surface SOTA features the original missed.

Blocked-by rf2-m6tu (can't compare to a doc that doesn't exist yet).

title: Research Storybook + JS-world component-dev tools to design re-frame-2-story artefact feature set
description:
Research-only. Survey Storybook, Histoire, Ladle, React Cosmos, Lookbook, devcards, Day8's workspaces (re-frame-7), Pattern Lab, etc. Identify the most-appreciated advanced features across them. Produce a feature spec for a new day8/re-frame-2-story artefact that adopts best practice + the most valued advanced features, while honouring re-frame2's ethos (spec-first / AI-implementable / frame-as-actor-system-boundary / trace-as-first-class-output / Tool-Pair integration / multi-substrate / production-elision / strict additive contract).

Output: findings/re-frame-2-story-feature-set.md.

Note: spec/007-Stories.md already exists — read first for context. May already define the surface partially; this research extends with cross-tool best-practice.

title: Guide ch.04 could narrow its scope to views and frames, then spin supporting topics out
description:
Suggestion to consider:

Chapter 04 has a strong core subject, but it currently carries several adjacent topics that dilute the through-line.

Evidence:
- docs/guide/04-views-and-frames.md:87-93 includes a significant implementation caveat about frame propagation.
- docs/guide/04-views-and-frames.md:206-223 introduces source-coordinate DOM annotations.
- docs/guide/04-views-and-frames.md:225-245 shifts into general app-db shape guidance.
- docs/guide/04-views-and-frames.md:247-267 introduces flows.
- docs/guide/04-views-and-frames.md:269-273 previews routing in detail even though routing has its own dedicated chapter 12.

Why it matters:
A chapter named "Views and frames" sets an expectation of a tight narrative around rendering, registration, frame scoping, and multi-frame mental models. The later sections broaden into app architecture and feature-specific topics, which makes the chapter feel heavier than its title promises.

Suggested improvement:
- Keep the chapter tightly focused on view registration, frame creation, frame targeting, and frame-provider composition.
- Move or shorten the app-db, flows, and routing preview sections unless they are directly needed for the chapter's example.
- If those topics stay, present them as short forward pointers so the main narrative remains about views and frames.

title: Guide ch.02 could defer substrate/package detail until after the first runnable example
description:
Suggestion to consider:

Chapter 02 is supposed to be the first practical walkthrough, but it front-loads adapter selection and Maven artefact detail before the reader sees the counter itself.

Evidence:
- docs/guide/02-your-first-app.md:7-38 spends the opening section on substrate choice, package coordinates, and the feature-opt-in artefact story.
- The actual app goal does not appear until docs/guide/02-your-first-app.md:40.
- The first complete code sample does not appear until docs/guide/02-your-first-app.md:46.
- The chapter already has a later callout for real-world mount wiring at docs/guide/02-your-first-app.md:216-230, which suggests some of the packaging/adapter context can also be handled as a focused sidebar rather than as the opening gate.

Why it matters:
The current structure increases cognitive load before the reader has any concrete picture of the re-frame2 loop. For a first-app chapter, the golden path is easier to learn if the first thing the reader sees is a runnable, single-substrate example.

Suggested improvement:
- Start with one canonical stack (Reagent) and get to the counter sooner.
- Move UIx/Helix and artefact-selection detail into a shorter sidebar, appendix, or end-of-chapter "same pattern on other adapters" section.
- Keep the explicit init!/adapter notes, but treat them as support for the example rather than the chapter's front door.

title: Guide README could separate the core learning path from optional deep dives
description:
Suggestion to consider:

The guide currently presents one flat chapter sequence in docs/guide/README.md, but the later chapters mix different reader intents: migration (08), optional long-form philosophy (09), practical testing/devtools material (10-11), and routing (12).

Evidence:
- docs/guide/README.md:20-24 lists chapters 08-12 as one continuous path.
- docs/guide/README.md:28 already hints at alternate behaviour ("If you're impatient, read 01 and skip to 02").
- docs/guide/09-the-dynamic-model.md:6 explicitly says chapter 09 is skippable for readers who just want to write code.
- docs/guide/10-testing.md:425-428 and docs/guide/11-devtools-and-pair-tools.md:433-437 continue the linear "Next" chain even though those chapters are better treated as practical reference material for many readers.

Why it matters:
New readers do not get a clearly signposted "core path" through the guide. The current order makes the practical onboarding story compete with optional essays and later reference material.

Suggested improvement:
- Reframe README around 2-3 tracks, for example: core onboarding, migration/reference, and deeper system/philosophy.
- Update chapter "Next" sections to support those tracks instead of implying every reader should proceed linearly through 08 -> 09 -> 10 -> 11 -> 12.
- At minimum, add explicit jump guidance for readers who need routing/testing before they need the long-form essay.

title: Remove stale implementation/substrates README or relocate it under adapters
description:
Why this issue exists:
The worktree currently contains an untracked implementation/substrates/README.md even though the implementation has already standardized on implementation/adapters/ as the canonical location and terminology. Keeping a substrates/ doc around reintroduces the old name, duplicates the packaging explanation already captured in implementation/README.md, and its relative links (reagent/, uix/, helix/) are broken from the implementation/substrates/ directory because those subdirectories do not exist there.

What needs to be done:
- Decide whether this content should live at implementation/adapters/README.md or be dropped entirely in favor of the existing top-level implementation README.
- If it is kept, move it to the canonical directory and fix its relative links.
- Remove the stale substrates/ path so the repo does not present both names for the same concept.

Notes:
This is documentation/package-structure drift rather than runtime behavior, but it directly affects discoverability and makes the implementation layout look less settled than it actually is.
close_reason: Resolved via PR #187. README relocated to implementation/adapters/README.md.

title: Rewrite findings/boot-as-statemachine-dash8-rf8.md after spec 005 gap beads complete
description: When the spec 005 gap beads filed from this doc all complete, rewrite findings/boot-as-statemachine-dash8-rf8.md to: (a) reflect the now-canonical spec 005 idiom (use :invoke-all per rf2-6vmw, use :timeout-ms per rf2-1lop, use the cancellation cascade per rf2-wvkn, optionally invoke :rf.http/managed as a machine per rf2-ijm7); (b) drop the 'spec 005 gaps' / mismatch sections — those gaps are closed; (c) keep the Dash8 + rf8 boot diagrams + auth-machine sketch as the canonical worked example showing best-practice idiom; (d) cross-link the canonical retry-ownership pattern (rf2-zg4g decision (c) hybrid). Result: a rewritten doc that's pure pedagogical guide to boot-as-state-machine, not a gap-analysis. Blocked-by rf2-6vmw (just merged via PR #185), rf2-wvkn, rf2-1lop, rf2-ijm7 — when all complete, dispatch the rewrite. rf2-zg4g already closed.

title: Document hybrid retry-ownership: managed-HTTP for transport, state machines for semantic (resolves rf2-zg4g)
description: Per Mike's decision (rf2-zg4g, 2026-05-10): :rf.http/managed owns transport-level retry; state machines own semantic retry. Document the teachable boundary in canonical patterns. Spec edits: spec/014-HTTPRequests.md §Retry — clarify :retry is for transport-level only (function-of-attempt-count policies); explicit guidance to escape to state-machine retry for response-conditional / state-conditional retry. spec/Pattern-Boot.md (or relevant Pattern doc) — show the auth-machine pattern from findings/boot-as-statemachine-dash8-rf8.md as the canonical 'semantic retry via machine' example (5xx-retry-with-backoff, 401-fatal, refresh-vs-init distinction). spec/Pattern-AsyncEffect.md (if exists) — cross-reference the boundary. spec/005 §Cross-spec interactions — cross-link. Disposition: P3. Blocked-by rf2-6y3q (HTTP middleware bead in flight, spec/014 hot zone) and rf2-6vmw (spec/005 hot zone).

title: Spec 014 gap: per-frame request middleware (Bearer auth header, instrumentation, common request transforms)
description: From findings/boot-as-statemachine-dash8-rf8.md L272 — Dash8's :configuring phase fetches with Bearer token via custom XHRIO wrappers. Both Dash8 and rf8 have to manually attach Authorization headers per-request. spec 014 :rf.http/managed has no middleware concept. Direction: add per-frame request middleware chain — (rf/reg-http-middleware frame-id name (fn [req] (assoc-in req [:headers :authorization] ...))). Each middleware fn transforms the request map before dispatch; fires in registration order. Useful for: Bearer auth (Dash8/rf8 both need this), CSRF token, request-correlation-id, instrumentation/tracing, base-URL rewriting in dev. Concrete shape options: (a) fn-list per frame; (b) interceptor-chain matching re-frame2 event-handler shape (composes with what authors already know); (c) :rf.http/middleware reg-fx that wraps requests structurally. (b) most likely natural fit. Spec 014 §Middleware new section. Disposition: P3 — non-gating but Day8 apps demonstrate it's load-bearing.

title: Spec 014 gap: :rf.http/managed not invokable as a child machine (machine-shape surface for :invoke integration)
description: From findings/boot-as-statemachine-dash8-rf8.md auth-machine sketch L312: the proposed boot pattern shows machines invoking :rf.http/managed AS A CHILD MACHINE — '{:invoke {:machine-id :rf.http/managed :data ...}}' — and observing :succeeded / :failed terminal events. Today :rf.http/managed is an fx, not a machine. To use it from a state machine you must (a) emit the fx as part of an :entry action, (b) route :on-success / :on-failure events back to the machine, (c) manually thread request correlation. Verbose and indirect. The findings sketch shows the cleaner shape: :rf.http/managed registered as a machine whose terminal states are :succeeded / :failed (with response data attached); machines compose via :invoke. Direction set if we want machines as the canonical async-orchestration shape: register a thin machine-flavoured wrapper around :rf.http/managed exposing :requesting → :succeeded / :failed transitions plus the response payload as data on terminal entry. Spec 014 + spec 005 cross-spec interaction; spec/Conventions §Per-feature substrate clearly. Disposition: P3. Boot-as-state-machine is the flagship pattern; not gating beta but landed cleanly here, machines-as-orchestration becomes the canonical async-effect shape.

title: Decision: should the boot machine OWN per-bucket retry, or delegate to :rf.http/managed?
description: From findings/boot-as-statemachine-dash8-rf8.md top-open-question. Dash8 auth has business logic (5xx-only retry, 401-fatal, refresh-vs-init distinction) that fits a custom state machine. Non-auth fetches fit :rf.http/managed :retry cleanly. Mike must rule on canonical guidance for spec 005 + spec 014. Options: (a) Always delegate to :rf.http/managed — one source of truth for retry; (b) Always state-machine — :rf.http/managed dumb, machine wraps with retry transitions; (c) Hybrid — managed-HTTP for transport-level retry (network errors, 5xx), state machines for semantic retry (401 fresh-token, business-rule fallbacks). Affects Pattern-AsyncEffect.md, Pattern-Boot.md, every author's mental model, plus the M2 (HTTP cancellation) bead's framing. Disposition: P3 design call; not gating beta but shapes canonical patterns going forward.
notes:
---

## Decision (Mike, 2026-05-10): Option (c) — Hybrid

`:rf.http/managed` owns transport-level retry. State machines own semantic retry.

The teachable boundary: if retry policy is a **function of attempt count** (5xx → retry up to N times with backoff), it's managed-HTTP. If it's a **function of prior responses + business state** (401 → refresh token → re-init; multi-tenant fallback URL chain; etc.), it's a state machine.

## Implementation

Tracked under separate impl bead — see linked.
close_reason: Decision (c) Hybrid recorded in notes; impl tracked under separate bead.

title: Interactive playground / tutorial for re-frame2 (inspired by Svelte's tutorial) — viable with self-hosted ClojureScript?
description:
Mike (2026-05-10): Svelte's tutorial at https://svelte.dev/tutorial is a brilliant interactive learning experience — edit code on the left, see live output on the right, compiled in the browser. Would love to create something like that for re-frame2.

## The question

Is self-hosted ClojureScript complete enough to power this?

## Background — what's needed

A Svelte-tutorial-like experience needs:
- In-browser code editing (Monaco / CodeMirror).
- In-browser compilation of user-edited code.
- Live execution into a sandboxed re-frame2 runtime.
- Tutorial step navigation with progressive concept reveal.

## Options — evaluate

### A. Self-hosted ClojureScript (cljs.js bootstrap)

CLJS can compile itself in-browser via cljs.js. Used by Klipse, Lambda Island, etc.

Open questions to research:
- Does Reagent work under self-hosted CLJS today? (Klipse demos suggest yes.)
- Does re-frame2's macro layer (reg-event-fx, reg-machine, reg-view, source-coord stamping) survive self-hosted bootstrap?
- Bundle size of cljs.js + Reagent + re-frame2 — is it acceptable for a tutorial site? (Likely 1-3MB minified.)
- First-compile latency — typical 'hello world' compile time in self-hosted CLJS is sub-second; complex deps slower.
- Macro packaging — re-frame2's macros need to ship as analyser metadata (cljs.analyzer) for self-hosted to expand them.
- Does the macro-rich code re-frame2 emits (reg-machine source-coord stamping, reg-view source-coord stamping) introspect okay under self-hosted?

### B. Klipse

Pre-built self-hosted CLJS playground. Mature. Used by lambdaisland.com and others. Could be embedded.

Open questions:
- Does Klipse handle re-frame2's adapter init (rf/init! adapter)?
- Per-step state isolation — Klipse cells share global state by default; may need adaptation.

### C. Pre-compiled snippets per step

Each tutorial step is a separately-compiled bundle. User clicks 'Next', the right-pane swaps to the next pre-compiled bundle. No in-browser compilation.

Pros: no self-hosted CLJS dep; bundle-per-step is small.
Cons: user can't edit code, defeating the Svelte-tutorial inspiration.

### D. Hybrid — pre-compiled lessons + Klipse for the user-edits-code part

Lesson narrative + hard-coded examples are pre-compiled; the 'try it' boxes are Klipse-powered. Best of both.

### E. Squint (CLJS-flavour to native JS)

https://github.com/squint-cljs/squint — fast in-browser compile. But: not full ClojureScript (subset). Macros work differently. Re-frame2's macro-heavy registration would likely need adaptation. Worth surveying.

## Likely answer

Self-hosted CLJS is viable but slow + large for a polished tutorial. A hybrid (lessons pre-compiled, Klipse for try-it boxes) is the pragmatic shape. Worth a research pass to confirm Reagent + re-frame2's macros work under self-hosted before investing.

## Disposition

P3 — post-beta. Outreach / DX investment, not pre-1.0 blocker. But the answer to 'self-hosted CLJS works?' affects what tooling we can ship in the guide chapters too (interactive snippets in docs/guide/).

## Recommended next step

A small spike: build one tutorial step ('your first counter') using self-hosted CLJS or Klipse, see if it works end-to-end. ~1 day of investigation. File as a separate bead when ready to spike.
notes:
---

## Svelte tutorial repo structure (reference, 2026-05-10)

https://github.com/sveltejs/svelte.dev/tree/main/apps/svelte.dev/content/tutorial

Three-level hierarchy: Course → Section → Step.

```
content/tutorial/
├── 01-svelte/                          ← Course
│   ├── 01-introduction/                ← Section
│   │   ├── 01-welcome-to-svelte/       ← Step
│   │   │   ├── +assets/                ← Starter code (right-pane editor initial content)
│   │   │   └── index.md                ← Lesson narrative (left-pane prose)
│   │   ├── 02-your-first-component/
│   │   └── ...
│   ├── 02-reactivity/
│   ├── ...
├── 02-advanced-svelte/
├── 03-sveltekit/
└── 04-advanced-sveltekit/
```

Each step's `+assets/` is the starting point; the user edits live; comparing against a "completed" version per step is implicit (next step's +assets is the previous step's solution).

For re-frame2, the analogous mapping:

- Course: 01-re-frame2 (basics) / 02-advanced (machines, flows, routing) / 03-tooling (devtools, testing).
- Sections inside 01: introduction, events-and-state, views, subscriptions, http.
- Each step is a self-contained CLJS namespace + (later) re-frame2 setup.

Mike's directive: investigate self-hosted CLJS (cljs.js bootstrap) viability for in-browser compile. Klipse uses this; spike one step end-to-end to confirm Reagent + re-frame2 macros survive. ~1 day.

title: Guide rework after adapter-canonical naming + explicit init! redesign land
description: Follow-up to rf2-m8np guide work + the adapter-canonical naming + explicit-init! redesign. After all three land: revisit chapters 02 / 04 / 05 (and any new chapters rf2-m8np wrote about substrates) to use the new naming and explicit init! contract. Concretely: (a) re-frame.substrate.<name> → re-frame.adapter.<name> in code samples; (b) (rf/init!) walkthroughs to use explicit (rf/init! reagent/adapter) form; (c) drop default-adapter language; (d) prefer 'adapter' for implementations and 'substrate' only when discussing the contract. Blocked-by both renamings.
notes:
rf2-s7fh sweep: guide already aligned with both renames. Commits 3b1d2bc (rf2-agql) + 839319d (rf2-0imy) + dc8924e (rf2-0imy) merged to main before this task ran — they swept docs/guide/ for both renames already.

Verification (run from worktree on origin/main):
- grep 're-frame\.substrate\.(reagent|uix|helix|context)|implementation/substrates/' docs/guide/ -> 0 hits
- grep '(rf/init!)|rf/init! :reagent|rf/init! :uix|rf/init! :helix|register-default-adapter' docs/guide/ -> 1 hit, intentional: docs/guide/02-your-first-app.md:245 documents that (rf/init!) / (rf/init! :reagent) raise :rf.error/no-adapter-specified, the canonical way to teach the new explicit-only contract.
- All rf/init! call sites in 02-your-first-app.md (lines 225/240/251/254/257) use the new explicit form: (rf/init! reagent/adapter), uix/adapter, helix/adapter, ssr/adapter.
- Remaining 'substrate' usages in chapters 02/04/05/07/08/README refer to abstract contracts (view substrate / SSR substrate / routing substrate / state-machine substrate / Spec 006 adapter contract) -- NOT to re-frame.substrate.<name> namespaces. Per task rule 'Substrate stays for the abstract contract', correct.

No code changes; no PR opened; halting per task rule about not expanding scope. Closing as no-op.
close_reason: No-op — guide already aligned. Renamings (rf2-0imy + rf2-agql) included docs/guide/ in their sweeps; verification grep returned 0 stale patterns. See agent report for per-chapter confirmation.

description: rf2-zq55 added reset-frame-db! as the canonical Tool-Pair write surface. Pair2's runtime.cljs:118-138 currently reaches into (handler-meta :frame) :app-db; should be replaced with a call to rf/reset-frame-db!. Pair2 repo: C:/Users/miket/code/re-frame-pair2/. ~5 LoC fix.

title: Implement :rf.warning/plain-fn-under-non-default-frame-once emission in re-frame.views
description: From rf2-o83z PR #166. Spec defines :rf.warning/plain-fn-under-non-default-frame-once: when a plain Reagent fn (not registered via reg-view) renders inside a non-default React-context frame, the resolution chain falls through to :rf/default and a one-shot warning per view-id should fire. Runtime does not yet emit this warning. The browser-test placeholder for this case (cross_spec_cljs_test #10) was halted by rf2-o83z agent — once the runtime emits the warning, the placeholder can become a real browser assertion. Likely surface: re-frame.views or whichever ns owns the view-resolution chain. Pattern: warn-once-per-view-id (mirror rf2-z7f7's source-coord fragment-fallback warn-once contract). P3 — non-default-frame consumers are rare today but the warning is documented in the spec. Direction set: implement the warning per the spec's contract.

title: spec/012-Routing.md fragment-change trace op name uses older :route.url/fragment-changed (3 sites)
description: Discovered during rf2-4o1c.3 work. The agent flagged that spec/012-Routing.md lines 83, 336, 706 still use the older :route.url/fragment-changed despite the bead's evidence list claiming 012 was canonical. Now that 009 has been aligned (PR #162), the corpus is: 5 canonical sites (Spec-Schemas, conformance fixture, trace_test.clj, routing.cljc, MIGRATION) vs 1 outlier (012's 3 sites). Same fix shape as rf2-4o1c.3 — direction is align 012 to canonical :rf.route/url-changed. May touch a code example; agent should preserve teaching value if the example is intentionally pedagogical.
close_reason: Resolved via PR #163 — Spec 012 fragment-change trace op name aligned to canonical :rf.route/url-changed (commit 0533e7c).

title: declares-3-arity? misclassifies CLJS variadic 2-plus-rest fns as 3-arity
description: From rf2-1e0n test work (PR #156). On CLJS, '(fn [data event & rest] ...)' mis-classifies as 3-arity in 'declares-3-arity?' (machines.cljc) — the CLJS check '(or (= 3 (.-length f)) (some? (unchecked-get f "cljs")))' returns true for compiled 2-plus-rest fns because the latter property is set on variadic fns. Result: user's '& rest' receives '(ctx)' rather than being empty. JVM matches the docstring at machines.cljc:88-90 (returns false for 2-plus-rest). Behaviour-vs-docstring divergence. Tests in PR #156 pin actual current behaviour per platform. Fix likely needs a smarter CLJS check that distinguishes a true 3-arity dispatch entry from variadic-with-arity-3-property. Also flagged: live initial-snapshot synthesis (machines.cljc:808) doesn't propagate spec-level ':meta' into the snapshot — minor secondary issue. P3 — not user-facing for the canonical 2-arity guards/actions, but the documented escape hatch behaves differently across platforms.
close_reason: Resolved via PR #159 — declares-3-arity? CLJS check distinguishes variadic-2-plus-rest fns (commits 4a1d42d, db89594).

Reproduces on unrebased PR HEAD (68247f6) AND on origin/main post-merge. Likely fix: add (:require [re-frame.machines]) to implementation/epoch/test/re_frame/epoch_test.clj so the machines late-bind hook publishes before rf/reg-machine is invoked from the test fixture. Equivalent fixes are already in conformance_test.clj per rf2-suue's wiring work. Test-only — runtime is unaffected. P3 — silent failure, not user-facing.

description: From rf2-o423 test coverage audit (G7 sweep bead). Multiple reg-view-related test gaps surfaced together. Three sub-gaps: (1) Auto-inject (rf2-d0pi): Form-1 happy path is covered, but Form-2 boundary behaviour (does Form-2 still get auto-inject under Reagent v2 idioms?) lacks an explicit test asserting the behaviour. (2) Source-coord (rf2-z7f7): the documented 'one-shot warning per id' for Fragment / interop / fn-headed roots has tests covering the attribute-emission path, but no test asserts the warn-fires-only-once contract. (3) SSR/CLJS divergence: the source-coord format across Reagent CLJS path ('format-source-coord' views.cljs:210-224) and SSR JVM path ('format-view-source-coord' ssr.cljc:137-151) was assumed canonical-identical by rf2-7g2q's parser fix but no test exercises both paths against a single sample to confirm they emit byte-identical attribute values. P3 because each is small individually; bundling them as one sweep keeps the bd queue cleaner.

title: Suggestion: review browser-harness coverage gaps for documented cross-spec interactions
description:
Suggestion for further agent review on suitability and possible action.

Review finding:
Several documented cross-spec interactions are still represented by placeholders or partial TODO coverage in the CLJS test suite, especially where a real browser / DOM / timer harness is required. Current examples include the SSR :after no-op end-to-end path, frame-destroy-during-render, plain-function warnings under non-default frames, and fuller server error-projection coverage.

Observed surface:
- implementation/reagent/test/re_frame/cross_spec_cljs_test.cljs contains placeholders / TODO notes pointing at f2-443l.

Why this may merit action:
These are spec-documented behaviours without full CI enforcement in the browser harness, so regressions in those paths may go undetected.

Suggested review questions:
- Which of the remaining placeholder cases are truly in scope for the current browser-test runner?
- Should these be split into smaller issues by interaction, or handled as one harness-completion pass?
- Is f2-443l still the right umbrella, or should the missing cases be tracked more granularly?

Possible action, if the review agrees:
Replace the placeholders with browser-test coverage where feasible, or explicitly narrow the documented contract and track the remaining cases with concrete sub-issues.

title: Conventions reserved-name table inconsistencies: :rf.machine vs :rf/machine sub-id, :rf/url-changed vs :rf.route/url-changed, dangling cross-refs
description:
Audit rf2-smpt findings F1, F3, F15, F22 — bundled because they all describe the same root cause: Conventions' tables and cross-references contain dangling/inconsistent reserved-name entries that have not been swept against their citation graph or against the per-feature specs that own each underlying decision.

Note: F2 (:destroy-machine row), F5 (:rf/pending-navigation row), F6/F11 (error-id grammar), F18 (collision-audit cross-ref) — confirmed merged via PR #141 and skipped per task brief.

F22 — :rf.machine vs :rf/machine sub-id (audit-conventions table line 74): 'Conventions.md §Reserved namespaces table — :rf.machine/* row (line 22): "machine framework subs ([:rf.machine <id>])". Note :rf.machine (no slash, no kw-namespace separator). 005-StateMachines.md:1453 registers the sub as :rf/machine (line 1453 of 005 — (rf/reg-sub :rf/machine ...)). API.md:61 says (rf/sub-machine machine-id) is "Sugar over (subscribe [:rf/machine machine-id])". So the sub-id is :rf/machine (single-segment) not :rf.machine (sub-namespace). Conventions' table cell is inconsistent with 005 and API.md.'

F15 — :rf/url-changed vs :rf.route/url-changed (audit-conventions table line 67): 'These two events are documented in API.md (lines 102-103) as :rf/url-changed / :rf/url-requested, AND :rf.route/handle-url-change. But Conventions's §Reserved namespaces table (:rf.route/* row, line 24) lists :rf.route/url-changed (with the dot). One of these is wrong — either the event is :rf/url-changed (matching the :rf/* "pattern-level event" row) or :rf.route/url-changed (matching the routing sub-namespace row). API.md and the substrate-adapter section say :rf/url-changed; the Reserved-namespace table says :rf.route/url-changed.'

F1 — "Conventions.md §Reading machines" doesn't exist (audit-conventions line 53): '005-StateMachines.md:1446 cites "Conventions.md §Reading machines" — the section does not exist. Dead reference.'

F3 — :story.* / :Workspace.* prefixes claimed reserved but absent from table (audit-conventions line 55): '007-Stories.md:39: "The :story.<...> and :Workspace.<...> prefixes are reserved (see Conventions §Reserved namespaces)". Neither :story.* nor :Workspace.* appears in the reserved-namespace table. The table opens with "re-frame2 reserves one root keyword namespace for framework-owned ids: :rf/*" — :story and :Workspace violate that single-root claim if reserved at all. Either 007's claim is wrong, or Conventions' single-root invariant has two undocumented exceptions.'

Cross-spec inconsistency per audit-criterion #2 + dangling references. Resolution per audit R-S6/R-S7/R-S1/R-M1 — small fixes per item; coordinated sweep recommended.
notes: 2026-05-09 bookkeeping: PR #164 (commit d701079) merged the dangling-references portion only — F1 (Conventions §Reading machines), and the dead-cross-ref entries. Naming-inconsistency portion (F22 :rf.machine vs :rf/machine sub-id, F15 :rf/url-changed vs :rf.route/url-changed, F3 :story.* / :Workspace.* prefix table inconsistency) remains OPEN pending rf2-ljw6 resolution — those are corpus-wide naming decisions that the wider :rf/route vs :route bead will determine. Leaving open.

title: Tool-Pair "complete and self-contained" claim contradicted by missing write surface (pair2 reaches past listed primitives)
description:
Audit rf2-ehpu finding F8.

Quote (audit-tool-pair line 30): 'Tool-Pair line 125: "The runtime contract above is complete and self-contained. A pair-shaped tool … attaches to a running re-frame2 application using only the framework primitives listed below." This is overclaim. Pair2 reaches into (rf/handler-meta :frame frame-id) :app-db to mutate the underlying container ref (runtime.cljs line 136 — app-db-reset!); Tool-Pair lists (rf/get-frame-db ...) as the read API and never specifies a write surface, but the consumer needed one for an evolved-state-shape probe and reached past the listed primitives. Either Tool-Pair commits a write surface (e.g. a documented reset-frame-db! or a listed acceptance that (handler-meta :frame …) exposes a mutable container), or it removes the "complete" claim.'

Pair2 corroboration (audit §5): runtime.cljs line 136 — '(when-let [container (some-> (rf/handler-meta :frame frame-id) :app-db)] (reset! container v))'. Pair2 mutates the underlying container by reaching past get-frame-db into handler-meta :frame and grabbing the :app-db key. Tool-Pair never specifies that (handler-meta :frame …) exposes a mutable container; the consumer guessed.

Audit-criterion #7 (undocumented public surface): an implementation surface real consumers depend on (pair2) but the spec doesn't acknowledge or contract it. If the spec commits this as a contract (it currently doesn't), pair2 is forward-compatible; if not, pair2 will break on the first refactor that moves the container.

Resolution per audit R-L (large): (a) add (rf/reset-frame-db! frame-id new-db) to public Tool-Pair surface — pair2 needs it, story tools need it, conformance harnesses need it — gated on interop/debug-enabled?; or (b) drop the 'complete and self-contained' claim and document the (handler-meta :frame …) :app-db container access as the supported escape hatch with stability commitments.

title: Spec 009 listener-ordering: three statements disagree on whether listener invocation order is contract
description:
Audit rf2-ra02 finding F-4 (also surfaced as rf2-ehpu F5).

Quote (audit-009 line 48): '§Listener ordering carries two incompatible statements. §Resolved decisions §Listener ordering (line 670): "Order is registration order (a swap! assoc on a single atom). Tools must not depend on order." But (a) swap! assoc on a Clojure map does NOT preserve insertion order (a hash-map is unordered) — so the implementation hint is technically incorrect; (b) register-epoch-cb's §Invocation rules line 161 says flatly "Listener ordering is not contract"; (c) Tool-Pair line 163 cites "009 §Listener ordering" as authority for "Listener ordering is not contract." So the field reads "registration-order is the order, but tools must not depend on it, but also it is not contract, but also swap! assoc doesn't actually preserve registration order."'

Cross-spec drift (rf2-ehpu F5 / audit-tool-pair line 27): 009 §Subscription-and-consumption says 'every registered listener is invoked once per emitted trace event, in registration order'; 009 §Listener-invocation-rules says 'In-order'; 009 §Listener ordering says 'tools must not depend on order'. Tool-Pair line 163 cites 009 as authority for 'not contract'.

Internal contradiction per audit-criterion: spec contradicts itself; an AI implementor / tool author would write tests against three different interpretations.

Resolution per audit R-S3: 'Listener ordering is not contract; an implementation MAY iterate in any order; tools MUST NOT depend on order.' Drop the swap! assoc aside. Aligns with Tool-Pair's existing citation.

title: reg-machine is shipped public surface but unmentioned in Spec 005 / CP-5-MachineGuide
description:
Audit rf2-goxn finding #6.

Quote (audit-005 line 21): 'reg-machine is shipped (machines.cljc:885) but unmentioned in 005 / CP-5. A real public surface used internally by spawn-fx; rf2-8bp3 plans to macro-ify for source-coord. Spec only documents reg-event-fx + create-machine-handler.'

Audit-criterion #7 (undocumented public surface): an implementation surface that pair2 / 10x v2 / users may reach for, but the spec doesn't acknowledge or contract it.

Note: rf2-8bp3 (in flight) plans to promote reg-machine to a macro for source-coord; that work should also fix the spec gap (audit §5 large bullet, line 89).

Resolution: document reg-machine in spec/005-StateMachines.md and CP-5-MachineGuide as a real public registration surface (versus the reg-event-fx + create-machine-handler form 005 currently documents).
close_reason: Resolved via PR #157 — reg-machine + reg-machine* documented in 005 + CP-5 (commit 312893c).

title: Tighten Spec 000-Vision: keep ~8 Contract clauses, drop the rest, move addressee-flipped to SPEC-AUTHORING.md (resolves rf2-uuoj / rf2-ehml)
description: Decision (Mike, 2026-05-09 from rf2-ehml-review.md): Refine narrowly. Apply reviewer §6 recommendation. KEEPERS (8): C-000.13-15 capability-vs-mechanism, C-000.19 identity-primitive anti-patterns, C-000.25 capability declaration, C-000.26 PDS O(1) revert, C-000.27 artefact independence (no transitive :require core->feature), C-000.49-50 explicit-frame view + behavioural equivalence, C-000.51 substrate decoupling. REWORD: C-000.31 loosen to match prose where-possible hedge; C-000.34 inline C-000.36 caveat; C-000.49 grammatically broken second sentence. DROP all others (~43 clauses): paraphrases, addressee-flipped, premature normative content. PLACEMENT: ONE summary Contract block at the END of spec/000-Vision.md, not 16 blocks throughout. Block-style format (kept). ADDRESSEE-FLIPPED CLAUSES: C-000.20, .37-.40, .43-.44 are spec/harness-author obligations, not implementor. Move to new spec/SPEC-AUTHORING.md (or extend spec/Conventions.md if that fits the corpus better — agent picks). HARD HOLD: C-000.06 (unregistered fx MUST raise) and C-000.35 (production elision behavioural equivalence) MUST NOT land in 000 in this bead; they introduce normative content prose doesn't commit to. Need ratification in spec 002 (for .06) and spec 009 (for .35) first. Reviewer recommends those happen via separate decision beads at upstream-spec layer if at all. Filing them is out of scope for this bead. SPEC EDITS: spec/000-Vision.md (delete 43 inline blocks, add 1 summary block at end with 8 clauses, reword 3); new spec/SPEC-AUTHORING.md (or section in Conventions.md) for the 7 addressee-flipped clauses; spec/MIGRATION.md additive entry. CRITERION: per drafter's own observation - if you could write a fixture that fails when the prose's claim is violated, the prose wanted a Contract block. Apply strictly. Tests: each landed Contract clause's MUST/SHOULD must be checkable by either an existing test or a new fixture; if not, drop the clause. Additionally: append decision notes to rf2-uuoj + rf2-ehml referencing this bead as the resolution. Sources: findings/rf2-ehml-review.md (verdict, clause-level flags §5), findings/rf2-ehml-spec000-contract-draft.md (the 51-clause draft to cherry-pick from). Disposition: P3 follow-up to the rf2-uuoj pilot. Not pre-beta blocker. Direction set; dispatch.
close_reason: Merged via PR #137: 11 clauses in spec/000-Vision.md + 7 in spec/SPEC-AUTHORING.md + 2 held (C-000.06, C-000.35). Resolves rf2-uuoj + rf2-ehml.

title: Spec 011 / 009 - reconcile head-mismatch trace category with current hydration runtime
description:
Spec 011 and Spec 009 describe head-specific hydration divergence as a distinct trace operation: `:rf.warning/head-mismatch`. The current runtime routes head divergence through the generic `verify-hydration!` path and emits `:rf.ssr/hydration-mismatch`, using `:failing-id :rf.ssr/head-mismatch` to distinguish the case.

Possible necessary change: decide whether head mismatch deserves its own operation as currently specced, or whether the spec should adopt the current generic-hydration operation plus the `:failing-id` discriminator.

Evidence:
- spec/011-SSR.md:440, 467
- spec/009-Instrumentation.md:473, 588
- spec/API.md:365
- implementation/core/src/re_frame/ssr.cljc:379-416
- implementation/core/test/re_frame/ssr_end_to_end_test.clj:646-694

title: Spec 011 - reconcile head hydration payload keys with current :rf/hydrate runtime
description:
The hydration payload schema and SSR conformance corpus include optional head payload keys `:rf/head` and `:rf/head-hash`, and Spec 011 says the client uses them for head-mismatch detection when present. The current `:rf/hydrate` runtime only reads `:rf/app-db`, `:app-db`, `:rf/render-hash`, and `:rf/version`; it does not read or persist `:rf/head` / `:rf/head-hash`, and the current SSR tests explicitly park head-hash under `:rf/render-hash` as a workaround.

Possible necessary change: either implement the published head-hydration payload contract, or tighten Spec 011 / Spec-Schemas / conformance so they no longer describe keys the runtime ignores.

Evidence:
- spec/011-SSR.md:64, 440-442, 467, 682
- spec/Spec-Schemas.md:642-644
- spec/conformance/README.md:231
- spec/conformance/fixtures/ssr-head-hydration.edn:2, 9, 32-35, 52-59
- implementation/core/src/re_frame/ssr.cljc:364-376
- implementation/core/test/re_frame/ssr_end_to_end_test.clj:655-666

title: Spec 009 - reconcile fragment-change trace op name with runtime and routing spec
description:
Spec 009 still documents fragment-only routing traces under the older operation name `:route.url/fragment-changed`, but the runtime, migration table, Spec-Schemas, and current conformance fixture all use `:rf.route/url-changed` for the fragment-only case.

Possible necessary change: reconcile the trace vocabulary so Spec 009 names the same operation the runtime and the rest of the spec corpus use, or deliberately restore the older op if that is still the intended surface.

Evidence:
- spec/009-Instrumentation.md:89, 227
- spec/012-Routing.md:83, 336, 706
- spec/Spec-Schemas.md:211
- spec/conformance/fixtures/route-fragment-change.edn:4, 12, 35, 59-60
- implementation/core/test/re_frame/trace_test.clj:374-380
close_reason: Resolved via PR #162 — Spec 009 fragment-change trace op name aligned to runtime canonical (commit 6a784a8).

title: Spec 005 - decide whether dynamic machine spawn/destroy is real runtime lifecycle or trace-only
description:
Spec 005 describes dynamic actors spawned by `[:rf.machine/spawn ...]` as real runtime-managed machine instances: the runtime allocates an id, registers the handler, creates a snapshot under `[:rf/machines <id>]`, optionally dispatches `:start`, and `[:rf.machine/destroy actor-id]` tears the instance back down. The current `spawn-fx` / `destroy-machine-fx` implementation only emits `:rf.machine/spawned` / `:rf.machine/destroyed` traces and explicitly says full actor lifecycle is still the user's responsibility.

Possible necessary change: decide whether dynamic spawn/destroy is meant to be a full runtime capability or merely a trace-level placeholder. If the former, the lifecycle machinery is still missing; if the latter, Spec 005 should be tightened so it no longer promises registration/snapshot/message-routing semantics that the runtime does not perform.

Evidence:
- spec/005-StateMachines.md:1084-1171, 1801, 1883
- implementation/machines/src/re_frame/machines.cljc:963-981

title: Spec 005 / MIGRATION - reconcile public machine lifecycle fx ids with implementation
description:
Spec 005 §Spawning and MIGRATION §M-26 describe the canonical public lifecycle surface as `[:rf.machine/spawn ...]` and `[:rf.machine/destroy actor-id]`. The current implementation registers only the machine-internal fx ids `:spawn` and `:destroy-machine` in `implementation/machines/src/re_frame/machines.cljc`; there is no registered `:rf.machine/spawn` or `:rf.machine/destroy` alias.

Possible necessary change: reconcile the public surface with the runtime by either (a) adding the canonical public fx ids as aliases, (b) renaming the implementation to match the published ids, or (c) tightening the spec/prose so the public and machine-internal surfaces are explicitly different and the examples stop using the public ids.

Evidence:
- spec/005-StateMachines.md:1115, 1139, 1729, 1883
- spec/MIGRATION.md:1035-1036
- implementation/machines/src/re_frame/machines.cljc:963-984
close_reason: Resolved by rf2-m83v PR #175 — option (b) executed: implementation renamed to :rf.machine/spawn / :rf.machine/destroy (published id). Spec 005 + MIGRATION + Conventions + Spec-Schemas + API now consistent with impl.

title: re-frame-pair2: align parse-rf2-coord with actual data-rf2-source-coord format shipped by rf2-z7f7
description:
## Context

rf2-z7f7 (PR #135) shipped the `data-rf2-source-coord` DOM annotation. The actual emitted format (per the PR's verified DOM snippet):

```
data-rf2-source-coord="counter.core:counter-buttons:47:11"
```

Shape: `<ns>:<handler-id>:<line>:<col>` (4 colon-separated segments).

re-frame-pair2's `scripts/runtime.cljs` parser (`parse-rf2-coord`) was written speculatively before rf2-z7f7 landed and assumed:

```
'<ns>|<file>:<line>:<col>'
```

(per `re-frame-pair2/STATUS.md` known-unknown #3). The shapes differ — pair2's parser will not extract correct fields from real DOM annotations as shipped.

## Task

In `C:/Users/miket/code/re-frame-pair2/`:

1. Read the canonical format definition. Sources of truth (in re-frame2 repo at `C:/Users/miket/code/re-frame2/`):
   - `spec/006-Components.md` §Source-coord annotation.
   - `spec/Tool-Pair.md` §Source-mapping.
   - The implementation that emits the attribute (likely under `implementation/reagent/src/...` and/or `implementation/core/src/.../source_coord.cljc` — find via grep for `data-rf2-source-coord`).
   - PR #135 diff for context.
2. Verify what fields are extractable: `:ns`, `:handler-id` (new — wasn't in the original parser spec), `:line`, `:col`. Confirm format isn't variable per Form-1 / Form-2 / SSR cases.
3. Update `scripts/runtime.cljs` `parse-rf2-coord` to handle the actual format. Return all four fields. Handle missing-handler-id case if format degrades for some Reagent shapes (per the rf2-z7f7 fragment / interop fallback warning — those cases may emit with fewer segments or fall back to handler-meta lookup).
4. Update / add tests in pair2 that exercise the parser with real DOM samples. Use the snippet from PR #135 + at least one Form-2 case + one SSR case (read PR #135's test fixtures to crib representative samples).
5. Update `re-frame-pair2/STATUS.md`:
   - Remove or resolve known-unknown #3 (source-coord format) — it's now known.
   - Note any dependency on subsequent `:configure` knobs (Tool-Pair.md says the value format is opaque, so document the shape we're parsing as a contract that consumers should not depend on stability of).

## Out of scope

- Running the pair2 spike against a live re-frame2 app (that's the §8a spike work — separate effort tracked under rf2-9ra3).

## Verification

- Pair2 unit tests pass: `cd C:/Users/miket/code/re-frame-pair2 && (whatever its test command is — check package.json / SKILL.md / scripts/)`.
- Parser correctly extracts all four fields from the canonical sample `counter.core:counter-buttons:47:11`.

## Deliverable

Branch + PR in the `re-frame-pair2` repo (NOT re-frame2). If `re-frame-pair2` has no remote yet (it was created locally yesterday), commit on a feature branch and report back — Mike can decide whether to push or just keep local.

No AI attribution.

## Disposition

Pre-alpha follow-up to rf2-9ra3. Small, mechanical fix discovered while explaining pair2 status.
notes: 2026-05-09 bookkeeping: pair2-side parse-rf2-coord alignment work to land on the re-frame-pair2 repo (sibling repo, separate PR flow). re-frame2-side input is stable — data-rf2-source-coord format is locked by rf2-z7f7 (PR #135) shipped to spec/006-Components.md and Tool-Pair.md. Pair2-side merge awaiting Mike. Leaving in_progress.

title: Pilot RFC 2119 Contract blocks on spec/000-Specifications.md (draft + dual review) — rf2-ehml
description:
## Approach (decided in rf2-ehml notes)

Pilot the RFC 2119 Contract-block pattern on **spec/000-Specifications.md**. Two-phase: draft → independent dual-perspective review → Mike decides on rollout.

## Phase 1 — Drafter agent

Read:
- `spec/000-Specifications.md` (the target).
- RFC 2119 keyword conventions (MUST / SHOULD / MAY / etc).
- `bd show rf2-ehml` (parent decision bead — note prior parking in favour of empirical audit).
- `bd show rf2-7cco` (the empirical-audit pattern that landed — context for "what we're testing against").
- A handful of other spec files (002, 005) to see the prevailing prose style.

Output:
- `findings/rf2-ehml-spec000-contract-draft.md` — full revised 000 with Contract blocks added at appropriate points.
- Drafter explains in a §Designer-notes section: where they added blocks, why, what they considered adding-but-didn't.
- DO NOT modify the real `spec/000-Specifications.md`.
- DO NOT branch / PR — this is a draft for review.

## Phase 2 — Reviewer agent (dispatched separately when drafter returns)

Reviewer must be a different agent so they form an independent judgment.

Reviewer reads:
- The drafted version (`findings/rf2-ehml-spec000-contract-draft.md`).
- The original (`spec/000-Specifications.md`).
- RFC 2119.
- The empirical-audit pattern (`bd show rf2-7cco`) for the alternative-strategy comparison.

Reviewer writes findings to `findings/rf2-ehml-review.md` covering:
- §AI-implementation perspective. Does the Contract block layer reduce ambiguity? Are AI agents likelier to produce conformant impl from this version? Or does the formal layer just paraphrase prose without adding precision? Specific examples — quote sections that improved or didn't.
- §Human-readability perspective. Does the doc still read? Is narrative flow preserved? Or does the Contract block layer interrupt? Specific quotes.
- §Comparison vs the empirical-audit pattern. Which one would they recommend going forward, and why?
- §Recommendation. Roll out / refine / kill, with justification.

## Phase 3 — Mike decides

Mike reads draft + review. Decides:
- Adopt across all specs.
- Adopt only in some specs (which?).
- Refine the pattern (specific tweaks).
- Kill the pilot, stick with empirical-audit.

## Disposition

P3 — process improvement. Not pre-beta. Sequence does not gate other work.

## Recommendation (placeholder)

Wait for both phases to land before forming a view. The empirical-audit pattern is producing real drift-reduction signal already (per the rf2-7cco merge); the case for adding a second formal layer needs to clear a high bar.
notes: Refinement landed at PR https://github.com/day8/re-frame2/pull/137 (rf2-04sm). The 51-clause draft was reduced to 11 clauses in 000-Vision (one summary Contract block at the end) plus 7 addressee-flipped clauses extracted to spec/SPEC-AUTHORING.md under the SA-N id scheme. C-000.06 (unregistered fx) and C-000.35 (production elision behavioural equivalence) were deliberately held — they need ratification in spec 002 / spec 009 first.
close_reason: Pilot complete: draft (Phase 1) + independent review (Phase 2) landed in findings/. Decision: refine narrowly. Outcome: rf2-04sm impl merged via PR #137.

title: XState parity: final states / onDone / actor output / termination contract
description:
## XState concept

What it is in XState:
- `type: 'final'` states
- Parent `onDone` transitions when a child machine reaches a final state
- Top-level termination semantics — actor stops accepting events when done
- Final `output` value carried from completed child to parent

Sources:
- https://stately.ai/docs/final-states
- https://stately.ai/docs/output

## What it buys

- A standard completion story for child workflows.
- A built-in "this actor is done; stop sending it events" contract.
- A canonical result channel from child to parent.
- Cleaner async-child orchestration than ad-hoc success/failure event conventions.

## What re-frame2 already has

- Explicit terminal-state conventions via per-state `:meta`.
- Explicit `:raise` / `:dispatch` from a terminal state's `:entry`.
- Machine `:data` for result storage.

## Local references

- `spec/005-StateMachines.md` documents per-state `:meta` and notes final-state `onDone` as post-v1.

## Gap

- No runtime-level notion of "done".
- No standardised parent completion hook.
- No standardised output / result surface.

## Judgment

**High-value nice-to-have.** More broadly useful than parallel / history because many workflows naturally have completion (form submission, multi-step wizards, async fetch flows, modal task workflows).

## Decisions to surface (when this is picked up)

1. **Mark final state via `:meta` flag or a dedicated `:final?` key on the state spec?**
   - `:meta {:rf.machine/final? true}` — keeps spec 005's existing pattern for state metadata.
   - First-class `:final?` key — louder, easier to lint / verify, easier for AI-implementation to spot.
2. **Parent `:on-done` hook syntax.** Mirrors `:on-spawn`?
   ```clojure
   {:invoke {:machine-id :child/flow
             :on-done    (fn [data result] (assoc data :child-result result))}}
   ```
3. **Output value source.** Pulled from child's `:data` at termination time (entire `:data` or a designated key)? Computed by an `:output` fn? Both?
   ```clojure
   ;; option a — designated key
   {:meta {:rf.machine/final? true
           :rf.machine/output-key :result}}
   ;; option b — output fn
   {:meta {:rf.machine/final? true
           :rf.machine/output-fn (fn [data] (:result data))}}
   ```
4. **Termination semantics.** When a final state is entered:
   - Auto-destroy the spawned actor (current snapshot cleared, handler unregistered)?
   - Or just mark "done" and require explicit `[:rf.machine/destroy id]`?
   - Auto-destroy is more xstate-flavoured and avoids leaks; explicit-destroy is more re-frame-conservative.
5. **Stop-accepting-events contract.** Once done, dispatches to that machine should be silently dropped (with a trace) or raise an error?
6. **Trace events.** New: `:rf.machine/done` (with the output value), `:rf.machine/output-published`. Or fold into the existing `:rf.machine/destroyed`?
7. **Top-level (non-spawned, non-invoked) machine termination.** Is the contract symmetric — a singleton machine reaching a final state behaves the same way? Or is `:on-done` only meaningful for spawned/invoked actors with parents?
8. **Interaction with `:system-id` (rf2-suue / Option B).** When a system-id-bound actor reaches done and gets auto-destroyed, the `[:rf/system-ids]` reverse-index entry should clear too. Mechanical but should be specced.
9. **Schema impact.** `spec/Spec-Schemas.md` § machine-state spec gets a `:rf.machine/final?` row.
10. **Capability matrix axis.** spec 005 §Capability matrix actor-model axis — add `:actor/final-output` row.

## Disposition

P3 / post-v1 per current spec 005 note. But: if rf2-suue (Option B :system-id) lands first, and rf2-tijr / rf2-nfyf surface 10x v2 needing the trace events, this could promote earlier. Track here; revisit when those land.

## Recommendation (placeholder for Mike)

Adopt at the spec level. Implementation can wait for v1.1 unless 10x v2 / a real user flushes it forward. When implemented:
- Use a first-class `:final?` key on the state spec, not `:meta` (loud > silent for AI-implementation).
- Mirror `:on-spawn` with `:on-done` for parent hook (consistent with rf2-een2 / data-relative pattern).
- Provide both `:output-key` and `:output-fn` for the output source.
- Auto-destroy the spawned actor on done (xstate-flavoured, avoids leaks).
- Dispatches to a done actor → silently dropped + warn-level trace (matches re-frame's permissive defaults).

Open for redirect.

title: Tool-Pair surface audit: walk re-frame-10x feature list against re-frame-2's Tool-Pair surfaces
description:
## Context

From rf2-tijr discussion: re-frame-10x v2 will consume re-frame-2's Tool-Pair surfaces (epoch-history, restore-epoch, register-trace-cb!, source-coord, etc.) instead of vendoring re-frame.

Before re-frame-2 freezes its Tool-Pair surface set for 1.0, we need to walk re-frame-10x's existing feature list and verify each feature has a viable rendering path on top of re-frame-2's current Tool-Pair surfaces — without vendoring or host-frame hacks.

Gaps surfaced by this audit influence what spec / Tool-Pair beads need to land before 1.0.

## Scope

Research-only. Write findings to `findings/rf2-tijr-toolpair-audit.md`. No code changes, no PR.

## Deliverable

1. Inventory of re-frame-10x v1 features (what each panel does, what data it surfaces).
2. Mapping of each feature → required re-frame-2 Tool-Pair surface(s).
3. Gap list — features where the required surface is missing, partial, or undecided.
4. Sizing each gap (small / medium / large) and a recommendation: file beads now (pre-1.0 blocker), defer to post-1.0, or kill the feature in v2.
5. Recommendation on the audit's most load-bearing finding.

## Disposition

P3 — research informs pre-1.0 spec work but isn't itself a blocker. Findings will be reviewed by Mike; bead-filing decisions follow.
notes: 2026-05-09 bookkeeping: top-3 audit findings have all been actioned: #1 sub-topology debugger surface -> rf2-8nzo (closed via PR #142); #2 Performance API instrumentation -> rf2-du3i (closed via PR #139); #3 :flow op-type + :rf.flow/* trace vocabulary -> rf2-2s1o (closed via PR #138). Plus rf2-90vq (PR #154) for trace category coverage. Remaining items in findings/rf2-tijr-toolpair-audit.md are stylistic / smaller-priority gaps that can be filed individually as work prompts arise. Leaving the audit bead itself open as the reference index until Mike confirms it can be closed.
close_reason: Audit complete. Top-3 recommendations all actioned: rf2-8nzo (sub-topology impl, PR #142), rf2-du3i (Performance API, PR #139), rf2-2s1o (:flow op-type, PR #138). Plus rf2-90vq + rf2-mmn5 sweep (PR #154). Findings doc remains as reference at findings/rf2-tijr-toolpair-audit.md.

title: re-frame-10x v2: repo placement + replace vendoring with multi-frame isolation
description:
## Question

Two coupled questions about a re-frame-10x v2 (devtools for re-frame-2):

1. **Repo placement.** Should re-frame-10x v2 live in this repo as another artefact (`day8/re-frame-2-10x`) or stay as a separate repo (mirroring today's `day8/re-frame-10x`)?
2. **Vendoring → multi-frame.** The old re-frame-10x vendored its own copy of re-frame (`day8.re-frame-10x.inlined-deps.re-frame.<version>.re-frame.core`) so 10x's internal events / subs / app-db state didn't collide with the host app's singleton runtime. With re-frame-2's frame primitive (EP 002), can 10x v2 replace the vendoring trick by running in its own frame? What does that look like concretely?

## Repo placement — pros / cons

### As an in-repo artefact (`day8/re-frame-2-10x`)
- Tight versioning under the lockstep-through-1.0 contract (rf2-w05l).
- Shared CI/CD; release.yml's topological deploy includes 10x.
- 10x's surface is intimate with Tool-Pair beads (rf2-z9n1 source-coord, rf2-z7f7 DOM annotation, register-trace-cb!, epoch-history, restore-epoch, source-coord) — these were designed *for* 10x. Co-locating preserves the design conversation.
- Multi-artefact convention (rf2-5vjj Strategy B) already established; 10x slots in cleanly.

### As a separate repo (`day8/re-frame-2-10x`)
- Big code surface (UI, monaco, syntax-hl, assertion forms) — different velocity from core.
- Heavy JS deps (monaco, prism, etc.) shouldn't pollute the core repo's classpath / shadow-cljs config.
- 10x evolves UI/UX independently; doesn't need to lockstep with core's stability.
- Today's `day8/re-frame-10x` is a separate repo and that's been fine for years.
- Separate issue tracker keeps 10x bugs out of core's bd.

### My lean
**Separate repo.** 10x is downstream tooling, not foundational substrate. It consumes Tool-Pair surfaces from this repo but doesn't shape them. Keeping it separate preserves velocity asymmetry and isolates the JS-dep surface. The Tool-Pair surfaces are already designed in this repo for 10x's benefit; they don't need 10x co-located to remain coherent.

## Vendoring → multi-frame — concrete sketch

### Why old 10x vendored
re-frame v1 has a singleton runtime. 10x's own events (`:re-frame-10x.events/show-panel`, `:re-frame-10x.subs/event-list`, etc.) would be dispatched into the same registry as the host app's events. To prevent name collisions and the host app's interceptors from running on 10x's events, 10x vendored re-frame: 10x dispatches into `day8.re-frame-10x.inlined-deps.re-frame.v1v3v0-rc3.re-frame.core/dispatch`, which is structurally a different runtime instance from the host's `re-frame.core/dispatch`.

### Why re-frame-2 makes this unnecessary
re-frame-2 has frames (EP 002). Each frame is its own runtime: own app-db, own registries (events, subs, fxs, schemas, flows, routing, machines), own dispatch queue, own interceptor chain.

Concrete sketch:
```clojure
(ns day8.re-frame-2-10x.runtime
  (:require [re-frame.core :as rf]))

(defonce 10x-frame (rf/create-frame {:frame-id ::10x}))

;; 10x's internal events register into 10x-frame, not the host frame.
(rf/reg-event-db
  {:frame 10x-frame
   :event-id ::show-panel}
  (fn [db _] (assoc db :panel-open? true)))

;; 10x observes the host frame's runtime via Tool-Pair surfaces.
(rf/register-trace-cb!
  host-frame
  (fn [trace] (rf/dispatch 10x-frame [::trace-captured trace])))
```

The vendoring trick is gone. 10x's events live in its frame; the host frame's interceptors don't see them. 10x reads the host's events / app-db / subs through Tool-Pair surfaces (register-trace-cb!, epoch-history, etc.) — these are explicit observation channels, not the same singleton runtime.

## What this surfaces about Tool-Pair design

10x is the canonical Tool-Pair consumer. The surfaces designed under rf2-z9n1 / rf2-z7f7 / register-trace-cb! / epoch-history / restore-epoch / source-coord are designed *for* 10x. A natural follow-up audit: take the current Tool-Pair surface set and walk through 10x's existing feature list — does each feature still have a path to render in v2 using only Tool-Pair surfaces (no vendoring, no host-frame hacks)?

Likely gap candidates:
- **Subscription graph visualisation** — needs a way to enumerate the host frame's reg-sub graph + cache state. Tool-Pair surface for this? File as bead if missing.
- **Component re-render highlighting** — needs a hook into Reagent's reactor. Tool-Pair surface for this? Or substrate-adapter responsibility?
- **Replay / time travel** — restore-epoch covers it, but 10x needs to enumerate the epoch history (epoch-history surface).
- **Trace filtering** — needs trace categorisation. Already exposed via register-trace-cb!?

## Open questions / decisions needed

1. **Repo placement.** In-repo artefact vs separate repo?
2. **10x v2 substrate.** Reagent (matches re-frame-2's canonical substrate) or revisit (could be a UIx pilot if it lands earlier)?
3. **Loading / bootstrap.** How does 10x wire itself in for dev mode? `(10x/init!)` after `(rf/init!)`? Same `init!` with a `:dev-tools` flag? Compile-time inclusion vs runtime opt-in?
4. **Production elision.** 10x today is dev-only. Same in v2? Is the production-elision contract any different (the goog.DEBUG gate)?
5. **Tool-Pair surface audit.** Walk 10x's existing features against re-frame-2's Tool-Pair surfaces and file beads for any gap. (See list above.)
6. **Migration story for existing 10x users.** What does upgrading from re-frame-1 + re-frame-10x to re-frame-2 + re-frame-2-10x look like? Is it just deps.edn change or are there 10x-side API breaks too?

## Disposition

P3 — not pre-beta. v0.0.1.beta can ship without 10x v2. But the Tool-Pair surface audit (point 5) is the load-bearing consequence of this bead — gaps surfaced now influence what spec 006 / Tool-Pair beads need to land before 1.0.

## Recommendation (placeholder for Mike)

- **Separate repo.** Match today's structure; 10x is downstream tooling.
- **Multi-frame replaces vendoring.** Frame isolation is exactly the property 10x needed. No more `inlined-deps`.
- **Tool-Pair surface audit as a follow-up bead.** Walk 10x's feature list against current Tool-Pair surface set; file beads for gaps. This is the actionable pre-1.0 work.
- **10x v2 substrate = Reagent** (canonical). Revisit if UIx adapter lands earlier and wants 10x as a battle-test.
- **10x repo can wait** until re-frame-2's surfaces stabilise (post-beta).

Open for redirect.

title: Consider Stately-style 'actor system' capability for spawned-machine coordination
description:
## Question

Should re-frame2 adopt a Stately-style 'system' surface (https://stately.ai/docs/system) for coordinating spawned machines?

## What Stately provides

- **Named-actor registry per system.** Actors get a `systemId` at spawn/invoke time; any other actor in the same system looks them up via `system.get('actorId')`.
- **System-relative addressing.** Cross-actor messaging is `sendTo(({system}) => system.get('notifier'), {type: :notify})` — the sender doesn't need a direct reference, only the name.
- **Implicit root actor → system.** `createActor(machine).start()` creates the system; descendants can be invoked/spawned under it. Only the root can stop the system.
- **System-level introspection.** `actor.system` is the entry point. Stately Studio integration for system-wide debugging is hinted as 'coming soon'.

## How this maps to re-frame2

- **Frame ≈ system.** Per-frame scoping (schemas, flows, routing, machines) already gives re-frame2 the per-instance container Stately gets from a 'system'. The frame is the natural home for an actor registry.
- **Spawned machines exist** (rf2-een2 :on-spawn data-relative, :spawn / :destroy-machine fxs in machines.cljc) but there is no named-machine discovery surface. Two spawned machines today coordinate via frame events, not point-to-point named addressing.
- **Dispatch model is broadcast-then-handled**, not point-to-point. Stately's sendTo is conceptually different from rf dispatch — adopting named-actor sendTo would be a new primitive sitting alongside dispatch, not replacing it.

## Concrete things this would unlock (if adopted)

- Named-machine lookup: (machine.system/get frame :notifier) returns the spawned machine ref.
- Targeted send: (machine.send :notifier {:type :notify}) — analogous to dispatch but addressed to a specific spawned machine, not the frame's event bus.
- Hierarchical machine introspection for Tool-Pair / epoch-history surfaces.
- Compositional patterns where parent machines spawn named children that other parts of the app message by name.

## Open questions / decisions needed

1. Is targeted point-to-point machine messaging a missing primitive, or is broadcast-then-handled-by-machine (current model) sufficient?
2. If yes — does it sit in spec 005 (machines) or warrant its own spec / EP?
3. Hierarchical actor trees: does Stately's parent/child machine model map onto re-frame2's spawned-machine concept, or is it a different shape?
4. System-level introspection: how does this interact with Tool-Pair epoch-history / register-trace-cb! surfaces?
5. Multi-frame story: re-frame2 already has per-frame scoping. Is the Stately 'system' just our 'frame', or is there a sub-system level (a system within a frame)?

## Disposition

Open question — Mike's call. Not pre-1.0 if it's a new spec/EP; could be pragmatic-add to spec 005 if it's just a named-machine lookup helper. Defer until Mike rules.
notes:
---

## Decision (2026-05-09, Mike)

**Option B** — opt-in :system-id named-machine addressing.

Implementation tracked under separate impl bead.
close_reason: Resolved via PR #136 — Stately-style actor system Option B (:system-id named addressing) + impl merged via rf2-suue.

title: Restructure examples/seven_guis -> examples/7Guis with per-example sub-folders + examples/README.md
description:

Target state:

    timer/

      timer.spec.cjs
      timer.html

Each sub-example becomes self-contained, mirroring the layout of other examples (counter, todomvc, realworld). The folder name is 7Guis (capital G; original 7GUIs project capitalisation; lowercase 'g' would mismatch).

## 2. Add examples/README.md

A top-level README in examples/ explaining how to run the examples. Likely already exists from rf2-0h9n (refresh example catalog) — this bead consolidates / updates if so.

Content:
- Where the orchestrator is (implementation/scripts/run-examples-tests.cjs).
- How to run all examples via Playwright: npm run test:examples.
- How to run a single example with shadow-cljs watch (shadow-cljs watch <id>).
- Per-example brief description (mirror examples/README.md from rf2-0h9n if it has this; otherwise add).

## Sweep targets

- implementation/shadow-cljs.edn — every 7Guis build target's source paths update from ../examples/seven_guis/<file>.cljs to ../examples/7Guis/<example>/<file>.cljs.
- implementation/scripts/run-examples-tests.cjs — spec-discovery glob picks up new locations (the rf2-kdvh glob examples/**/*.spec.cjs should already work; verify).
- examples/README.md — update or add the example index reflecting new paths.
- spec/README.md — refresh the worked-examples row to use new paths.
- Any guide chapter that references examples/seven_guis/<file> — sweep.
- spec/Construction-Prompts.md if any references — sweep.

## Verify

After the move:
- npm run test:examples runs all 15 specs (no regression).
- Each example builds via shadow-cljs.
- Bundle isolation per rf2-p7va et al. preserved (counter still has 0 hits for unused features).
- Spec docs / guide cross-links resolve.

## Out of scope

- Don't rename the .cljs source files inside (cells.cljs stays cells.cljs).
- Don't touch the namespace identifiers (seven-guis.cells namespace stays the same — only the on-disk path changes... wait, namespace HAS to match path. Reconsider: if the file moves to examples/7Guis/cells/cells.cljs, the ns becomes cells.cells or seven-guis.cells.cells — pick a consistent rule).
- Actually do touch the namespaces if path-mirroring requires it. Whatever the current ns is, update to match the new path.

## Naming question

7Guis or 7-guis for the folder? Original project is 7GUIs.com; 7Guis is the natural shortening. Mike specified 7Guis.
notes:
Scope expansion (Mike, 2026-05-09): also move example-tooling out of implementation/scripts/ into examples/scripts/.

## Additional moves

- implementation/scripts/run-examples-tests.cjs -> examples/scripts/run-examples-tests.cjs
- implementation/scripts/spec-helpers.cjs -> examples/scripts/spec-helpers.cjs

The orchestrator references node_modules via relative path (../implementation/node_modules). Don't add a separate examples/package.json — minimal friction wins. If examples/ tooling grows enough to warrant its own npm workspace later, that's a separate bead.

## Cascading sweep

- npm scripts in implementation/package.json — 'test:examples' invokes the orchestrator; update path.
- .github/workflows/test.yml — references the orchestrator path; update.
- examples/<name>/<name>.spec.cjs — require path for spec-helpers updates from '../../implementation/scripts/spec-helpers.cjs' to '../scripts/spec-helpers.cjs'.

## Folder layout end-state

examples/
  scripts/
    run-examples-tests.cjs
    spec-helpers.cjs
  README.md
  counter/
    core.cljs
    counter.spec.cjs
    index.html
  todomvc/
    ...
  7Guis/                                <- renamed from seven_guis
    cells/
      cells.cljs
      cells.spec.cjs
      cells.html
    circle_drawer/
      ...
    crud/
    flight_booker/
    temperature/
    timer/
  ...

title: SSR head-element management surface (rf2-gr0n defer)
description:
The :head registry kind exists but no machinery — registration / rendering / active-head selection for SSR pages. Real production SSR feature; non-trivial impl. Defer post-v1.

Surface: reg-head, render-head, active-head — the trio advertised in spec/API.md and 011-SSR.md as v1 surfaces but with no impl, no test, and no fixture exercising them.

Discovered from rf2-gr0n during the API.md drift sweep — the SSR head trio was triaged as defer (not drop), since SSR-aware applications will need this. The API.md rows stay but the impl is post-v1.

title: Public init-platform — JVM-side substrate init equivalent (rf2-gr0n defer)
description:
The substrate adapter pattern (rf2-0hxm + rf2-4cb6) covers CLJS init via substrate adapter. JVM-side init for SSR / non-browser contexts may want an equivalent named entry. Currently init! covers all paths; init-platform would be a JVM-flavoured alias if pattern emerges. Defer post-v1.

Discovered from rf2-gr0n during the API.md drift sweep — init-platform was triaged as defer (not drop), so the API.md row stays but no impl is required for v1.

title: Public make-restore-fn — fixture-style frame-restore helper (rf2-gr0n defer)
description:
A public fn that wraps current epoch-restore semantics in a fixture-shaped helper for tests. Useful for v1-style test code that called make-restore-fn to snapshot/restore between tests. Currently with-fresh-registrar and reset-runtime-fixture cover this; make-restore-fn would be a public ergonomic wrapper. Defer post-v1.

Discovered from rf2-gr0n during the API.md drift sweep — make-restore-fn was triaged as defer (not drop), so the API.md row stays but no impl is required for v1.

title: Bundle: split epoch into day8/re-frame-2-epoch (rf2-5vjj feature)
description:

implementation/core/src/re_frame/epoch* -> implementation/epoch/src/re_frame/epoch*
Plus tests + deps.edn + shadow-cljs build target.

day8/re-frame-2-epoch — optional dep.

title: Promote remaining browser-only placeholder cross-spec tests
description:
Why this issue exists
The repo now has a browser-test runner, but several cross-spec tests are still placeholders that explicitly defer real DOM/browser assertions. Those cases sit on load-bearing UI/runtime boundaries rather than pure business logic.

What needs to be done
Convert the remaining placeholders in implementation/test/re_frame/cross_spec_cljs_test.cljs into real browser-run tests where feasible, especially:
1. frame destroy during render
2. plain Reagent fn under a non-default frame warning behavior
3. any remaining timer/render-path cases currently left as browser TODOs

Expected outcome
- The browser runner, not just node-test, covers these cases.
- Placeholder comments can be removed or narrowed.
- Real browser semantics become part of CI coverage for these interactions.
close_reason: Resolved via PR #166 — browser-only placeholder cross-spec tests promoted to real assertions (commit cbd06af). Halt-flagged sub-case for plain-fn-under-non-default-frame is tracked separately on rf2-d3k3.

title: Add restore-epoch tests for reactive surfaces after rewind
description:
Why this issue exists
poch_test.clj validates epoch recording and the basic estore-epoch happy/failure paths, but the coverage stops at raw app-db replacement. For Tool-Pair and time-travel debugging, the load-bearing question is whether materialized runtime surfaces observe the rewind correctly.

What needs to be done
1. Add tests showing that estore-epoch correctly updates already-materialized subscriptions.
2. Add tests for flows after restore:
- restored upstream inputs
- restored flow output paths
- no stale cached last-inputs / recompute state
3. Add route-slice coverage where a restored epoch changes :route and the observable routing state follows.
4. If there are known non-goals, pin them explicitly in tests or comments so time-travel semantics stay clear.

Current related coverage
- implementation/test/re_frame/epoch_test.clj covers app-db rewind and failure modes.
- implementation/test/re_frame/cross_spec_cljs_test.cljs has a Tool-Pair-style revert case, but not materialized subs/flows/routes after estore-epoch.

title: Revisit verifier and runtime-monitoring direction
description:
Why this issue exists
Revisit the verification / monitoring direction after the current implementation and spec work settles. The codebase already has strong ingredients for this work: conformance fixtures, pure machine-transition, structured traces, epoch history, and an AI/tool-facing spec surface. There are also concrete gaps to resolve before deeper verifier work is worth doing.
What needs to be done
1. Revisit the runtime/spec drift called out in the review:
